topics.title: BJT Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** BJT Amplifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of BJT Amplifiers

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **BJT Amplifier** is an electronic circuit that uses a Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) as the active amplifying device to convert a small AC input signal applied at one port into a larger, faithful replica AC signal at the output port, while drawing power from an external DC supply. The amplification occurs because the transistor operates in the **active region** of its output characteristics, where a small change in the base–emitter voltage (or base current) produces a proportionally larger change in the collector current, which is then delivered to a load.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCPT304)**, the module-level focus is on the three fundamental single-stage BJT amplifier topologies:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1)**
> 1. **Common-Emitter (CE) Amplifier** – voltage amplifier with phase inversion.
> 2. **Common-Base (CB) Amplifier** – current-buffer / high-frequency amplifier.
> 3. **Common-Collector (CC) Amplifier** – emitter follower, voltage buffer.

Each configuration is analysed using the **small-signal low-frequency model** (r-parameter / hybrid-π model) under sinusoidal steady-state excitation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **water tap connected to a powerful municipal pipeline**:
- The **light touch of your hand** on the tap handle is the *small input signal* (base current $i_b$).
- The **massive rush of water** that follows is the *amplified output* (collector current $i_c$).
- The **tap mechanism** itself is the **BJT**, which uses a tiny physical effort to control an enormous flow.
- The **mains water pressure** provided by the corporation is the **DC supply ($V_{CC}$)** that powers the whole action.

The BJT doesn't *create* energy from nothing — it merely **shapes and redirects** the energy drawn from the DC battery into a larger, more useful version of the input signal. This is the essence of all electronic amplification.

## 1.3 Why BJT Amplifiers Matter in Engineering

| Application Area | Role of BJT Amplifier |
|---|---|
| Audio electronics (microphone preamps, hearing aids) | Voltage gain stage |
| RF communication front-ends | Common-base low-noise stage |
| Op-amp internal input stage | Matched BJT differential pair |
| Power electronics driver stages | Darlington / emitter follower buffers |
| Instrumentation sensors | Impedance matching (emitter follower) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operating Point Metrics (Bolded for KTU Emphasis)**
> - **DC supply voltage ($V_{CC}$):** typically **+12 V** or **±15 V**.
> - **Thermal voltage ($V_T$):** **≈ 26 mV** at room temperature (300 K).
> - **Transistor current gain ($\beta$ or $h_{fe}$):** typically **50 to 400**.
> - **Biasing is mandatory** to fix the Q-point at the centre of the active region so that the full AC swing is undistorted.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Need for Small-Signal Analysis

A BJT is a **non-linear device** — its collector current $I_C$ depends *exponentially* on $V_{BE}$. To perform linear circuit analysis on the amplifier, we superimpose a *small* AC signal ($v_{be} \le 5$ mV) on top of the **DC quiescent (Q) point** and approximate the BJT's behaviour as a **linear small-signal model** valid around the operating point.

## 2.2 The Hybrid-π Small-Signal Model (r-parameters)

The **hybrid-π** model replaces the physical BJT with the following linear elements, all evaluated at the Q-point:

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Base–emitter dynamic resistance | $r_\pi$ | $\dfrac{\beta \, V_T}{I_C}$ | Input resistance looking into the base |
| Transconductance (output current per input voltage) | $g_m$ | $\dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | Voltage-to-current conversion gain |
| Output resistance (Early effect) | $r_o$ | $\dfrac{V_A}{I_C}$ | Finite output slope in active region |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Relationship (used everywhere in KTU derivations)**
> $$g_m = \dfrac{\beta}{r_\pi} = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$$
> Memorise this. The examiner expects it in nearly every CE/CB/CC derivation.

## 2.3 The Three Configurations — Operating Principle

### 2.3.1 Common-Emitter (CE) Amplifier
- **Input** is applied between **Base and Emitter**.
- **Output** is taken between **Collector and Emitter**.
- Emitter is **common** to both input and output (grounded through bypass capacitor).
- Provides **high voltage gain**, **moderate current gain**, and **180° phase inversion** between input and output.

### 2.3.2 Common-Base (CB) Amplifier
- **Input** is applied between **Emitter and Base**.
- **Output** is taken between **Collector and Base**.
- Base is **common** (AC grounded).
- Provides **high voltage gain** but **current gain < 1**, excellent high-frequency response.

### 2.3.3 Common-Collector (CC) / Emitter Follower
- **Input** is applied between **Base and Collector**.
- **Output** is taken between **Emitter and Collector**.
- Collector is **common** (AC grounded).
- **Voltage gain ≈ 1** (no amplification of voltage, hence "follower"), but provides **high current gain** and **very high input impedance** — used as a **buffer**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> All gains are **small-signal AC gains**; $R_S$ = source resistance, $R_L$ = AC load, $R_C$ = collector resistor, $R_E$ = emitter resistor (un-bypassed portion), $R_B$ = base-bias resistance.

| Quantity | Common-Emitter (CE) | Common-Base (CB) | Common-Collector (CC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage Gain $A_v$ | $-g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | $g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | $\dfrac{g_m R_E}{1 + g_m R_E} \approx 1$ |
| Current Gain $A_i$ | $-\beta$ (approx, no $R_L$ loss) | $-\alpha \approx -1$ | $\dfrac{\beta}{1 + \beta}$ (approx 1) |
| Input Resistance $R_{in}$ | $r_\pi \parallel R_B$ | $r_e \approx \dfrac{1}{g_m}$ | $r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E$ |
| Output Resistance $R_{out}$ | $R_C \parallel r_o$ | $R_C \parallel r_o$ | $R_E \parallel \left( \dfrac{R_S \parallel R_B + r_\pi}{\beta+1} \right)$ |
| Phase Relationship | **180°** (inverted) | **0°** (in-phase) | **0°** (in-phase) |

Where the small-signal emitter resistance is:

$$r_e = \dfrac{V_T}{I_E} \approx \dfrac{26 \text{ mV}}{I_E \text{ (mA)}} \, \Omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick (Recurring in 2023 & 2024 papers)**
> If $r_o$ is **not** mentioned in the problem data, **assume $r_o = \infty$** (ideal current source) and drop it from all formulas. Do not invent a value.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **CE** → Audio voltage pre-amplifiers, IF amplifier stages in radio receivers.
- **CB** → RF amplifier at VHF/UHF, low-noise input stage, coaxial driver.
- **CC** → Output buffer driving low-impedance loads (speakers, transmission lines), impedance transformer between high-Z source and low-Z load.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the **voltage gain ($A_v$)**, **current gain ($A_i$)**, and **input resistance ($R_{in}$)** for the **Common-Emitter amplifier with unbypassed emitter resistor** — the most frequently asked KTU derivation.

## 3.1 The Circuit and Its AC Equivalent

Consider a voltage-divider biased CE amplifier with:
- Collector resistor $R_C$
- Emitter resistor $R_E$ with a **partially bypassed** portion (let $R_E$ be the *unbypassed* part)
- Source resistance $R_S$
- AC load $R_L$ at the output

In the **midband frequency region**, all coupling and bypass capacitors behave as **short circuits**, and the DC supply $V_{CC}$ becomes AC ground. Replacing the BJT with its small-signal hybrid-π model gives the AC equivalent circuit.

> **Key idea:** $V_{CC}$ is AC ground, so $R_C$ and $R_L$ appear in **parallel** as the effective load $R_{C}'$:

$$R_{C}' = R_C \parallel R_L = \dfrac{R_C R_L}{R_C + R_L}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Voltage Gain $A_v$

### Step 1 — Write KVL around the input (base–emitter) loop

Going around the loop: $v_{in} = v_{be} + i_e R_E$.

Using the small-signal model, the emitter current $i_e$ is related to the base–emitter voltage through $r_\pi$:

$$i_b = \dfrac{v_{be}}{r_\pi}$$

And from BJT current relationships:

$$i_e = (\beta + 1) i_b = (\beta + 1) \dfrac{v_{be}}{r_\pi}$$

### Step 2 — Express $v_{in}$ in terms of $v_{be}$

Substituting $i_e$ into the input loop equation:

$$v_{in} = v_{be} + (\beta + 1) \dfrac{v_{be}}{r_\pi} \cdot R_E$$

$$v_{in} = v_{be} \left[ 1 + \dfrac{(\beta + 1) R_E}{r_\pi} \right]$$

### Step 3 — Write the output voltage

The collector current is $i_c = \beta i_b = \beta \dfrac{v_{be}}{r_\pi} = g_m \, v_{be}$.

This current flows through the effective load $R_C \parallel R_L$, producing:

$$v_{out} = - i_c (R_C \parallel R_L) = - g_m \, v_{be} \, R_{C}'$$

The **negative sign** appears because an *increase* in $v_{be}$ increases $i_c$, which *decreases* the collector voltage $v_c = V_{CC} - i_c R_C$, hence the **180° phase inversion**.

### Step 4 — Form the ratio

$$A_v = \dfrac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \dfrac{- g_m \, v_{be} \, R_{C}'}{v_{be} \left[ 1 + \dfrac{(\beta+1) R_E}{r_\pi} \right]}$$

The $v_{be}$ cancels:

$$A_v = \dfrac{- g_m \, R_{C}'}{1 + \dfrac{(\beta+1) R_E}{r_\pi}}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the identity $g_m = (\beta+1)/r_\pi$

Substituting $g_m \, r_\pi = \beta + 1$:

$$A_v = \dfrac{- g_m \, R_{C}'}{1 + g_m R_E}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU-Ready Expression for CE with unbypassed $R_E$:**
> $$\boxed{\,A_v = - \dfrac{g_m \, (R_C \parallel R_L)}{1 + g_m R_E}\,}$$
> This is the **most tested formula** for the CE amplifier in KTU 2024 papers. Derive it in 4–5 clean steps for full marks.

## 3.3 Derivation of Input Resistance $R_{in}$

Looking into the base of the BJT (with unbypassed $R_E$):

$$R_{in,\text{base}} = \dfrac{v_{be}}{i_b} + (\beta+1) R_E = r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E$$

The total input resistance including the bias network $R_B \parallel R_B'$ (or $R_1 \parallel R_2$ for voltage-divider bias) is:

$$R_{in} = R_B \parallel \big[ r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E \big]$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Current Gain $A_i$

Define the current gain as the ratio of load current to source current:

$$A_i = \dfrac{i_L}{i_{in}} = \dfrac{i_c}{i_b} \cdot \dfrac{i_L}{i_c} = \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_C}{R_C + R_L}$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A CE amplifier has $I_C = 2$ mA, $R_C = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, $R_L = 10$ k$\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $R_E = 470$ $\Omega$ (unbypassed), $R_S = 1$ k$\Omega$. Find $A_v$, $R_{in}$, and the overall voltage gain from source to load $A_{vs}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $g_m$:**

$$g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T} = \dfrac{2 \text{ mA}}{26 \text{ mV}} = 0.0769 \text{ S} = 76.9 \text{ mA/V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $R_{C}'$:**

$$R_{C}' = R_C \parallel R_L = \dfrac{4.7 \times 10}{4.7 + 10} = \dfrac{47}{14.7} = 3.197 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Compute $A_v$ (using the boxed formula):**

$$A_v = - \dfrac{76.9 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.197 \times 10^{3}}{1 + 76.9 \times 10^{-3} \times 470}$$

$$A_v = - \dfrac{245.9}{1 + 36.14} = - \dfrac{245.9}{37.14} = - 6.62$$

**Step 4 — Compute $r_\pi$:**

$$r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{100}{0.0769} = 1300 \, \Omega = 1.3 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Compute $R_{in}$ (assuming large $R_B$, so $R_{in} \approx r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E$):**

$$R_{in} = 1.3 \text{ k} + 101 \times 0.47 \text{ k} = 1.3 + 47.47 = 48.77 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Compute $A_{vs}$ (voltage gain from source to load):**

$$A_{vs} = A_v \cdot \dfrac{R_{in}}{R_S + R_{in}} = -6.62 \times \dfrac{48.77}{1 + 48.77} = -6.62 \times 0.9799 = -6.49$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary:** $A_v \approx -6.62$, $R_{in} \approx 48.8$ k$\Omega$, $A_{vs} \approx -6.49$. The negative sign confirms the 180° phase inversion characteristic of the CE configuration.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (CE vs CB vs CC)

| Performance Metric | CE | CB | CC | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage Gain Magnitude | High | High | $\approx 1$ | CE / CB |
| Current Gain Magnitude | High ($\beta$) | $< 1$ ($\alpha$) | High | CE / CC |
| Power Gain | High | Moderate | Moderate | **CE** |
| Input Impedance | Moderate | **Low** | **High** | CC |
| Output Impedance | Moderate | High | **Low** | CC |
| Phase Inversion | **Yes (180°)** | No | No | CE only |
| High-Frequency Response | Moderate | **Best** | Moderate | CB |
| Typical Use | Voltage amplifier | RF/Low-noise | Buffer | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CE Amplifier — Functional Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Source Signal vS] --> B[Source Resistance RS]
    B --> C[Coupling Capacitor Cin]
    C --> D[Base Node of BJT]
    D --> E[BJT Q1 in Active Region]
    E --> F[Collector Node]
    E --> G[Emitter Node]
    G --> H[Emitter Resistor RE]
    H --> I[Ground]
    F --> J[RC parallel RL Load]
    J --> K[Coupling Capacitor Cout]
    K --> L[Output vout 180 degree inverted]
    M[DC Supply VCC] --> N[RC Bias Resistor]
    N --> F
    M --> O[Voltage Divider R1 R2]
    O --> D
    P[Bypass Capacitor CE] -.-> H
```

## 4.2 CB Amplifier — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Signal vin] --> B[Coupling Capacitor Cin]
    B --> C[Emitter Node]
    C --> D[BJT Q1 Active Region]
    D --> E[Base Node AC Grounded]
    D --> F[Collector Node]
    E --> G[Ground via CB]
    F --> H[RC parallel RL]
    H --> I[Cout Coupling Capacitor]
    I --> J[Output vout in phase]
    K[VCC Supply] --> L[RC Resistor]
    L --> F
    M[VEE Negative Supply] --> N[Emitter Bias RE]
    N --> C
```

## 4.3 CC Emitter Follower — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input vin] --> B[Source Resistance RS]
    B --> C[Base Node]
    C --> D[BJT Q1 Active Region]
    D --> E[Collector Node AC Grounded]
    D --> F[Emitter Node Output]
    F --> G[Emitter Resistor RE]
    G --> H[Ground]
    F --> I[Coupling Capacitor Cout]
    I --> J[Load RL Low Impedance]
    J --> K[vout follows vin phase same]
    L[VCC Supply] --> E
    M[Bias Divider R1 R2] --> C
```

## 4.4 Hybrid-Pi Small-Signal Model — Internal Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Base Terminal B] --> B[Resistance rpi]
    B --> C[Internal Emitter Node E prime]
    C --> D[Ground Reference]
    A --> E[Dependent Current Source gm vbe]
    E --> F[Collector Terminal C]
    F --> G[Output Resistance ro to E prime]
    G --> D
    A --> H[Bprime Internal Base Node]
    H --> B
```

## 4.5 Signal Flow Summary — From Source to Load

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Small AC Source] --> B[Coupling Capacitor blocks DC]
    B --> C[BJT Amplification Stage]
    C --> D[Inversion 180 deg CE only]
    D --> E[Effective AC Load RC parallel RL]
    E --> F[Coupling Capacitor blocks DC]
    F --> G[Amplified Output to Load]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The bypass capacitor $C_E$ in the CE diagram, when present, **short-circuits $R_E$ for AC signals**, restoring the maximum voltage gain $A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$. Without it, the gain is reduced (as derived in §3.2) but **stability and linearity improve** — a deliberate engineering trade-off.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q:** Draw the small-signal hybrid-π model of a BJT and label all components. Why is the Early resistance $r_o$ often ignored in introductory analysis?

**Model Answer:**

The hybrid-π model consists of:
- A **base–emitter resistance $r_\pi$** between B and E.
- A **voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{be}$** between C and E.
- An **output resistance $r_o$** (due to the Early effect) in parallel with the current source.
- An optional **feedback resistance $r_\mu$** (usually neglected at low frequencies).

**Why $r_o$ is ignored:**
- In most low-frequency, introductory problems, the output characteristics of the BJT are assumed to be **perfectly horizontal**, i.e., the collector current is independent of $V_{CE}$.
- Ignoring $r_o$ simplifies the analysis significantly.
- $r_o$ is typically very large (tens to hundreds of k$\Omega$) compared to $R_C$, so its parallel effect is small.
- When the problem statement does not give the Early voltage $V_A$, $r_o$ cannot be computed — hence it is omitted.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q:** Compare the three BJT amplifier configurations (CE, CB, CC) in terms of voltage gain, current gain, input impedance, and phase relationship.

**Model Answer (Tabular):**

| Configuration | Voltage Gain | Current Gain | Input Impedance | Phase Shift |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Common-Emitter (CE)** | High (≈ $-g_m R_C$) | High (≈ $\beta$) | Moderate ($r_\pi$) | **180°** |
| **Common-Base (CB)** | High (≈ $g_m R_C$) | $< 1$ (≈ $\alpha$) | Low ($r_e$) | 0° |
| **Common-Collector (CC)** | $\approx 1$ | High (≈ $\beta$) | High ($r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E$) | 0° |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **voltage-divider biased Common-Emitter amplifier** using an npn transistor. Identify the role of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expressions for **voltage gain ($A_v$)**, **input resistance ($R_{in}$)** and **current gain ($A_i$)** of the CE amplifier with a **fully bypassed** emitter resistor. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Circuit Operation:**

The voltage-divider biased CE amplifier consists of:
- **$R_1$, $R_2$:** Form a voltage divider that sets the base DC voltage $V_B = V_{CC} \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$.
- **$R_C$:** Collector resistor — converts collector current variations into output voltage.
- **$R_E$:** Emitter resistor — provides DC stability against temperature variations (thermal runaway protection).
- **$C_E$:** Bypass capacitor — short-circuits $R_E$ for AC signals, restoring full AC voltage gain.
- **$C_{in}$, $C_{out}$:** Coupling capacitors — block DC between stages while passing the AC signal.
- **$V_{CC}$:** DC supply powers the circuit.
- **Q1 (npn BJT):** Active amplifying device.

**Operation:** A small AC input $v_{in}$ applied at the base produces a corresponding variation in base current $i_b$, which is amplified $\beta$ times to produce a much larger collector current $i_c$. This varying $i_c$ develops an amplified, **180° inverted** voltage across $R_C$, which is delivered to the load through $C_{out}$.

**(b) Derivations:**

**Voltage Gain $A_v$:**

With $C_E$ fully bypassing $R_E$, the emitter is at AC ground. Hence the small-signal model has $R_E$ shorted.

$$v_{in} = v_{be} = i_b \, r_\pi$$

$$i_c = \beta \, i_b = g_m \, v_{be}$$

$$v_{out} = - i_c (R_C \parallel R_L) = - g_m \, v_{be} \, R_{C}'$$

$$A_v = \dfrac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \dfrac{-g_m \, v_{be} \, R_{C}'}{v_{be}} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

$$\boxed{A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)}$$

**Input Resistance $R_{in}$:**

$$R_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$$

(If bias resistors are very large, $R_{in} \approx r_\pi$.)

**Current Gain $A_i$:**

$$A_i = \dfrac{i_L}{i_{in}} = \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_C}{R_C + R_L}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Draw the **hybrid-π small-signal model** of a BJT and explain each component. Define $g_m$ and $r_\pi$ and derive the relationship between them. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a **Common-Collector (emitter follower) amplifier**, derive the expressions for **voltage gain**, **input resistance**, and **output resistance**. State **two practical applications** of this configuration. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Hybrid-π Model and Component Explanation:**

The hybrid-π model represents the BJT by linear elements valid for small AC signals around the Q-point:

- **$r_\pi$:** Resistance between internal base node B' and emitter E, representing the dynamic base–emitter junction. Formula: $r_\pi = V_T / I_B = \beta V_T / I_C$.
- **$g_m v_{be}$:** Voltage-controlled current source from collector to emitter, where $g_m$ is the **transconductance** (in Siemens or A/V), defined as $g_m = I_C / V_T$.
- **$r_o$:** Output resistance representing the Early effect; $r_o = V_A / I_C$.
- **$r_b$** (optional): Base-spreading resistance.

**Derivation of the relationship between $g_m$ and $r_\pi$:**

Starting from the definitions:

$$g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T} \quad \text{and} \quad r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta \, V_T}{I_C}$$

Multiplying:

$$g_m \cdot r_\pi = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T} \cdot \dfrac{\beta V_T}{I_C} = \beta$$

$$\boxed{g_m = \dfrac{\beta}{r_\pi}}$$

**(b) Common-Collector (Emitter Follower) Derivations:**

In the CC configuration, the collector is AC-grounded and the output is taken at the emitter. Apply KVL to the input loop:

$$v_{in} = v_{be} + v_{out}$$

The output voltage across $R_E$ is:

$$v_{out} = i_e R_E = (\beta+1) i_b R_E = (\beta+1) \dfrac{v_{be}}{r_\pi} R_E$$

**Voltage Gain:**

$$A_v = \dfrac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \dfrac{(\beta+1) R_E / r_\pi}{1 + (\beta+1) R_E / r_\pi} = \dfrac{g_m R_E}{1 + g_m R_E}$$

For $g_m R_E \gg 1$:

$$\boxed{A_v \approx 1}$$

Hence the output "follows" the input.

**Input Resistance:**

Looking into the base, the input loop gives:

$$R_{in,\text{base}} = \dfrac{v_{in}}{i_b} = r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E$$

$$\boxed{R_{in} = R_B \parallel \left[ r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E \right]}$$

**Output Resistance:**

Setting $v_{in} = 0$ and applying a test voltage $v_x$ at the output:

$$\boxed{R_{out} = R_E \parallel \dfrac{R_S \parallel R_B + r_\pi}{\beta+1}}$$

**Two Practical Applications:**
1. **Buffer amplifier** between a high-impedance source (e.g., signal generator) and a low-impedance load (e.g., speaker) — prevents loading effect.
2. **Impedance matching stage** at the input of a power amplifier or transmission line driver.
3. *(Bonus)* Used as the **final output stage** of multi-stage amplifiers and within op-amp output stages.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (Repeatedly Observed in KTU Valuation)**
> 1. **Sign Error in $A_v$:** Forgetting the negative sign for CE amplifier → **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Using $V_T = 25$ mV instead of 26 mV** without justification — either is acceptable, but **state the value you are using** at the start of the solution.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert $R_C$ and $R_L$ to parallel** — using only $R_C$ instead of $R_C \parallel R_L$ when load is mentioned.
> 4. **Drawing the wrong circuit:** Confusing CB with CC — read the question carefully; the *common* terminal is the one **grounded** (AC-wise).
> 5. **Skipping the assumption:** Not stating "$r_o = \infty$" or "bypassed $R_E$" before the derivation — loses **1 mark** for incomplete problem definition.
> 6. **Forgetting units** in the final numerical answer (e.g., writing $A_v = -6.62$ without saying "V/V" or "dimensionless") — minor penalty but cumulatively costly.
> 7. **In Part B (a), writing paragraphs without a labelled diagram** — at least **2 marks** are reserved for the diagram in KTU scheme.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final-Day Revision Flash Card**

- [ ] **Hybrid-π model** is the foundation of all BJT small-signal analysis — know $r_\pi$, $g_m$, $r_o$ cold.
- [ ] **Master identity:** $g_m = I_C / V_T = \beta / r_\pi$ — appears in *every* derivation.
- [ ] **CE** = high $A_v$, 180° phase shift, moderate $R_{in}$ — the **default** voltage amplifier.
- [ ] **CB** = high $A_v$, 0° phase, **low $R_{in}$** — used in **RF / high-frequency** circuits.
- [ ] **CC** = $A_v \approx 1$, 0° phase, **very high $R_{in}$** and **very low $R_{out}$** — the **buffer**.
- [ ] **Voltage gain with bypassed $R_E$:** $A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$.
- [ ] **Voltage gain with unbypassed $R_E$:** $A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L) / (1 + g_m R_E)$.
- [ ] **Input resistance of CC:** $R_{in} = r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E$ — this is *very large*.
- [ ] **$r_e$ shortcut:** $r_e = 26 \text{ mV} / I_E \text{(mA)} \, \Omega$ — useful for CB amplifier.
- [ ] **Always state assumptions** explicitly: $r_o = \infty$, $V_T = 26$ mV, midband analysis.
- [ ] **Phase inversion = CE only.** CB and CC are in-phase.
- [ ] **Output resistance** of CC is *low* → can drive low-impedance loads.
- [ ] **High-frequency preference:** CB > CE > CC (CB has no Miller effect at input).
- [ ] **DC blocking:** Coupling capacitors $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ are **AC short, DC open** at midband.
- [ ] **Bypass capacitor $C_E$** short-circuits $R_E$ for AC, **stabilises DC** — must be large enough at lowest signal frequency.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RC Coupled Amplifier: Need of Different Components and Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCPT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** RC Coupled Amplifier: Need of Different Components and Design ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **RC Coupled Amplifier (Definition – KTU 2024 Standard):** > An **RC Coupled Amplifier** is a multi-stage transistor amplifier in which the output of one stage is coupled to the input of the next stage through a **resistor–capacitor (RC) network**. The capacitor blocks the DC bias of the preceding stage from disturbing the bias of the succeeding stage, while the resistor allows the AC signal to pass with minimal attenuation. It is the most widely used audio-frequency (AF) voltage amplifier because of its excellent **frequency response**, **high gain**, and **low cost**. The word "RC" stands for **Resistor–Capacitor**, which are the two passive components that form the inter-stage coupling network. A single common-emitter (CE) stage is built first, and two (or more) such stages are cascaded to obtain a large overall voltage gain. The complete amplifier is therefore often called a **Two-Stage RC Coupled CE-CE Amplifier**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** > The student is expected to justify *why* each component (R₁, R₂, R_E, R_C, C_in, C_out, C_E, and the coupling capacitor C_C) is required, and then *design* the component values for a specified quiescent operating point (Q-point) using the **voltage-divider bias** rule. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Think of an RC coupled amplifier as a **relay race of sound waves** in a stadium. | Stadium Element | Electronic Counterpart | Function | |-----------------|------------------------|----------| | A runner with a small voice (first mic) | First CE transistor stage | Produces a weak amplified signal | | A short track (coupling zone) | **R_C and C_C coupling network** | Passes the AC signal but **blocks the DC battery bias** (like a checkpoint that only lets the runners through, not the cheering crowd) | | Energy drink station | DC bias network (R₁, R₂, R_E) | Restores stamina (sets the new operating point) | | Loud speaker (final audience) | Load R_L | Converts electrical signal back to sound | | First-aider | Bypass capacitor C_E | Removes the AC noise picked up around the runner | > [!TIP] > **Plain-English Summary:** A single transistor can amplify a signal only up to a certain limit (typically a voltage gain of a few hundred). To get a much higher gain (e.g., for a microphone pre-amplifier), we chain several such transistors. Between them, we place a **capacitor** (C_C) that acts like a one-way valve: it lets the *fluctuating* audio signal pass, but blocks the *steady* DC voltage that powers each transistor. This protects the bias of the next stage from being disturbed by the previous one. ### 1.3 Why "Different Components" are Needed Every component in the circuit has a **specific, non-negotiable job**. Removing any one of them either destroys the bias, removes the gain, or short-circuits the signal. > [!NOTE] > **The "Big Picture" of an RC Coupled Stage:** > 1. **Active device (BJT)** – Provides the amplification. > 2. **Biasing resistors (R₁, R₂, R_E)** – Fix the Q-point in the active region. > 3. **Collector resistor (R_C)** – Converts collector current variations into voltage variations (load). > 4. **Coupling capacitors (C_in, C_C, C_out)** – Block DC, pass AC. > 5. **Emitter bypass capacitor (C_E)** – Increases AC gain without disturbing DC bias. > 6. **Load resistor (R_L)** – Represents the next stage / speaker. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Need for Cascading (Why a Single Stage is Not Enough) A single CE stage provides a voltage gain of the order of: $$A_v \approx -\frac{R_C \parallel R_L}{r_e}$$ Because $r_e$ is typically $25\ \Omega$ to a few hundred ohms, and $R_C \parallel R_L$ is in the kilo-ohm range, the gain of a single stage is limited to roughly **50 – 500**. For most practical applications (audio amplifiers, op-amp input stages, communication receivers) we need gains of $10^3$ to $10^5$. The overall gain of $n$ cascaded identical stages, assuming they are properly buffered, is: $$A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \times A_{v2} \times \ldots \times A_{vn}$$ In decibels: $$A_{v,\text{total (dB)}} = A_{v1\ (\text{dB})} + A_{v2\ (\text{dB})} + \ldots + A_{vn\ (\text{dB})}$$ Cascading is therefore the **multiplicative** method of building gain. ### 2.2 Need for a Coupling Network (Why DC Coupling Fails) If we directly connected the collector of stage 1 to the base of stage 2 (DC coupling): * The collector DC voltage of stage 1 ($V_{C1}$) would become the base voltage of stage 2, completely **upsetting the bias** of stage 2. * The **drift** (temperature-dependent $I_{CBO}$ variation) of stage 1 would be amplified by stage 2, causing **thermal runaway** in the output stage. * The operating point of each stage would **inter-depend** on the other. The **RC coupling network** solves this elegantly: the **series capacitor C_C** offers infinite reactance to DC (open circuit) and very low reactance to AC signals in the audio band. Hence the DC bias of each stage is **isolated**, but the AC signal is freely transferred. ### 2.3 Function of Each Component — The "Why" Behind the "What" | Component | Symbol | Function | "What if we remove it?" | |-----------|--------|----------|------------------------| | Base resistor 1 | $R_1$ (or $R_B$) | Supplies base current; upper arm of voltage divider | Base floats; transistor saturates or cuts off | | Base resistor 2 | $R_2$ | Lower arm of voltage divider; fixes $V_{B}$ | Bias becomes supply-dependent; unstable Q-point | | Emitter resistor | $R_E$ | Provides DC negative feedback → thermal stability | $I_C$ increases exponentially with temperature → burnout | | Collector resistor | $R_C$ | Converts $\Delta I_C$ into $\Delta V_C$ (acts as load) | No voltage gain; $V_{CE} = V_{CC}$ always | | Coupling capacitor (input) | $C_{in}$ | Blocks DC from source, passes AC into base | DC from source disturbs bias of stage 1 | | Inter-stage coupling capacitor | $C_C$ | Blocks DC between stages, passes AC | DC of stage 1 destroys bias of stage 2 | | Output coupling capacitor | $C_{out}$ | Blocks DC, passes AC to load | DC current flows through load → damage | | Bypass capacitor | $C_E$ | Short-circuits $R_E$ for AC → keeps AC gain high | AC gain drops to $\approx 1$ (gain is lost) | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU question on this topic.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|----------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Thermal voltage | $V_T = kT/q \approx 25\ \text{mV}$ at $27^\circ\text{C}$ | Volts | | 2 | Emitter resistance of BJT | $r_e = V_T / I_E \approx 26\ \text{mV}/I_E(\text{mA})$ | Ohms | | 3 | Base–emitter dynamic resistance | $r_\pi = \beta\, r_e$ | Ohms | | 4 | Transconductance | $g_m = I_C / V_T = 1 / r_e$ | Siemens (A/V) | | 5 | Voltage gain of CE stage (with $C_E$) | $A_v = -g_m\,(R_C \parallel R_L) = -\dfrac{R_C \parallel R_L}{r_e}$ | Dimensionless | | 6 | Voltage gain of CE stage (without $C_E$, i.e. unbypassed) | $A_v = -\dfrac{R_C \parallel R_L}{r_e + R_E}$ | Dimensionless | | 7 | Input impedance | $Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$ | Ohms | | 8 | Output impedance | $Z_{out} \approx R_C$ | Ohms | | 9 | Overall (2-stage) voltage gain | $A_{V,\text{total}} = A_{V1} \times A_{V2}$ | Dimensionless | | 10 | Overall gain in dB | $A_{V,\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\vert A_{V,\text{total}}\vert$ | Decibels | | 11 | Lower cutoff frequency (one capacitor) | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\, R_{\text{eq}}\, C}$ | Hertz | | 12 | Voltage-divider bias (approximate $V_B$) | $V_B \approx V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Volts | | 13 | Emitter voltage | $V_E = V_B - V_{BE} \approx V_B - 0.7$ | Volts | | 14 | Quiescent collector current | $I_{CQ} = V_E / R_E$ | Amperes | | 15 | Quiescent collector–emitter voltage | $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ}(R_C + R_E)$ | Volts | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** RC coupled amplifiers are the **workhorse of audio electronics** — every microphone pre-amplifier, op-amp input buffer, hearing aid, and old transistor radio used one. They are *flat* (constant gain) over a 3-dB bandwidth of roughly **50 Hz to 20 kHz**, which is exactly the human voice + music band. The design steps below show how a KTU examiner expects you to size each resistor and capacitor to land the Q-point at the center of the load line for **maximum symmetrical swing**. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Design Procedure) ### 3.1 Design Specifications — A Standard KTU-Style Problem > **Given:** > $V_{CC} = 12\ \text{V},\ \ \beta = 100,\ \ V_{BE} = 0.7\ \text{V},\ \ I_{CQ} = 2\ \text{mA},\ \ V_{CEQ} = 6\ \text{V}$ (midpoint of load line). > **To design:** $R_1,\ R_2,\ R_C,\ R_E$ and to compute $A_v,\ Z_{in},\ Z_{out}$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 1 — Q-Point and the Required $R_E$ The collector current at the Q-point is set by the emitter current (since $I_C \approx I_E$ for large $\beta$). The emitter voltage is: $$V_E = I_{CQ} \cdot R_E$$ The base voltage (assuming $V_{BE} = 0.7\ \text{V}$) is: $$V_B = V_E + V_{BE} = I_{CQ}\,R_E + 0.7$$ **Step-by-step numerical evaluation** (for a typical KTU problem with $I_{CQ} = 2\ \text{mA}$ and choosing $V_E = 2\ \text{V}$ for good bias stability): $$ \begin{aligned} V_E &= I_{CQ} \cdot R_E \\[2pt] 2\ \text{V} &= 2\ \text{mA} \cdot R_E \\[2pt] R_E &= \frac{2\ \text{V}}{2\ \text{mA}} = 1\ \text{k}\Omega \end{aligned} $$ So $R_E = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 2 — Calculation of $R_C$ from $V_{CEQ}$ Applying KVL across the output loop $V_{CC} \rightarrow R_C \rightarrow$ transistor $\rightarrow R_E \rightarrow$ ground: $$V_{CC} = I_{CQ}\,R_C + V_{CEQ} + I_{CQ}\,R_E$$ Rearranging for $R_C$: $$R_C = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CEQ} - I_{CQ}\,R_E}{I_{CQ}}$$ **Numerical substitution:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_C &= \frac{12\ \text{V} - 6\ \text{V} - (2\ \text{mA})(1\ \text{k}\Omega)}{2\ \text{mA}} \\[2pt] &= \frac{12 - 6 - 2}{2\ \text{mA}} \\[2pt] &= \frac{4\ \text{V}}{2\ \text{mA}} = 2\ \text{k}\Omega \end{aligned} $$ So $R_C = 2\ \text{k}\Omega$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mid-point bias check:** $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}/2 = 6\ \text{V}$ ✔ — maximum symmetrical AC swing is achieved because the Q-point is centred on the DC load line. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Calculation of $R_1$ and $R_2$ (Voltage Divider) The base voltage is $V_B = V_E + V_{BE} = 2 + 0.7 = 2.7\ \text{V}$. The voltage-divider rule gives: $$V_B = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{V_B}{V_{CC}} = \frac{2.7}{12} = 0.225$$ Hence: $$R_1 + R_2 = \frac{R_2}{0.225} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R_1 = R_2 \left(\frac{1}{0.225} - 1\right) = 3.44\,R_2$$ To make the bias **independent of $\beta$** (stiff divider), the current through $R_1$ and $R_2$ should be at least **10 times** the base current $I_B$. $$ \begin{aligned} I_B &= \frac{I_{CQ}}{\beta} = \frac{2\ \text{mA}}{100} = 20\ \mu\text{A} \\[2pt] I_{R_1} &= 10 \cdot I_B = 200\ \mu\text{A} \\[2pt] R_1 + R_2 &= \frac{V_{CC}}{I_{R_1}} = \frac{12\ \text{V}}{200\ \mu\text{A}} = 60\ \text{k}\Omega \end{aligned} $$ Solving the two simultaneous equations $R_1 = 3.44\,R_2$ and $R_1 + R_2 = 60\ \text{k}\Omega$: $$ \begin{aligned} 3.44\,R_2 + R_2 &= 60\ \text{k}\Omega \\[2pt] 4.44\,R_2 &= 60\ \text{k}\Omega \\[2pt] R_2 &= \frac{60}{4.44} = 13.5\ \text{k}\Omega \quad (\text{use standard } 12\ \text{k}\Omega \text{ or } 15\ \text{k}\Omega) \\[2pt] R_1 &= 60\ \text{k}\Omega - 13.5\ \text{k}\Omega = 46.5\ \text{k}\Omega \quad (\text{use standard } 47\ \text{k}\Omega) \end{aligned} $$ So $R_1 \approx 47\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $R_2 \approx 15\ \text{k}\Omega$. --- ### 3.5 Derivation 4 — AC Small-Signal Voltage Gain $A_v$ The internal emitter resistance is: $$r_e = \frac{V_T}{I_E} = \frac{25\ \text{mV}}{2\ \text{mA}} = 12.5\ \Omega$$ Assuming the next stage is also a CE stage presenting an input impedance of $Z_{in} \approx 1.5\ \text{k}\Omega$ (we will compute it below), the AC load seen by this collector is: $$R_{ac} = R_C \parallel Z_{in,\text{next}} = 2\ \text{k}\Omega \parallel 1.5\ \text{k}\Omega$$ $$ \begin{aligned} R_{ac} &= \frac{R_C \cdot Z_{in,\text{next}}}{R_C + Z_{in,\text{next}}} \\[2pt] &= \frac{2\ \text{k}\Omega \times 1.5\ \text{k}\Omega}{2\ \text{k}\Omega + 1.5\ \text{k}\Omega} \\[2pt] &= \frac{3}{3.5}\ \text{k}\Omega = 0.857\ \text{k}\Omega = 857\ \Omega \end{aligned} $$ The small-signal voltage gain (with $C_E$ fully bypassing $R_E$) is: $$ \begin{aligned} A_v &= -\frac{R_{ac}}{r_e} \\[2pt] &= -\frac{857\ \Omega}{12.5\ \Omega} \\[2pt] &\approx -68.6 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output — a hallmark of the common-emitter configuration. --- ### 3.6 Derivation 5 — Input and Output Impedances **Input impedance:** $$ \begin{aligned} r_\pi &= \beta \cdot r_e = 100 \times 12.5\ \Omega = 1.25\ \text{k}\Omega \\[2pt] Z_{in} &= R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi = 47\ \text{k}\Omega \parallel 15\ \text{k}\Omega \parallel 1.25\ \text{k}\Omega \\[2pt] Z_{in} &\approx 1.06\ \text{k}\Omega \end{aligned} $$ **Output impedance:** $$Z_{out} \approx R_C = 2\ \text{k}\Omega$$ --- ### 3.7 Derivation 6 — Lower Cutoff Frequency (Brief) For a single coupling capacitor, the cutoff is: $$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi\, R_{\text{eq}}\, C}$$ where $R_{\text{eq}}$ is the Thevenin resistance seen by the capacitor. * For $C_{in}$: $R_{\text{eq}} = R_S \parallel (R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi)$. * For $C_C$ (between stages): $R_{\text{eq}} = (R_C \parallel Z_{out1}) + (R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi)_{\text{next}}$. * For $C_{out}$: $R_{\text{eq}} = (R_C \parallel R_L)$. * For $C_E$: $R_{\text{eq}} = R_E \parallel \left(r_e + \dfrac{R_S \parallel (R_1 \parallel R_2)}{\beta+1}\right)$. The **dominant** (highest) of these four lower-cutoff frequencies is the overall $f_L$ of that stage. --- ### 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Designed Values | Component / Quantity | Designed Value | Role | |----------------------|----------------|------| | $R_1$ | $47\ \text{k}\Omega$ | Upper bias resistor | | $R_2$ | $15\ \text{k}\Omega$ | Lower bias resistor | | $R_C$ | $2\ \text{k}\Omega$ | Converts $\Delta I_C$ to $\Delta V_C$ | | $R_E$ | $1\ \text{k}\Omega$ | DC stability, thermal feedback | | $C_{in}, C_C, C_{out}$ | $1\ \text{to}\ 10\ \mu\text{F}$ (electrolytic) | Block DC, pass AC | | $C_E$ | $47\ \text{to}\ 100\ \mu\text{F}$ (electrolytic) | Bypass $R_E$ for AC | | $I_{CQ}$ | $2\ \text{mA}$ | Set the operating current | | $V_{CEQ}$ | $6\ \text{V}$ | Centre of load line | | $r_e$ | $12.5\ \Omega$ | Internal emitter resistance | | $A_v$ (per stage) | $\approx -68.6$ | Voltage gain | | $A_{v,\text{total}}$ (2 stages) | $\approx 4707$ | $\approx 73.4\ \text{dB}$ | | $Z_{in}$ | $\approx 1.06\ \text{k}\Omega$ | Driven from previous stage | | $Z_{out}$ | $\approx 2\ \text{k}\Omega$ | Drives next stage / load | > [!TIP] > **Why is the Q-point chosen at $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}/2$?** > Because the maximum unclipped AC swing on the collector is symmetric: the collector can swing up by $V_{CEQ} - V_{CE,\text{sat}}$ and down by $V_{CC} - V_{CEQ}$. Setting them equal gives the **largest possible peak-to-peak output** without distortion. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Two-Stage RC Coupled Amplifier ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SRC["INPUT SOURCE Vs with internal resistance Rs"] nodeS((Vs)) nodeRs((Rs)) end subgraph STAGE1["STAGE 1 - Common Emitter Amplifier"] nodeCin((Cin)) nodeBJT1{{Q1 BJT}} nodeRC1((RC1)) nodeRE1((RE1)) nodeCE1((CE1)) end subgraph COUPLING["RC COUPLING NETWORK"] nodeCC((CC)) end subgraph STAGE2["STAGE 2 - Common Emitter Amplifier"] nodeBJT2{{Q2 BJT}} nodeRC2((RC2)) nodeRE2((RE2)) nodeCE2((CE2)) end subgraph OUT["OUTPUT SECTION"] nodeCout((Cout)) nodeRL((RL)) end nodeS --> nodeRs --> nodeCin --> nodeBJT1 nodeBJT1 --- nodeRC1 nodeBJT1 --- nodeRE1 nodeRE1 --- nodeCE1 nodeBJT1 -- Vc1 --> nodeCC --> nodeBJT2 nodeBJT2 --- nodeRC2 nodeBJT2 --- nodeRE2 nodeRE2 --- nodeCE2 nodeBJT2 -- Vc2 --> nodeCout --> nodeRL ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Signal Path and Role of Each Block ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Stage 1: CE Transistor Q1
DC Bias set by R1, R2, RE1
AC Gain from RC1 parallel rL"] B["Coupling Capacitor CC
Blocks DC of Stage 1
Passes AC signal"] C["Stage 2: CE Transistor Q2
DC Bias set by R1, R2, RE2
AC Gain from RC2 parallel RL"] D["Output Capacitor Cout
Blocks DC from Q2
Passes AC to Speaker RL"] A -- "Vc1 contains DC + small AC" --> B B -- "AC only, DC stripped off" --> C C -- "Vc2 contains DC + amplified AC" --> D D -- "Pure amplified AC signal" --> E["Load RL (Speaker or next stage)"] F["Bypass Capacitor CE1"] -. "Shorts RE1 for AC
preserves high gain" .-> A G["Bypass Capacitor CE2"] -. "Shorts RE2 for AC
preserves high gain" .-> C ``` ### 4.3 Frequency Response of an RC Coupled Amplifier ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LOW["LOW FREQUENCY REGION"] F1["fL1: due to Cin"] F2["fL2: due to CC"] F3["fL3: due to Cout"] F4["fL4: due to CE"] end subgraph MID["MIDBAND FLAT REGION"] FLAT["Constant Gain Av_mid
Bandwidth: 50 Hz to 20 kHz"] end subgraph HIGH["HIGH FREQUENCY REGION"] FH["fH: due to internal
device capacitances Cbe, Cbc"] end F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4 --> FLAT --> FH ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Response Curve:** > * In the **mid-band**, the coupling and bypass capacitors behave as perfect short circuits and the internal device capacitances behave as open circuits → gain is flat and equal to $A_{v,\text{mid}}$. > * In the **low-frequency** region, the reactance of $C_C$, $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, and $C_E$ rises, attenuating the signal — the gain falls at $-20\ \text{dB/decade}$ for each capacitor. > * In the **high-frequency** region, the internal junction capacitances $C_{be}$ and $C_{bc}$ (Miller effect) shunt the signal — the gain falls at $-20\ \text{dB/decade}$. > * The **3 dB bandwidth** is $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ (since $f_H \gg f_L$). ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]** > **Q1. Why is an inter-stage coupling capacitor used in an RC coupled amplifier? What happens if it is removed?** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The inter-stage coupling capacitor $C_C$ is used to **block the DC bias voltage** of the preceding stage from reaching the base of the next stage, while allowing the **AC signal** to pass with very low reactance ($X_C = 1/2\pi f C_C$ is small in the audio range). If it is removed, the DC collector voltage of stage 1 becomes the base bias of stage 2, **shifting its Q-point** away from the centre of the load line. This leads to severe **clipping distortion**, unstable operation, and possible saturation or cutoff of the second stage. *(Full 3-mark answer expected.)* > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]** > **Q2. What is the function of the emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$ in an RC coupled CE amplifier? What is its typical value?** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The emitter resistor $R_E$ is necessary for **DC thermal stability**, but it also reduces the **AC gain** (since the unbypassed $R_E$ appears in the gain expression $A_v = -R_C/(r_e + R_E)$). The bypass capacitor $C_E$ provides a **low-reactance short circuit to AC signals** around $R_E$, effectively removing $R_E$ from the AC equivalent circuit. This **restores the high AC gain** to $-R_C/r_e$ while keeping the DC bias stable. Typical value: electrolytic capacitor of **47 µF to 100 µF**, chosen so that $X_{C_E} \ll R_E$ at the lowest signal frequency (e.g., 100 Hz). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) #### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *Design of a Single-Stage RC Coupled CE Amplifier* > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B1]** > **Design a single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier using an NPN silicon transistor (β = 100, $V_{BE}$ = 0.7 V) to meet the following specifications:** > $V_{CC} = 12\ \text{V},\ I_{CQ} = 2\ \text{mA},\ V_{CEQ} = 6\ \text{V},\ f_L = 100\ \text{Hz},\ R_S = 600\ \Omega,\ R_L = 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega.$ > > **(a) Calculate the values of $R_1,\ R_2,\ R_E$ and $R_C$. (7 marks)** > **(b) Calculate the voltage gain $A_v$, input impedance $Z_{in}$, output impedance $Z_{out}$, and the 3-dB lower cutoff frequency due to $C_E$. (7 marks)** **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** *Step 1 — Choose $V_E$ for stability.* A common choice is $V_E \approx 10\%\ V_{CC} = 1.2\ \text{V}$. We will use $V_E = 2\ \text{V}$ for stronger stability. *Step 2 — Calculate $R_E$:* $$R_E = \dfrac{V_E}{I_{CQ}} = \dfrac{2\ \text{V}}{2\ \text{mA}} = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$$ *Step 3 — Calculate $R_C$ from KVL:* $$R_C = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CEQ} - I_{CQ}\,R_E}{I_{CQ}} = \dfrac{12 - 6 - 2}{2\ \text{mA}} = 2\ \text{k}\Omega$$ *Step 4 — Compute $V_B$:* $$V_B = V_E + V_{BE} = 2 + 0.7 = 2.7\ \text{V}$$ *Step 5 — Apply stiff-divider rule:* Take $I_{R_1} = 10\,I_B = 10\,(I_C/\beta) = 10 \times 20\ \mu\text{A} = 200\ \mu\text{A}$. *Step 6 — Compute $R_1 + R_2$:* $$R_1 + R_2 = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{I_{R_1}} = \dfrac{12\ \text{V}}{200\ \mu\text{A}} = 60\ \text{k}\Omega$$ *Step 7 — Use divider ratio $V_B/V_{CC} = R_2/(R_1+R_2)$:* $$R_2 = (R_1+R_2)\,\dfrac{V_B}{V_{CC}} = 60 \times \dfrac{2.7}{12} = 13.5\ \text{k}\Omega \approx 15\ \text{k}\Omega\ \text{(standard)}$$ *Step 8 — Find $R_1$:* $$R_1 = 60\ \text{k}\Omega - 13.5\ \text{k}\Omega = 46.5\ \text{k}\Omega \approx 47\ \text{k}\Omega\ \text{(standard)}$$ > **Result of (a):** $R_E = 1\ \text{k}\Omega,\ R_C = 2\ \text{k}\Omega,\ R_1 = 47\ \text{k}\Omega,\ R_2 = 15\ \text{k}\Omega$. **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** *Step 1 — Compute $r_e$:* $$r_e = \dfrac{25\ \text{mV}}{I_E} = \dfrac{25\ \text{mV}}{2\ \text{mA}} = 12.5\ \Omega$$ *Step 2 — Compute $r_\pi$:* $$r_\pi = \beta\,r_e = 100 \times 12.5\ \Omega = 1.25\ \text{k}\Omega$$ *Step 3 — Compute $Z_{in}$:* $$Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi = 47\,\text{k}\Omega \parallel 15\,\text{k}\Omega \parallel 1.25\,\text{k}\Omega \approx 1.06\ \text{k}\Omega$$ *Step 4 — Compute AC load $R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L$:* $$R_{ac} = \dfrac{2 \times 4.7}{2 + 4.7} = \dfrac{9.4}{6.7} = 1.403\ \text{k}\Omega$$ *Step 5 — Compute $A_v$:* $$A_v = -\dfrac{R_{ac}}{r_e} = -\dfrac{1403\ \Omega}{12.5\ \Omega} \approx -112.2\ \quad (\text{i.e., } \approx 41\ \text{dB})$$ *Step 6 — Compute $Z_{out}$:* $$Z_{out} \approx R_C = 2\ \text{k}\Omega$$ *Step 7 — Compute $f_L$ due to $C_E$:* Use the standard formula $$f_{L,E} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\, C_E \cdot (R_E \parallel r_e)} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi \cdot C_E \cdot r_e}$$ For $f_L = 100\ \text{Hz}$ and $r_e = 12.5\ \Omega$: $$C_E = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \cdot 100 \cdot 12.5} = \dfrac{1}{7854} = 127\ \mu\text{F} \approx 150\ \mu\text{F (standard)}$$ > **Result of (b):** $A_v \approx -112.2,\ Z_{in} \approx 1.06\ \text{k}\Omega,\ Z_{out} = 2\ \text{k}\Omega,\ C_E \approx 150\ \mu\text{F}$. --- #### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — *Two-Stage Cascade and Frequency Response* > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B2]** > **(a) With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a two-stage RC coupled CE-CE amplifier. Discuss the role of each component. (7 marks)** > **(b) Derive the expression for the overall voltage gain. A two-stage RC coupled amplifier has each stage with $A_v = -50$. If the input signal is 5 mV, find the output voltage and the overall gain in dB. (7 marks)** **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** The two-stage RC coupled CE-CE amplifier consists of: 1. **Stage 1:** Transistor $Q_1$ in CE configuration with bias network $R_{1A}, R_{2A}, R_{EA}$ and collector resistor $R_{CA}$. The bypass capacitor $C_{EA}$ shorts $R_{EA}$ for AC. 2. **Inter-stage coupling:** Capacitor $C_C$ couples the collector of $Q_1$ to the base of $Q_2$ — **blocks DC of $Q_1$, passes AC**. 3. **Stage 2:** Identical CE stage with $Q_2$, bias network $R_{1B}, R_{2B}, R_{EB}$, collector $R_{CB}$, bypass $C_{EB}$. 4. **Output coupling:** Capacitor $C_{out}$ couples the collector of $Q_2$ to load $R_L$, blocking DC. **Role of each component** (refer to the table in Section 2.3 of these notes for the complete mapping). The key idea is that the **bias of each stage is set independently**, the **AC signal is amplified twice**, and the **DC levels are isolated** by the coupling capacitors. **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** *Derivation of overall gain (assuming mid-band, no loading issues):* The input of stage 2 presents an input impedance $Z_{in2}$, which acts as the load $R_{L1}$ for stage 1. The overall gain is: $$ \begin{aligned} A_{V,\text{total}} &= A_{V1} \times A_{V2} \\[2pt] &= \left(-\dfrac{R_{C1} \parallel Z_{in2}}{r_{e1}}\right) \times \left(-\dfrac{R_{C2} \parallel R_L}{r_{e2}}\right) \end{aligned} $$ *Numerical calculation:* Given $A_{V1} = -50$ and $A_{V2} = -50$: $$ \begin{aligned} A_{V,\text{total}} &= (-50) \times (-50) = +2500 \end{aligned} $$ *Output voltage:* $$V_o = A_{V,\text{total}} \times V_{in} = 2500 \times 5\ \text{mV} = 12.5\ \text{V}$$ *Overall gain in dB:* $$A_{V,\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\vert A_{V,\text{total}}\vert = 20\log_{10}(2500) = 20 \times 3.398 = 67.96\ \text{dB} \approx 68\ \text{dB}$$ > **Result of (b):** $V_o = 12.5\ \text{V},\ A_{V,\text{dB}} \approx 68\ \text{dB}$. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic — Read Carefully!** > > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in $A_v$** — CE amplifier inverts phase (180°). If you write $A_v = +R_C/r_e$ you lose 1 mark. > 2. **Using $I_C$ instead of $I_E$ in $r_e = V_T/I_E$** — A common mistake. $I_E = I_C + I_B \approx I_C$ is acceptable, but in rigorous evaluation, examiners want $I_E$ explicitly. > 3. **Not justifying the "10× rule" for stiff voltage divider** — If you simply state $R_1 = 47\ \text{k}\Omega$ without showing that the divider current is $10\,I_B$, you lose the *design rationale* marks. > 4. **Confusing $f_L$ and $f_H$ in the response curve** — $f_L$ is set by the **external** capacitors; $f_H$ by the **internal** junction capacitances (Miller effect). > 5. **Writing $Z_{out} = R_C \parallel R_L$** — Wrong. $Z_{out}$ is the impedance *seen back into the amplifier output terminals* with the source nulled, so $Z_{out} \approx R_C$ (or $R_C \parallel r_c$ for a more accurate model). $R_L$ is the *external* load. > 6. **Skipping units and standard resistor values** — KTU examiners expect E-series standard values (1 kΩ, 1.5 kΩ, 2.2 kΩ, 4.7 kΩ, 47 kΩ, etc.). Always round off to the nearest standard. > 7. **Forgetting to mention "Bypass capacitor $C_E$" in design** — This is a *very common omission* and costs 1 mark directly. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - An **RC coupled amplifier** uses a **resistor–capacitor** network to cascade two (or more) CE stages, blocking DC and passing AC. - The **need for cascading** is to multiply the gain ($A_{V,\text{total}} = A_{V1} \times A_{V2} \times \ldots$). - The **need for $C_C$** is **DC isolation** between stages — without it, the Q-point of stage 2 is destroyed. - **Biasing resistors** ($R_1, R_2, R_E$) fix the Q-point using the **voltage-divider rule**; the divider should be "stiff" ($I_{R_1} \ge 10\,I_B$). - $R_C$ is the **AC load** that converts $\Delta I_C$ to $\Delta V_C$ — it is *not* a current-limiting resistor. - $C_E$ (bypass) is **mandatory for high AC gain**; it shorts $R_E$ only for AC. - $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ are also DC-blocking; they set the **lower cutoff frequency**. - $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}/2$ gives **maximum symmetric swing** (mid-point bias). - $r_e = 25\ \text{mV}/I_E(\text{mA})$ is the **fundamental small-signal parameter** of a BJT. - $A_v = -R_{ac}/r_e$ (with $C_E$) and $A_v = -R_{ac}/(r_e + R_E)$ (without $C_E$). - $Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$ and $Z_{out} \approx R_C$. - $A_{V,\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\vert A_V\vert$ for voltage gain. - Frequency response has **three regions**: low-frequency (capacitive), mid-band (flat), high-frequency (Miller effect). - **Two cascaded CE stages** give a total phase shift of $0°$ (or $360°$ — i.e., *non-inverting overall*). - **Standard capacitor values:** $C_{in}, C_C, C_{out} \sim 1$–$10\ \mu\text{F}$ (electrolytic); $C_E \sim 47$–$100\ \mu\text{F}$ (electrolytic). > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Tip:** Whenever a design question appears, always start with a labelled circuit diagram (3 marks), show the Q-point calculation (3 marks), compute $R_E$ and $R_C$ (3 marks), find $R_1, R_2$ using the 10× rule (3 marks), and end with the AC analysis ($A_v, Z_{in}, Z_{out}$) (2 marks). This 14-mark pattern appears in nearly every KTU model paper.

topics.title: Multistage Amplifiers: Effect of Cascading on Gain and Bandwidth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multistage Amplifiers: Effect of Cascading on Gain and Bandwidth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Multistage Amplifier** is a cascaded arrangement of two or more single-stage amplifier blocks connected in series (typically via coupling networks — RC, direct, or transformer coupling) such that the output of one stage drives the input of the next. The purpose of cascading is to achieve a **larger overall voltage (or power) gain**, a **higher input impedance**, and/or a **lower output impedance** than is practically realizable from a single active device stage.

In formal KTU terminology, for *n* cascaded stages with midband voltage gains $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$, the **overall midband gain** is:

$$A_{total} = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 \cdots A_n$$

When expressed in decibels (dB), the overall gain in dB is the **algebraic sum** of individual stage gains (provided source and load loading effects are neglected or absorbed into the stage gains).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):**
> The Board Examiner expects students to articulate the **Gain–Bandwidth Trade-off** explicitly. Higher gain from cascading *always* comes at the cost of reduced bandwidth, governed by the **Gain–Bandwidth Product (GBP)** figure of merit.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bucket Relay"

Imagine a row of workers, each holding a **bucket with a small leak at the bottom**:

- **Single worker (single stage):** Carries a fixed amount of water (gain) but loses some through the leak (bandwidth limitation). The leak rate represents the high-frequency roll-off.
- **Relay of workers (cascade):** Each worker scoops and passes the bucket to the next. The **total water delivered** (overall gain) multiplies — but each pass adds a little more time, and the cumulative leakage (combined roll-off) means the system "responds slower" to sudden pours (reduced bandwidth).

Just as multiplying buckets multiplies water but the relay takes longer, cascading amplifier stages **multiplies gain** but **shrinks the effective bandwidth**. The leakage-per-worker is the **GBP** — a fundamental design budget that cannot be exceeded.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Voltage Gain (per stage) | $A_v$ | Dimensionless (V/V) or **dB** |
| Bandwidth (per stage) | $BW$ or $\Delta f$ | **Hz (kHz / MHz)** |
| Gain–Bandwidth Product | $GBP$ | **Hz** |
| Upper Cutoff Frequency | $f_H$ | **Hz** |
| Lower Cutoff Frequency | $f_L$ | **Hz** |
| Rise Time | $t_r$ | **seconds (s)** |
| Slew Rate | $SR$ | **V/μs** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants Used in Calculations:**
> - $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.3010$
> - $20 \log_{10}(10) = 20$ dB
> - **Rise-time–bandwidth relation** (single-pole): $t_r \approx \dfrac{0.35}{BW}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Block Model

A multistage amplifier is treated as a **cascade of independent two-port networks**. Two key assumptions simplify analysis:

1. **Inter-stage loading is neglected** (each stage's input impedance is infinite, output impedance is zero) — valid for ideal voltage amplifiers such as op-amp-based stages.
2. **Each stage is single-pole**, contributing one dominant low-frequency pole ($f_L$) and one dominant high-frequency pole ($f_H$).

Under these assumptions, the **voltage gain of stage $j$** as a function of frequency is:

$$A_j(f) = \frac{A_{mj} \left(j\frac{f}{f_{Lj}}\right)}{\left(1 + j\frac{f}{f_{Lj}}\right)\left(1 + j\frac{f}{f_{Hj}}\right)}$$

where $A_{mj}$ is the midband gain, $f_{Lj}$ is the lower $-3$ dB cutoff, and $f_{Hj}$ is the upper $-3$ dB cutoff of the $j$-th stage.

## 2.2 Core Operational Principles

- **Gain Multiplication Principle:** Because stages are in cascade, complex gains **multiply in linear scale** and **add in dB scale**. This is the **"Why"** behind using multistage designs — to achieve gains of 60–100 dB that single transistors cannot deliver with stability.
- **Bandwidth Shrinkage Principle:** Each stage's roll-off **compounds**. For $n$ identical stages, the **effective roll-off slope doubles** to $-20n$ dB/decade at the high-frequency end (and rises to $+20n$ dB/decade near the low-frequency end). The overall $-3$ dB bandwidth therefore **shrinks**.
- **Gain–Bandwidth Product (GBP):** For a single-pole amplifier, the product of midband gain and upper cutoff frequency is a **constant** determined by the active device (transistor's $f_T$ or op-amp's GBP specification). This is the **fundamental design invariant**.
- **Slew Rate Limitation:** In multistage op-amp designs, the **slowest stage's slew rate** dictates the overall system's large-signal response.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` (not raw `\vert`) inside the table cells to avoid breaking markdown parsing.

| \# | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overall Midband Gain | $A_M = \prod_{j=1}^{n} A_{mj} = A_{m1} \cdot A_{m2} \cdots A_{mn}$ | Dimensionless (or dB: $A_{dB} = \sum A_{mj(dB)}$) |
| 2 | Overall Gain in dB | $A_{M(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(A_M)$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 3 | Overall Upper Cutoff (n identical stages) | $f_H^{(n)} = f_H \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$ | Hz |
| 4 | Overall Lower Cutoff (n identical stages) | $f_L^{(n)} = \dfrac{f_L}{\sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}}$ | Hz |
| 5 | Overall Bandwidth (n identical stages) | $BW_n = f_H^{(n)} - f_L^{(n)} \approx f_H^{(n)}$ (when $f_L \ll f_H$) | Hz |
| 6 | Bandwidth Shrinkage Factor | $k_n = \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$ | Dimensionless ($< 1$) |
| 7 | Single-Stage Gain–Bandwidth Product | $GBP_1 = A_{m1} \cdot BW_1$ | Hz |
| 8 | Overall GBP | $GBP_n = A_M \cdot BW_n$ | Hz |
| 9 | High-Frequency Roll-off Slope | $-20n$ dB/decade | dB/decade |
| 10 | Rise Time (single pole) | $t_r \approx \dfrac{0.35}{f_H}$ | seconds |
| 11 | Overall Rise Time (n stages) | $t_r^{(n)} \approx \dfrac{0.35}{f_H^{(n)}}$ | seconds |
| 12 | Slew-Rate Limitation (large signal) | $SR_{system} = \min(SR_1, SR_2, \ldots, SR_n)$ | V/μs |

### 2.4 Numerical Values of the Shrinkage Factor $k_n$ (Must Memorize)

| $n$ (number of stages) | $k_n = \sqrt{2^{1/n}-1}$ | Overall BW vs. Single-Stage |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.000 | 100% (no shrinkage) |
| 2 | 0.6436 | 64.4% |
| 3 | 0.5098 | 51.0% |
| 4 | 0.4350 | 43.5% |
| 5 | 0.3852 | 38.5% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> These formulas underpin the design of **multi-stage op-amp circuits**, **RF/IF amplifier chains in communication receivers**, and **operational transconductance amplifiers (OTAs)**. The GBP concept is the bedrock of analog IC design — every additional gain stage in a feedback amplifier trades bandwidth for loop gain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Overall Gain of *n* Cascaded Stages

**Starting point** — the overall transfer function is the **product** of individual stage transfer functions:

$$A(f) = A_1(f) \cdot A_2(f) \cdots A_n(f) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} A_j(f)$$

In the **midband region**, all reactive elements (coupling and bypass capacitors, internal device capacitances) become negligible. The midband gain of each stage is constant, $A_{mj}$, so:

$$A_M = \prod_{j=1}^{n} A_{mj} = A_{m1} \cdot A_{m2} \cdots A_{mn}$$

In **decibel** form, products convert to sums:

$$A_{M(dB)} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\prod_{j=1}^{n} A_{mj}\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} 20\log_{10}(A_{mj}) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{mj(dB)}$$

**Conclusion:** *Linear gains multiply; dB gains add.*

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Overall Upper Cutoff Frequency for *n* Identical Stages

**Step 1 —** Write the single-stage high-frequency transfer function (single-pole low-pass model):

$$A_j(f) = \frac{A_{mj}}{1 + j(f/f_{Hj})}$$

**Step 2 —** For *n* identical stages in cascade:

$$A(f) = \left[\frac{A_{mj}}{1 + j(f/f_{Hj})}\right]^{n}$$

**Step 3 —** The magnitude response in the high-frequency region:

$$\left|\frac{A(f)}{A_{mj}^n}\right| = \frac{1}{\left[\sqrt{1 + (f/f_{Hj})^2}\right]^{n}}$$

**Step 4 —** Define the **overall $-3$ dB upper cutoff** $f_H^{(n)}$ as the frequency at which the magnitude drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$ of its midband value:

$$\frac{1}{\left[\sqrt{1 + (f_H^{(n)}/f_{Hj})^2}\right]^{n}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 5 —** Invert and square both sides:

$$\left[1 + (f_H^{(n)}/f_{Hj})^2\right]^{n} = 2$$

**Step 6 —** Take the $n$-th root:

$$1 + (f_H^{(n)}/f_{Hj})^2 = 2^{1/n}$$

**Step 7 —** Solve for the cutoff frequency ratio:

$$(f_H^{(n)}/f_{Hj})^2 = 2^{1/n} - 1$$

**Step 8 —** Final closed-form result:

$$\boxed{\,f_H^{(n)} = f_{Hj} \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}\,}$$

This is the **canonical KTU result** for the upper bandwidth of *n* cascaded identical stages.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Overall Lower Cutoff Frequency for *n* Identical Stages

**Step 1 —** Single-stage high-pass response (single-pole):

$$A_j(f) = \frac{A_{mj}}{1 - j(f_{Lj}/f)}$$

**Step 2 —** Magnitude:

$$\left|\frac{A_j(f)}{A_{mj}}\right| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f_{Lj}/f)^2}}$$

**Step 3 —** For *n* identical stages:

$$\frac{1}{\left[\sqrt{1 + (f_{Lj}/f)^2}\right]^{n}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 4 —** Algebraic manipulation (mirror of Section 3.2):

$$\left[1 + (f_{Lj}/f_L^{(n)})^2\right]^{n} = 2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 1 + (f_{Lj}/f_L^{(n)})^2 = 2^{1/n}$$

**Step 5 —** Solve:

$$(f_{Lj}/f_L^{(n)})^2 = 2^{1/n} - 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f_L^{(n)} = \frac{f_{Lj}}{\sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}}$$

$$\boxed{\,f_L^{(n)} = \frac{f_{Lj}}{\sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** The lower cutoff **increases** (moves right) as stages are added — meaning the amplifier becomes less effective at low frequencies.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Gain–Bandwidth Product (GBP) Invariance

For a **single-pole amplifier**, the high-frequency response is:

$$A(f) = \frac{A_m}{1 + j(f/f_H)}$$

Multiplying both sides by $f_H$ and noting that the **open-loop gain–bandwidth product is a device constant** (equal to the transition frequency $f_T$ for a BJT, or the op-amp's unity-gain frequency for op-amps):

$$A_m \cdot f_H = \text{constant} = GBP$$

**For the multistage case:**

$$GBP_n = A_M \cdot f_H^{(n)} = \left(\prod_{j=1}^{n} A_{mj}\right) \cdot \left(f_{Hj} \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n}-1}\right)$$

$$GBP_n = (A_m \cdot f_H)^{n} \cdot f_H^{1-n} \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n}-1}$$

For $n=2$ with $A_m f_H = GBP_1$:

$$GBP_2 = (GBP_1)^2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2^{1/2}-1}}{f_H}$$

The **key insight** is that the *individual* stage's GBP is invariant — it is a property of the **active device**, not the configuration. Cascading *does not* violate the per-stage GBP; rather, it **exponentially multiplies gain while only multiplicatively shrinking bandwidth**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1: Two-Stage Identical Amplifier

**Given:** $n=2$, $A_{m1} = A_{m2} = 100$ (V/V), $f_{H1} = f_{H2} = 20$ kHz, $f_{L1} = f_{L2} = 50$ Hz.

**(a) Overall midband gain:**

$$A_M = 100 \times 100 = 10{,}000 \text{ V/V}$$

In dB: $A_{M(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(10{,}000) = 20 \times 4 = 80$ dB.

**(b) Overall upper cutoff:**

$$f_H^{(2)} = 20{,}000 \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/2} - 1} = 20{,}000 \cdot \sqrt{0.4142} = 20{,}000 \cdot 0.6436$$

$$f_H^{(2)} = 12{,}872 \text{ Hz} \approx 12.87 \text{ kHz}$$

**(c) Overall lower cutoff:**

$$f_L^{(2)} = \frac{50}{\sqrt{0.4142}} = \frac{50}{0.6436} = 77.69 \text{ Hz}$$

**(d) Overall bandwidth:**

$$BW_2 = f_H^{(2)} - f_L^{(2)} = 12{,}872 - 77.69 \approx 12{,}794 \text{ Hz} \approx 12.79 \text{ kHz}$$

**(e) Gain–bandwidth product:**

$$GBP_2 = 10{,}000 \times 12{,}872 = 1.287 \times 10^8 \text{ Hz} = 128.7 \text{ MHz}$$

### Example 2: Three-Stage Cascade

**Given:** $n=3$, $A_{mj} = 50$ V/V, $f_{Hj} = 100$ kHz.

**(a) Overall midband gain:**

$$A_M = 50^3 = 125{,}000 \text{ V/V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_{M(dB)} = 20\log_{10}(125{,}000) \approx 101.94 \text{ dB}$$

**(b) Overall upper cutoff:**

$$f_H^{(3)} = 100{,}000 \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/3} - 1} = 100{,}000 \cdot \sqrt{0.2599} = 100{,}000 \cdot 0.5098$$

$$f_H^{(3)} \approx 50{,}980 \text{ Hz} \approx 50.98 \text{ kHz}$$

**(c) Rise time of single stage:**

$$t_{r1} = \frac{0.35}{100{,}000} = 3.5 \text{ μs}$$

**(d) Rise time of three-stage cascade:**

$$t_{r3} = \frac{0.35}{50{,}980} \approx 6.87 \text{ μs}$$

**(e) Gain–bandwidth product:**

$$GBP_3 = 125{,}000 \times 50{,}980 \approx 6.37 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz} = 6.37 \text{ GHz}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cascaded Amplifier

The diagram below shows the **signal flow** through a three-stage cascade, with each stage's gain and bandwidth annotated. Node identifiers use alphanumeric prefixes (`stgA`, `stgB`, `stgC`) to comply with Mermaid's reserved-word constraints.

```mermaid
graph LR
    inNode[Input Signal Vin] --> stgA[Stage 1 Amplifier]
    stgA -- Intermediate Vout Stage 1 --> stgB[Stage 2 Amplifier]
    stgB -- Intermediate Vout Stage 2 --> stgC[Stage 3 Amplifier]
    stgC --> outNode[Output Signal Vout]
    stgA -. Annotation .- stgAdata[Gain Am1 BW BW1 SR SR1]
    stgB -. Annotation .- stgBdata[Gain Am2 BW BW2 SR SR2]
    stgC -. Annotation .- stgCdata[Gain Am3 BW BW3 SR SR3]
    stgAdata --- stgAdataEq[Am1 eq A1 midband BW1 eq fH1 minus fL1]
    stgBdata --- stgBdataEq[Am2 eq A2 midband BW2 eq fH2 minus fL2]
    stgCdata --- stgCdataEq[Am3 eq A3 midband BW3 eq fH3 minus fL3]
```

## 4.2 Frequency Response Architecture — Single vs. Cascaded

The figure below shows how the **roll-off slope** becomes **steeper** with cascading ($-20n$ dB/decade) and how the $-3$ dB point **shifts leftward** at the high end and **rightward** at the low end.

```mermaid
graph TB
    titleNode[Frequency Response Comparison]
    titleNode --> singleStage[Single Stage Response]
    titleNode --> twoStage[Two Stage Cascaded Response]
    titleNode --> threeStage[Three Stage Cascaded Response]
    singleStage --- singlePts[Midband flat at Am dB Slope negative 20 dB per decade at HF and LF fH1 and fL1 marked at negative 3 dB]
    twoStage --- twoPts[Midband flat at Am squared Slope negative 40 dB per decade at HF and LF fH2 shifted left of fH1 fL2 shifted right of fL1]
    threeStage --- threePts[Midband flat at Am cubed Slope negative 60 dB per decade at HF and LF fH3 shifted further left fL3 shifted further right]
    singleStage --> fH1Node[Cutoff fH1]
    twoStage --> fH2Node[Cutoff fH2 smaller than fH1]
    threeStage --> fH3Node[Cutoff fH3 smallest]
```

## 4.3 Functional State Flow — Gain–Bandwidth Trade-off Logic

The diagram below summarizes the **engineering decision flow** when a designer chooses to cascade stages.

```mermaid
graph TD
    startNode[Required Spec: Gain GT, Bandwidth BWT] --> checkSingle{Single Stage Sufficient?}
    checkSingle -- Yes --> useSingle[Use Single Stage Gain Am eq Gainreq BW eq BWreq GBP eq Am times BW]
    checkSingle -- No --> decideCascade[Cascade n Stages]
    decideCascade --> calcN[Solve for n from Gainreq eq Am to power n]
    calcN --> calcBW[Compute BWn eq BH1 times sqrt of 2 to 1 over n minus 1]
    calcBW --> checkBWT{BWn geq BWT?}
    checkBWT -- Yes --> finalDesign[Accept Design GBP system eq Gainreq times BWn]
    checkBWT -- No --> incStages[Increase n or Select Device with Higher fT]
    incStages --> calcN
    finalDesign --> doneNode[Output Realizable Multistage Amplifier]
```

## 4.4 Summary Schematic of Key Stage Parameters

```mermaid
graph LR
    paramBlock[Per Stage Parameters] --> g1[Midband Gain Am]
    paramBlock --> g2[Upper Cutoff fH]
    paramBlock --> g3[Lower Cutoff fL]
    paramBlock --> g4[Rise Time tr]
    paramBlock --> g5[Slew Rate SR]
    paramBlock --> g6[Gain Bandwidth Product GBP]
    g6 --- g6eq[GBP eq Am times fH]
    g4 --- g4eq[tr approx 0.35 over fH]
    g5 --- g5eq[SRsystem eq min of SRj]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Gain–Bandwidth Product. Why is it considered an important figure of merit for an amplifier?

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Gain–Bandwidth Product (GBP)** of a single-pole amplifier is the product of its midband voltage gain ($A_m$) and its upper $-3$ dB cutoff frequency ($f_H$):

$$GBP = A_m \cdot f_H = \text{constant (for a given active device)}$$

It is considered a fundamental **figure of merit** because:

1. **(1 mark)** It is a **device-inherent constant** — for a BJT, $GBP \approx f_T$ (transition frequency); for an op-amp, it equals the **unity-gain bandwidth** specified in the datasheet.
2. **(1 mark)** It represents the **maximum gain–bandwidth trade-off** available to the designer. Once a device is selected, the product cannot be increased — gain can only be exchanged for bandwidth, or vice versa.
3. **(1 mark)** In multistage designs, this constant is preserved per stage; the *system* GBP grows because the gains multiply, but the per-stage GBP limit is the binding constraint in feedback amplifier design.

---

### Question 2: State the expression for the overall bandwidth of *n* cascaded identical amplifier stages. What is the bandwidth shrinkage factor for 2 and 3 stages?

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For *n* cascaded identical single-pole stages, each with upper cutoff $f_H$:

$$f_H^{(n)} = f_H \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$$

The **bandwidth shrinkage factor** is $k_n = \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$. **(1 mark)**

Values: **(2 marks)**

$$k_2 = \sqrt{2^{0.5} - 1} = \sqrt{0.4142} \approx 0.644$$

$$k_3 = \sqrt{2^{1/3} - 1} = \sqrt{0.2599} \approx 0.510$$

Thus, cascading **shrinks** the bandwidth to about 64.4% (for 2 stages) and 51.0% (for 3 stages) of the single-stage value.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the expression for the overall gain and the overall upper cutoff frequency of *n* cascaded identical amplifier stages.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Overall gain derivation (3 marks):**

For *n* stages, each with midband gain $A_{mj}$, the overall midband gain is the product of individual gains:

$$A_M = \prod_{j=1}^{n} A_{mj}$$

For **identical** stages with $A_{mj} = A_m$:

$$A_M = A_m^n$$

In decibels:

$$A_{M(dB)} = n \cdot A_{m(dB)} = 20 n \log_{10}(A_m)$$

**Step 2 — Overall bandwidth derivation (4 marks):**

The single-stage high-frequency response (single-pole model):

$$A_j(f) = \frac{A_m}{1 + j(f/f_H)}$$

Cascading *n* identical stages:

$$A(f) = \left[\frac{A_m}{1 + j(f/f_H)}\right]^n$$

The magnitude at the new $-3$ dB cutoff $f_H^{(n)}$:

$$\left|\frac{A(f_H^{(n)})}{A_m^n}\right| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{1}{\left[1 + (f_H^{(n)}/f_H)^2\right]^{n/2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Squaring and taking the *n*-th root:

$$1 + (f_H^{(n)}/f_H)^2 = 2^{1/n}$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{f_H^{(n)} = f_H \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Two identical amplifier stages, each with a midband gain of 100 and a bandwidth of 20 kHz, are cascaded. Calculate: (i) overall gain in dB, (ii) overall bandwidth, (iii) overall gain–bandwidth product, (iv) rise time of the cascaded system.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $n = 2$, $A_m = 100$ V/V, $BW_1 = 20$ kHz.

**(i) Overall gain in dB (2 marks):**

$$A_M = 100^2 = 10{,}000 \text{ V/V}$$

$$A_{M(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(10{,}000) = 20 \times 4 = 80 \text{ dB}$$

**(ii) Overall bandwidth (2 marks):**

$$BW_2 = 20 \times \sqrt{2^{0.5} - 1} = 20 \times 0.6436 = 12.87 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iii) Overall GBP (1.5 marks):**

$$GBP_2 = A_M \cdot BW_2 = 10{,}000 \times 12{,}870 = 1.287 \times 10^8 \text{ Hz} \approx 128.7 \text{ MHz}$$

**(iv) Rise time (1.5 marks):**

$$t_{r2} = \frac{0.35}{BW_2} = \frac{0.35}{12{,}870} \approx 27.2 \text{ μs}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A):**
> 1. **(−1 mark)** Students often forget to convert the **linear gain to dB** in part (i) — only linear answer is given. Always state: *"Overall gain = 80 dB."*
> 2. **(−2 marks)** A common error is using $f_H^{(n)} = f_H/n$ instead of $f_H \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n}-1}$. The shrinkage factor is **not** a simple reciprocal.
> 3. **(−1 mark)** Rise time formula $t_r \approx 0.35/BW$ applies only to **single-pole systems**; for cascaded single-pole stages, use the **overall** $BW_n$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concept of gain–bandwidth product in a multistage amplifier. Discuss its significance in the design of cascaded amplifier systems.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Gain–Bandwidth Product is the product of the midband voltage gain and the upper $-3$ dB bandwidth of an amplifier stage. For a single-pole system, $GBP = A_m \cdot f_H$ is a **constant determined by the active device** (e.g., the transistor's transition frequency $f_T$, or an op-amp's unity-gain bandwidth).

**Derivation of Invariance (2 marks):** The single-stage high-frequency transfer function is:

$$A(f) = \frac{A_m}{1 + j(f/f_H)}$$

At the unity-gain frequency $f_T$ (where $|A| = 1$):

$$1 = \frac{A_m}{\sqrt{1 + (f_T/f_H)^2}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f_T \approx A_m \cdot f_H = GBP$$

**Significance in Multistage Design (3 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** **Per-stage limit:** Each stage has a fixed GBP inherited from the active device. The designer cannot violate this constant — gain and bandwidth are exchanged, not added.
2. **(1 mark)** **System advantage:** For *n* cascaded identical stages, the system gain grows as $A_m^n$ while bandwidth shrinks only as $\sqrt{2^{1/n}-1}$. For large $n$, the *system* GBP grows as $(A_m \cdot f_H)^{n-1} \cdot f_H \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n}-1}$ — vastly exceeding any single-stage GBP.
3. **(1 mark)** **Feedback design implication:** In op-amp feedback loops, the loop gain available at any frequency is limited by the GBP. Multistage designs must ensure that the open-loop GBP at the crossover frequency is sufficient to provide stable, distortion-free closed-loop operation.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A three-stage amplifier has identical stages, each with a midband gain of 50 and an upper cutoff frequency of 100 kHz. Calculate: (i) the overall voltage gain, (ii) the overall bandwidth, (iii) the rise time of the cascaded system, (iv) the overall gain–bandwidth product.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $n = 3$, $A_m = 50$ V/V, $f_{H1} = 100$ kHz.

**(i) Overall voltage gain (1.5 marks):**

$$A_M = 50^3 = 125{,}000 \text{ V/V}$$

$$A_{M(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(125{,}000) = 20 \times 5.0969 \approx 101.94 \text{ dB}$$

**(ii) Overall bandwidth (2 marks):**

$$f_H^{(3)} = 100 \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/3} - 1} = 100 \cdot \sqrt{0.2599} = 100 \times 0.5098 \approx 50.98 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iii) Rise time (1.5 marks):**

$$t_{r3} = \frac{0.35}{f_H^{(3)}} = \frac{0.35}{50{,}980} \approx 6.87 \text{ μs}$$

**(iv) Overall GBP (2 marks):**

$$GBP_3 = 125{,}000 \times 50{,}980 \approx 6.37 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz} = 6.37 \text{ GHz}$$

**Comparison table (optional, for bonus):**

| Quantity | Single Stage | 3-Stage Cascade |
|---|---|---|
| Gain (V/V) | 50 | 125,000 |
| Gain (dB) | 33.98 dB | 101.94 dB |
| Bandwidth | 100 kHz | 50.98 kHz |
| Rise Time | 3.5 μs | 6.87 μs |
| GBP | 5 MHz | 6.37 GHz |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> 1. **(−1 mark)** Students sometimes compute $2^{1/3}$ as $\sqrt{2}/2$ (incorrect) or $1.26$ without enough decimals. Use $2^{1/3} \approx 1.2599$ and $\sqrt{0.2599} \approx 0.5098$.
> 2. **(−1.5 marks)** Skipping the dB conversion in part (i). KTU board examiners **deduct marks** if dB is not computed alongside the linear value.
> 3. **(−1 mark)** Confusing the **per-stage GBP** ($A_m \cdot f_H = 5$ MHz) with the **system GBP** ($A_M \cdot f_H^{(3)} = 6.37$ GHz). State both clearly.
> 4. **(−1 mark)** For rise-time calculations, students sometimes use $BW = f_H$ instead of the *overall* $f_H^{(n)}$ — make sure to use the cascaded value.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Must Memorize Before Exam**

- **Multistage amplifier = cascade of 2 or more single-stage amplifiers** to boost overall gain, input impedance, and/or lower output impedance.
- **Overall midband gain** (linear scale): $A_M = A_{m1} \cdot A_{m2} \cdots A_{mn} = A_m^n$ for identical stages.
- **Overall midband gain** (dB scale): $A_{M(dB)} = \sum A_{mj(dB)} = n \cdot A_{m(dB)}$ — *gains add in dB*.
- **Overall upper cutoff** for *n* identical stages: $f_H^{(n)} = f_H \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$ — **bandwidth shrinks**.
- **Overall lower cutoff** for *n* identical stages: $f_L^{(n)} = f_L / \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$ — **lower cutoff rises**.
- **Bandwidth shrinkage factor** $k_n = \sqrt{2^{1/n}-1}$: values to remember — $k_2 \approx 0.644$, $k_3 \approx 0.510$, $k_4 \approx 0.435$.
- **Roll-off slope** becomes $-20n$ dB/decade at high frequency (and $+20n$ dB/decade at low frequency) for *n* cascaded stages.
