- **Gain–Bandwidth Product (GBP)** is a **per-stage device constant**: $GBP = A_m \cdot f_H = f_T$ (for BJT) or unity-gain bandwidth (for op-amp).
- **System GBP** grows as $A_M \cdot f_H^{(n)}$ and can vastly exceed per-stage GBP for large $n$.
- **Rise time** (single-pole approximation): $t_r \approx 0.35 / f_H^{(n)}$ — **rises with cascading**.
- **Slew rate** of the overall system is **limited by the slowest stage**: $SR_{system} = \min(SR_1, SR_2, \ldots, SR_n)$.
- **KTU numerical approach:** Always compute (1) $A_M$ in V/V, (2) $A_M$ in dB, (3) $f_H^{(n)}$ using $k_n$, (4) $t_r$, and (5) GBP — the 5-step solution pattern.
- **Coupling types** (RC, direct, transformer) affect low-frequency response but not midband/high-frequency gain–bandwidth relations derived here.
- **Inter-stage loading** (if not buffered) modifies the per-stage gain — for op-amp stages with $Z_{in} \to \infty$ and $Z_{out} \to 0$, loading is negligible and the formulas apply directly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NMOSFET Amplifiers: MOSFET Circuits at DC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** NMOSFET Amplifiers: MOSFET Circuits at DC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **MOSFET DC Analysis (DC Biasing)** refers to the systematic process of determining the **Quiescent Operating Point (Q-Point)** of a Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET) under zero-input signal conditions. The Q-point is defined by the DC drain current ($I_D$), the gate-to-source voltage ($V_{GS}$), and the drain-to-source voltage ($V_{DS}$). This point must lie in the correct region of operation (cutoff, triode/ohmic, or saturation) for the amplifier to function as intended.

The DC analysis of a MOSFET circuit involves replacing the MOSFET by its large-signal equivalent model (essentially an open-circuit between gate and source because $I_G = 0$ for a MOSFET, and a voltage-controlled current source between drain and source) and then applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** and **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** to the resulting purely resistive network.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Water Tap and Pressure Gauge:**
> Imagine a MOSFET as a **pressurized water tap (faucet)** connected between a water tank (drain, D) and a sink (source, S). 
> - The **gate (G)** is the handle you turn — the harder you twist it (higher $V_{GS}$), the more water flows.
> - The **drain current $I_D$** is the rate of water flow.
> - The **drain-to-source voltage $V_{DS}$** is the water pressure difference between the tank and the sink.
> 
> When the handle is barely touched ($V_{GS} < V_{th}$), **no water flows** — this is the **cutoff region**. 
> When you twist it more, water flows freely — this is the **saturation region** (where amplifiers operate). 
> If the pipe gets blocked downstream, pressure builds up but flow stays constant — this is the **triode/ohmic region** (where the MOSFET acts like a variable resistor).

### 1.3 The Three Operating Regions of an NMOSFET

| Region | Condition on $V_{GS}$ | Condition on $V_{DS}$ | Physical Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutoff** | $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ | Any | No channel formed; $I_D = 0$; MOSFET acts as an **open switch**. |
| **Triode (Ohmic/Linear)** | $V_{GS} \geq V_{th}$ | $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | Continuous channel; MOSFET acts as a **voltage-controlled resistor**; $I_D$ depends on both $V_{GS}$ and $V_{DS}$. |
| **Saturation (Active)** | $V_{GS} \geq V_{th}$ | $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | Channel pinched off; MOSFET acts as a **voltage-controlled current source**; $I_D$ depends only on $V_{GS}$. |

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Parameters

- **Threshold Voltage $V_{th}$ (or $V_{tn}$ for NMOS)**: The minimum gate-to-source voltage required to create a conducting channel. Typical value: **$0.5\,\text{V}$ to $3\,\text{V}$**.
- **Process Conduction Parameter $k_n'$**: A technology parameter (in $\mu\text{A/V}^2$) that depends on oxide capacitance and carrier mobility.
- **Transconductance Parameter $k_n$**: Defined as $k_n = k_n' \cdot \dfrac{W}{L}$, where $W$ is the channel width and $L$ is the channel length.
- **Body/Substrate Effect Parameter $\gamma$**: Accounts for the change in $V_{th}$ when the source is not at body potential.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the 2024 scheme, the module primarily focuses on **enhancement-type NMOSFETs** in DC analysis. The body of the NMOSFET is typically tied to the most negative supply (ground in single-supply circuits) to **eliminate the body effect**, which simplifies $V_{th}$ to a constant.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Quadratic I-V Relationship (Long-Channel Model)

The fundamental DC equations governing the NMOSFET in the three regions are derived from the gradual channel approximation:

**Step 1 — Triode (Ohmic) Region:**
When the channel is continuous and not pinched off, the drain current is given by:

$$I_D = k_n \cdot \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th}) \cdot V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Saturation (Active) Region:**
When $V_{DS}$ reaches the **overdrive voltage** $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$, the channel pinches off at the drain end. Beyond this point, $I_D$ becomes nearly independent of $V_{DS}$ (assuming no channel-length modulation):

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \cdot k_n \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

**Step 3 — Cutoff Region:**
If $V_{GS} < V_{th}$, no conducting channel exists and the drain current is essentially zero:

$$I_D = 0$$

### 2.2 Why Each Step Happens — The Physics

- **Why is $I_D$ zero in cutoff?** Because the gate voltage is insufficient to repel holes from the p-type substrate and attract electrons to form an n-type conducting channel (inversion layer). Without this channel, there are no mobile charge carriers to support current flow.
- **Why does $I_D$ saturate?** As $V_{DS}$ increases, the voltage across the oxide at the drain end decreases. When $V_{DS} = V_{OV}$, the effective voltage at the drain end is zero, meaning the channel thickness shrinks to zero at that point (**pinch-off**). Any additional $V_{DS}$ drops across the narrow depletion region, but the current remains limited by the channel's ability to inject carriers — hence **saturation**.
- **Why a quadratic $I_D$ dependence on $V_{GS}$?** The charge density in the inversion layer is proportional to $(V_{GS} - V_{th})$, and the drift velocity of carriers is proportional to the lateral electric field. The product yields the square-law relationship.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU exam problem on MOSFET DC analysis.**

| # | Equation | Region | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_D = 0$ | Cutoff | $I_D$ in A | Switch-off state in digital logic |
| 2 | $I_D = k_n \left[(V_{GS} - V_{th}) V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$ | Triode | $k_n$ in A/V² | Used in **transmission gates** & analog switches |
| 3 | $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2} k_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ | Saturation | $I_D$ in A | **Primary biasing equation** for amplifiers |
| 4 | $k_n = k_n' \cdot \dfrac{W}{L}$ | All | $k_n'$ in A/V² | **Device sizing parameter** in IC design |
| 5 | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | Saturation | $V_{OV}$ in V | **Overdrive voltage** — key design parameter |
| 6 | $g_m = k_n (V_{GS} - V_{th}) = \sqrt{2 k_n I_D}$ | Saturation | $g_m$ in S (Siemens) | **Transconductance** — gain metric for small-signal |
| 7 | $V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$ | All (with body effect) | $\gamma$ in V½ | Accounts for **substrate bias effect** |
| 8 | $P_{DISS} = I_D \cdot V_{DS}$ | Saturation | $P$ in W | DC power consumption in amplifier stages |

### 2.4 Common DC Biasing Configurations

The four most common NMOSFET biasing topologies tested in KTU exams are:

1. **Fixed (Gate) Bias** — A single voltage source sets $V_{GS}$.
2. **Self-Bias (Source Bias)** — A source resistor $R_S$ creates feedback biasing.
3. **Voltage-Divider Bias** — A resistive divider sets $V_G$ independently.
4. **Source-Bias with Gate Resistor** — Simplest practical bias for discrete designs.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

DC biasing is the **foundational first step** in amplifier design. The Q-point must be:
- **Stable** against temperature variations, supply voltage drifts, and device parameter mismatches.
- **Located in the saturation region** so that the MOSFET acts as a transconductance amplifier (current source controlled by input voltage).
- **Centrally positioned** on the load line to allow maximum undistorted output swing for both positive and negative signal excursions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world applications:** Every CMOS op-amp, every analog-to-digital converter (ADC) input stage, every radio-frequency (RF) low-noise amplifier (LNA) in your mobile phone begins with a careful DC bias design. The famous **"0.18 µm CMOS process"** used in billions of chips has its $V_{th}$ precisely controlled so that DC biasing yields predictable, repeatable $I_D$ across millions of devices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Saturation Current $I_D$ from First Principles (Long-Channel Square Law)

We start from the **gradual channel approximation** and integrate the channel charge per unit length from source ($y=0$, $V(0)=0$) to drain ($y=L$, $V(L)=V_{DS}$).

The charge per unit area in the inversion layer at position $y$ is:

$$Q_i(y) = -C_{ox} \cdot [V_{GS} - V(y) - V_{th}]$$

The drain current (drift-dominated) is:

$$I_D = -\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \int_0^{V_{DS}} [V_{GS} - V - V_{th}] \, dV$$

Evaluating the integral step-by-step:

$$I_D = -\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th})V - \frac{V^2}{2} \right]_0^{V_{DS}}$$

$$I_D = k_n \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th}) V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right] \quad \text{...(Triode Region)}$$

Now, for the **saturation region**, the drain voltage at pinch-off equals $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$. We substitute $V_{DS} = V_{OV}$ into the triode equation:

$$I_{D,sat} = k_n \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th})(V_{GS} - V_{th}) - \frac{(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2}{2} \right]$$

$$I_{D,sat} = k_n \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 - \frac{1}{2}(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 \right]$$

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} k_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 \quad \text{...(Saturation Region)}$$

This is the **fundamental square-law I-V equation** in saturation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Q-Point Analysis of a Standard Common-Source Amplifier (Voltage-Divider Bias)

Consider the canonical NMOSFET common-source amplifier with voltage-divider biasing. The circuit consists of: a drain resistor $R_D$, a source resistor $R_S$ (possibly bypassed by $C_S$ for AC), and a gate voltage divider $R_1$ (from $V_{DD}$ to gate) and $R_2$ (from gate to ground).

**Step 1: Find the gate voltage $V_G$ using the unloaded voltage divider.**

Because the MOSFET gate draws no DC current ($I_G = 0$), no current flows through $R_1$ and $R_2$. Hence, the voltage divider is unloaded:

$$V_G = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2: Apply KVL around the gate-source loop.**

The source voltage is $V_S = I_D \cdot R_S$. Therefore:

$$V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - I_D R_S$$

**Step 3: Assume saturation region and substitute the square-law equation.**

$$\frac{1}{2} k_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 = I_D$$

We now have **two equations** in two unknowns ($I_D$ and $V_{GS}$). Let $V_{GS}$ be the independent variable. Substitute $I_D$:

$$V_{GS} = V_G - R_S \cdot \frac{1}{2} k_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

This is a **quadratic equation** in $V_{GS}$. Expanding and rearranging:

$$\frac{1}{2} k_n R_S \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 + V_{GS} - V_G = 0$$

Let $x = V_{GS} - V_{th}$. Then $V_{GS} = x + V_{th}$:

$$\frac{1}{2} k_n R_S \cdot x^2 + x + V_{th} - V_G = 0$$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$x = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 2 k_n R_S (V_{th} - V_G)}}{k_n R_S}$$

Since $x = V_{GS} - V_{th} \geq 0$ (must be in saturation), we select the **physically valid positive root**. Then $V_{GS} = x + V_{th}$, and $I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n x^2$.

**Step 4: Verify saturation by computing $V_{DS}$.**

Applying KVL from drain to source:

$$V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D (R_D + R_S)$$

Saturation is confirmed if:

$$V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$$

If this condition fails, the MOSFET operates in the **triode region** and the triode equation must be used instead — a common KTU exam trap.

### 3.3 Worked Example: KTU-Style Numerical Problem

**Problem Statement:**
An NMOSFET has $V_{th} = 1.5\,\text{V}$, $k_n = 0.4\,\text{mA/V}^2$. It is biased in a common-source amplifier with $V_{DD} = 12\,\text{V}$, $R_D = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_S = 1.5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_1 = 1\,\text{M}\Omega$, $R_2 = 500\,\text{k}\Omega$. Find the Q-point $(I_D, V_{GS}, V_{DS})$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute gate voltage.**

$$V_G = 12 \cdot \frac{500\,\text{k}}{1\,\text{M} + 500\,\text{k}} = 12 \cdot \frac{500}{1500} = 4\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2: Set up the KVL equation.**

$$V_{GS} = 4 - 1.5 \cdot I_D \quad \text{(with } I_D \text{ in mA)}$$

**Step 3: Apply saturation equation.**

$$I_D = 0.5 \cdot 0.4 \cdot (V_{GS} - 1.5)^2 = 0.2 \cdot (V_{GS} - 1.5)^2$$

**Step 4: Solve the coupled equations.**

Substituting $V_{GS} = 4 - 1.5 I_D$ into the saturation equation:

$$I_D = 0.2 \cdot (4 - 1.5 I_D - 1.5)^2 = 0.2 \cdot (2.5 - 1.5 I_D)^2$$

Expanding the square:

$$I_D = 0.2 \cdot (6.25 - 7.5 I_D + 2.25 I_D^2)$$

$$I_D = 1.25 - 1.5 I_D + 0.45 I_D^2$$

Rearranging into standard quadratic form:

$$0.45 I_D^2 - 2.5 I_D + 1.25 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$I_D = \frac{2.5 \pm \sqrt{6.25 - 4 \cdot 0.45 \cdot 1.25}}{2 \cdot 0.45} = \frac{2.5 \pm \sqrt{6.25 - 2.25}}{0.9} = \frac{2.5 \pm 2}{0.9}$$

This gives two solutions: $I_D = 5\,\text{mA}$ or $I_D = 0.556\,\text{mA}$.

**Step 5: Check physical validity.**

- For $I_D = 5\,\text{mA}$: $V_{GS} = 4 - 7.5 = -3.5\,\text{V}$ — **invalid** because $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ (MOSFET would be in cutoff).
- For $I_D = 0.556\,\text{mA}$: $V_{GS} = 4 - 0.833 = 3.167\,\text{V}$ — **valid**, since $V_{GS} > V_{th}$.

**Step 6: Compute $V_{DS}$.**

$$V_{DS} = 12 - 0.556 \cdot (4.7 + 1.5) = 12 - 0.556 \cdot 6.2 = 12 - 3.447 = 8.553\,\text{V}$$

**Step 7: Verify saturation.**

$$V_{DS} = 8.553\,\text{V} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th} = 3.167 - 1.5 = 1.667\,\text{V} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Q-Point:** $I_D = 0.556\,\text{mA}$, $V_{GS} = 3.17\,\text{V}$, $V_{DS} = 8.55\,\text{V}$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Q-Point Problems

| Step | Action | Tool / Equation | Pitfall to Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compute $V_G$ (if voltage-divider bias) | Voltage divider rule | Do not forget $I_G = 0$ for MOSFET |
| 2 | Write KVL on gate-source loop | $V_{GS} = V_G - I_D R_S$ | Sign convention: $V_S$ is positive if $R_S$ connected to ground |
| 3 | Assume saturation | Use $I_D = 0.5 k_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ | If $V_G < V_{th} + I_D R_S$, MOSFET is in cutoff |
| 4 | Solve coupled equations | Quadratic formula | Always check both roots for physical validity |
| 5 | Compute $V_{DS}$ | KVL: $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D(R_D + R_S)$ | Forgetting $R_S$ in the loop is a common mistake |
| 6 | Verify saturation | $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | If violated, redo using triode equation |
| 7 | Compute $g_m$ (if asked) | $g_m = k_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})$ | Units: Siemens (S) or mA/V |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Common-Source NMOSFET Amplifier with Voltage-Divider Bias (DC Equivalent)

The following Mermaid block-diagram captures the **functional DC architecture** of the biasing network and signal path interactions. This is the topology analyzed in Section 3.3.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[VDD DC Supply 12V] -->|R1 1M| B[Gate Node G]
    A -->|RD 4.7k| C[Drain Node D]
    B -->|Gate Source Zero Current| D[NMOSFET Channel]
    C --> D
    D -->|Source Node S| E[Source Resistor RS 1.5k]
    B -.->|R2 500k| F[Ground Reference]
    E --> F
    F -->|Return Path| A
    G[QG 4V at Gate] --> B
    H[ID 0.556 mA flows] --> C
    I[VDS 8.55V across] --> C
    J[VGS 3.17V controls] --> D
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#404040,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 MOSFET I-V Characteristic Curves (Functional Regions Map)

This block diagram maps the **decision logic** an engineer follows when identifying the operating region of an NMOSFET at a given bias point.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Compute VGS and VDS] --> B{VGS Less Than Vth}
    B -->|Yes| C[Region: CUTOFF]
    B -->|No| D{Compute VOV}
    D --> E{VDS Less Than VOV}
    E -->|Yes| F[Region: TRIODE OHMIC]
    E -->|No| G[Region: SATURATION ACTIVE]
    C --> H[ID equals 0 mA]
    F --> I[ID from Triode Equation]
    G --> J[ID equals Half kN VOV squared]
    style A fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#808080,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Load-Line Analysis Schematic (Conceptual Block)

The **DC load line** graphically represents the constraint imposed by the output loop KVL equation $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D (R_D + R_S)$. The intersection of the load line with the device's $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ characteristic curve yields the Q-point.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[VDD on V axis] -->|Intercept| B[ID max = VDD divide RD plus RS]
    B -->|Slope| C[Load Line in ID vs VDS plane]
    C -->|Intersect with curve family| D[Q Point Coordinates]
    D -->|VGS parameter| E[VGS1 Curve]
    D -->|VGS parameter| F[VGS2 Curve]
    D -->|VGS parameter| G[VGS3 Curve]
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#ffc000,color:#000000
```

### 4.4 Operating Region Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Start: Given VGS, VDS, Vth] --> Q1{Is VGS Greater Than Vth}
    Q1 -->|No| Cutoff[MOSFET OFF: ID is Zero]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is VDS Greater or Equal to VGS minus Vth}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Sat[Saturation: Use Half kN VOV squared]
    Q2 -->|No| Tri[Triode: Use Full Quadratic Equation]
    Cutoff --> End[Q-Point Established]
    Sat --> End
    Tri --> End
    style Start fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style Cutoff fill:#808080,color:#ffffff
    style Sat fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
    style Tri fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
    style End fill:#ffc000,color:#000000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Define the threshold voltage $V_{th}$ of an NMOSFET. Explain what happens to the drain current when $V_{GS} < V_{th}$.

**Model Answer:**

The **threshold voltage $V_{th}$** is defined as the minimum gate-to-source voltage required to induce a conducting inversion layer (channel) between the source and drain terminals of an NMOSFET. Below this voltage, the surface of the p-type substrate beneath the gate oxide remains in accumulation or weak inversion, and no significant current can flow.

When $V_{GS} < V_{th}$, the NMOSFET operates in the **cutoff region**. The drain current is essentially zero ($I_D \approx 0$), and the device behaves as an **open switch** between drain and source. This is the OFF state in digital CMOS logic.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Distinguish between the **triode** and **saturation** regions of operation of an NMOSFET. Write the corresponding $I_D$ equations.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Triode Region | Saturation Region |
|---|---|---|
| Condition | $V_{GS} \geq V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} < V_{OV}$ | $V_{GS} \geq V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} \geq V_{OV}$ |
| Channel | Continuous from source to drain | Pinched off at drain end |
| Behavior | Voltage-controlled resistor | Voltage-controlled current source |
| $I_D$ equation | $I_D = k_n \left[(V_{GS} - V_{th})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$ | $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2}k_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ |
| $I_D$ dependence | Depends on both $V_{GS}$ and $V_{DS}$ | Depends only on $V_{GS}$ |
| Application | Analog switches, transmission gates | Amplifiers (linear region) |

---

### Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks Total)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Question A:**

**(a)** Derive the DC drain current expression of an NMOSFET operating in the **saturation region**, starting from the gradual channel approximation. State clearly all assumptions made. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the NMOSFET circuit shown, determine the Q-point $(I_D, V_{GS}, V_{DS})$ and confirm the region of operation.

Given: $V_{th} = 2\,\text{V}$, $k_n = 0.5\,\text{mA/V}^2$, $V_{DD} = 15\,\text{V}$, $R_D = 6\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_S = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_1 = 2\,\text{M}\Omega$, $R_2 = 1\,\text{M}\Omega$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Long-channel device (channel length modulation $\lambda = 0$).
2. Body is tied to source, so body effect is neglected ($V_{SB} = 0$).
3. Gradual channel approximation is valid.
4. Drift current dominates; diffusion current is neglected.

**Derivation:**

The charge per unit area in the inversion layer at position $y$ along the channel:

$$Q_i(y) = -C_{ox}\bigl[V_{GS} - V(y) - V_{th}\bigr]$$

The drain current (drift-dominated):

$$I_D = -\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\int_0^{V_{DS}}\bigl[V_{GS} - V - V_{th}\bigr]\,dV$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS} - V_{th})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

Defining $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}$:

$$I_D = k_n\left[(V_{GS} - V_{th})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right] \quad \text{(Triode)}$$

At pinch-off, $V_{DS} = V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$. Substituting:

$$I_{D,sat} = k_n\left[(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 - \frac{(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2}{2}\right] = \frac{1}{2}k_n(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Compute gate voltage** (unloaded divider, $I_G = 0$):

$$V_G = 15 \cdot \frac{1\,\text{M}}{2\,\text{M} + 1\,\text{M}} = 5\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2: KVL on gate-source loop** ($I_D$ in mA):

$$V_{GS} = V_G - I_D R_S = 5 - 2 I_D$$

**Step 3: Saturation equation:**

$$I_D = 0.5 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (V_{GS} - 2)^2 = 0.25 (V_{GS} - 2)^2$$

**Step 4: Substitute and solve:**

$$I_D = 0.25 (5 - 2 I_D - 2)^2 = 0.25 (3 - 2 I_D)^2$$

$$I_D = 0.25 (9 - 12 I_D + 4 I_D^2) = 2.25 - 3 I_D + I_D^2$$

$$I_D^2 - 4 I_D + 2.25 = 0$$

$$I_D = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{16 - 9}}{2} = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{7}}{2} = \frac{4 \pm 2.646}{2}$$

Roots: $I_D = 3.323\,\text{mA}$ or $I_D = 0.677\,\text{mA}$.

For $I_D = 3.323\,\text{mA}$: $V_{GS} = 5 - 6.646 = -1.646\,\text{V}$ — **invalid** (cutoff).
For $I_D = 0.677\,\text{mA}$: $V_{GS} = 5 - 1.354 = 3.646\,\text{V}$ — **valid**.

**Step 5: Compute $V_{DS}$:**

$$V_{DS} = 15 - 0.677 \cdot (6 + 2) = 15 - 5.416 = 9.584\,\text{V}$$

**Step 6: Verify saturation:**

$$V_{DS} = 9.584\,\text{V} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th} = 3.646 - 2 = 1.646\,\text{V} \quad \checkmark$$

**Q-Point:** $I_D = 0.677\,\text{mA}$, $V_{GS} = 3.65\,\text{V}$, $V_{DS} = 9.58\,\text{V}$, **Saturation region confirmed**.

---

### Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks Total)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Question B:**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the **voltage-divider biasing** scheme for an NMOSFET amplifier. List its advantages over fixed bias. (7 marks)

**(b)** An NMOSFET is biased with $V_{GS} = 5\,\text{V}$, $V_{th} = 1.5\,\text{V}$, $k_n = 1\,\text{mA/V}^2$. If $V_{DS} = 8\,\text{V}$, determine: (i) drain current $I_D$, (ii) region of operation, (iii) transconductance $g_m$, and (iv) DC power dissipation. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

**Circuit description:** A voltage-divider biasing network consists of two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ forming a divider across $V_{DD}$, with the gate connected to the midpoint. The MOSFET drain is connected to $V_{DD}$ through $R_D$, and the source is connected to ground through $R_S$ (with optional bypass capacitor $C_S$).

**Functional flow:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[VDD] -->|R1| B[Gate Node]
    A -->|RD| C[Drain Node]
    B --> D[NMOSFET]
    C --> D
    D -->|RS| E[Ground]
    A -->|R2| E
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#404040,color:#ffffff
```

**Advantages over fixed bias:**

1. **Q-point stability:** The gate voltage $V_G$ is set by the resistor ratio, making it largely independent of MOSFET parameter variations (e.g., $V_{th}$ spread).
2. **Temperature stability:** Negative feedback through $R_S$ stabilizes $I_D$ against thermal drift in $V_{th}$ and $k_n$.
3. **Wider operating range:** Works for a broader range of device parameters.
4. **Larger input impedance:** The biasing network adds $R_1 \Vert R_2$ to the gate, but is still high (megaohms).

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Compute $I_D$ assuming saturation:**

$$I_D = 0.5 \cdot 1 \cdot (5 - 1.5)^2 = 0.5 \cdot 12.25 = 6.125\,\text{mA}$$

**(ii) Region of operation:**

$$V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th} = 3.5\,\text{V}$$

Since $V_{DS} = 8\,\text{V} \geq 3.5\,\text{V}$, the MOSFET is in **saturation** ✓.

**(iii) Transconductance $g_m$:**

$$g_m = k_n (V_{GS} - V_{th}) = 1 \cdot 3.5 = 3.5\,\text{mA/V} = 3.5\,\text{mS}$$

**(iv) DC power dissipation:**

$$P_{DISS} = I_D \cdot V_{DS} = 6.125 \cdot 8 = 49\,\text{mW}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check both roots of the quadratic:** Always verify the physical validity of both solutions; the larger root often yields $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ (cutoff), which is invalid.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify saturation after computing $V_{DS}$:** The saturation assumption can fail if $R_D$ is small or $R_S$ is large; this requires re-solving with the triode equation.
> 3. **Sign errors in KVL:** When $R_S$ is present, $V_{GS} = V_G - I_D R_S$, NOT $V_G + I_D R_S$.
> 4. **Forgetting the body effect:** If the source is NOT tied to the body, $V_{th}$ must be recomputed using $V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$.
> 5. **Not converting units:** $I_D$ in mA and $k_n$ in mA/V² must be consistent, or you must convert to A and A/V².

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Three regions of NMOSFET:** Cutoff ($V_{GS} < V_{th}$), Triode ($V_{DS} < V_{OV}$), Saturation ($V_{DS} \geq V_{OV}$).
- ✅ **Square law in saturation:** $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2}k_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ — the single most important equation.
- ✅ **Triode equation:** $I_D = k_n \left[(V_{GS} - V_{th})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$.
- ✅ **Overdrive voltage $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$** is the key design parameter.
- ✅ **Gate draws no DC current** ($I_G = 0$), so voltage dividers are unloaded.
- ✅ **Always check both roots** of the quadratic; reject the one giving $V_{GS} < V_{th}$.
- ✅ **Always verify saturation** at the end: $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$.
- ✅ **Voltage-divider bias** is the most stable and is the standard for amplifier design.
- ✅ **Transconductance $g_m$** is the small-signal gain parameter: $g_m = k_n V_{OV} = \sqrt{2 k_n I_D}$.
- ✅ **Body effect** can be eliminated by tying the body to the source.
- ✅ **DC power dissipation** is $P_D = I_D \cdot V_{DS}$ — important for thermal design.
- ✅ **Q-point stability** is the central goal of biasing: immune to $\Delta V_{th}$, $\Delta k_n$, $\Delta T$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MOSFET as an Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** MOSFET as an Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **MOSFET (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor) operating as an amplifier** is a three-terminal semiconductor device in which a small time-varying input voltage applied between the **Gate (G)** and **Source (S)** terminals controls a much larger time-varying output current flowing between the **Drain (D)** and **Source (S)** terminals. For linear amplification, the MOSFET must be biased in the **Saturation (Active) Region** of operation, where the drain current $I_D$ is controlled by the gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$ through a quadratic transfer characteristic.

The amplifying action occurs through **transconductance** ($g_m$), defined as the rate of change of drain current with respect to gate-to-source voltage at the operating (quiescent) point, with the drain-to-source voltage held constant.

$$g_m = \left.\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\right|_{V_{DS} = \text{const.}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For the MOSFET to act as a **linear voltage amplifier**, the device must operate in the **Saturation Region** with the condition $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TH}$. The transistor behaves as a **Voltage-Controlled Current Source (VCCS)** for small AC signals superimposed on the DC bias.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a MOSFET amplifier like a **smart water tap** in a large irrigation system:

- The **Gate (G)** is the **lightweight handle** of the tap. You only need to apply a tiny twisting force (a small voltage $v_{gs}$) to operate it.
- The **Drain (D)** is the **high-pressure water main** (the high voltage supply $V_{DD}$) that delivers the large water flow.
- The **Source (S)** is the **outlet pipe** that delivers the controlled water to the crops.
- The **oxide layer** beneath the gate is the **insulated grip** — no water (current) flows into the handle, making the input almost effortless.

By **biasing the tap** at a partially-open steady position (DC operating point) and then applying a small wiggle to the handle, you can produce a large, proportional variation in the water flow. That proportional, scaled-up output **is amplification**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for Students:**
> - **Small input voltage (Gate)** → **Large output current (Drain)**
> - The **gate draws virtually no DC current** because of the insulating $\text{SiO}_2$ layer.
> - This is why MOSFETs have an **extremely high input impedance** ($\geq 10^{12}\ \Omega$) compared to BJTs.

## 1.3 Why MOSFETs Are Used as Amplifiers

1. **High input impedance** — easy to cascade multiple stages without loading effects.
2. **Low power consumption** — gate draws negligible DC current.
3. **Excellent scalability** — ideal for **VLSI and CMOS integrated circuits**.
4. **Good thermal stability** — less prone to thermal runaway than BJTs.
5. **Quadratic transfer characteristic** — provides moderate gain with predictable linearity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conditions for MOSFET Amplification

For the MOSFET to function as a **linear amplifier**, the following three conditions must be simultaneously satisfied:

1. **Biasing in the Saturation Region:**
$$V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TH}$$
   This ensures the channel is "pinched off" near the drain, and $I_D$ becomes a function of $V_{GS}$ only.

2. **DC Operating Point (Q-Point) Established:**
   A suitable $V_{GS,Q}$ and $I_{D,Q}$ must be set so that the transistor sits in the middle of the transfer curve, allowing **maximum undistorted swing** for the AC input.

3. **Small-Signal Operation:**
   The instantaneous input signal $v_{gs}(t)$ must be **small** compared to the overdrive voltage $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$, so that the non-linear quadratic $I_D$–$V_{GS}$ relation can be **linearized** using a Taylor series expansion about the Q-point.

## 2.2 The DC Transfer Characteristic (Saturation)

The drain current in saturation follows the **square-law equation**:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

where:
- $\mu_n$ = electron mobility in the channel (cm²/V·s)
- $C_{ox}$ = gate-oxide capacitance per unit area (F/cm²)
- $W/L$ = aspect ratio of the MOSFET
- $V_{TH}$ = threshold voltage

## 2.3 Small-Signal Parameters of the MOSFET

When a small AC signal $v_{gs}$ is superimposed on the DC bias $V_{GS,Q}$, the resulting small-signal drain current is:

$$i_d = g_m \cdot v_{gs} + \frac{v_{ds}}{r_o}$$

The two small-signal parameters are:

**Transconductance $g_m$:**
$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH}) = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}$$

**Output Resistance $r_o$ (Channel-Length Modulation):**
$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{V_A}{I_D}$$

where $\lambda$ is the channel-length modulation parameter (units: V⁻¹) and $V_A$ is the Early voltage.

## 2.4 Three Basic Amplifier Configurations

| Configuration | Input Terminal | Output Terminal | Common Terminal | Voltage Gain $\vert A_v \vert$ | Input Impedance | Output Impedance |
|---------------|----------------|------------------|------------------|--------------------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Common Source (CS)** | Gate | Drain | Source (grounded) | Large (Inverting) | Very High | Moderate |
| **Common Drain (CD) / Source Follower** | Gate | Source | Drain (AC grounded) | Slightly < 1 (Non-inverting) | Very High | Low |
| **Common Gate (CG)** | Source | Drain | Gate (AC grounded) | Large (Non-inverting) | Low | Moderate |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description / Application |
|---|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | $I_{D,Q} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$ | DC drain current (saturation) |
| 2 | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH}) = \frac{2 I_{D,Q}}{V_{OV}}$ | Transconductance at Q-point |
| 3 | $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L} I_{D,Q}}$ | $g_m$ expressed in terms of $I_D$ |
| 4 | $r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_{D,Q}} = \frac{V_A}{I_{D,Q}}$ | Output resistance due to channel-length modulation |
| 5 | $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | Voltage gain of CS amplifier (with $r_o$) |
| 6 | $A_v = -g_m R_D$ | Voltage gain of CS amplifier (ideal, $r_o \to \infty$) |
| 7 | $A_v = \frac{g_m (R_S \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m (R_S \parallel r_o)}$ | Voltage gain of Source Follower |
| 8 | $A_v = g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | Voltage gain of Common-Gate amplifier |
| 9 | $R_{in} \approx \infty$ | Input resistance (gate is insulated) |
| 10 | $R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o$ | Output resistance of CS amplifier |
| 11 | $A_{i,CS} = \infty$ | Current gain of CS amplifier (ideal) |
| 12 | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | Overdrive voltage (effective gate drive) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Reference:**
> - $g_m$ → Siemens (S), typically **mA/V** or **mS** in MOSFET amplifiers
> - $r_o$ → Ohms ($\Omega$), typically **k$\Omega$** range
> - $\lambda$ → V⁻¹
> - $V_A$ → Volts (V)

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

MOSFET amplifiers form the **front-end of every modern electronic system**:
- **Audio amplifiers** in smartphones, headphones, and hearing aids use CMOS source followers for impedance matching.
- **RF front-ends** in mobile phones use common-gate LNA (Low Noise Amplifier) stages because of their excellent high-frequency response.
- **Operational amplifier (Op-Amp) input stages** are built using differential MOSFET pairs.
- **Biomedical signal acquisition** (ECG/EEG amplifiers) use MOSFET input stages because of the ultra-high input impedance required to sense tiny biological potentials without loading the source.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Transconductance ($g_m$) of a MOSFET

We start from the DC saturation drain current equation.

**Step 1 — Write the saturation current equation:**
$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of transconductance:**
$$g_m = \left.\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\right|_{Q\text{-point}}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $V_{GS}$ (treat $V_{TH}$ as constant):**
$$g_m = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L} \cdot 2(V_{GS} - V_{TH}) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial V_{GS}}(V_{GS})$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**
$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of overdrive voltage $V_{OV}$:**
Since $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$, we get:
$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L} \cdot V_{OV}$$

**Step 6 — Express in terms of $I_{D,Q}$ by using $V_{OV} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2I_D}{\mu_n C_{ox} W/L}}$:**
$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2I_D}{\mu_n C_{ox} W/L}} = \sqrt{2\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L} I_D}$$

**Step 7 — Alternative compact form using $I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}V_{OV}^2$:**
$$g_m = \frac{2I_{D,Q}}{V_{OV}}$$

This is the most frequently used form in KTU problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Voltage Gain of the Common-Source (CS) Amplifier

Consider a CS amplifier with drain resistor $R_D$, source grounded, and a load resistor $R_L$ connected at the drain through a coupling capacitor.

**Step 1 — Draw the small-signal equivalent circuit:**
- Gate-to-source input: voltage source $v_{gs}$ (since gate current $\approx 0$).
- Drain-to-source output: dependent current source $g_m v_{gs}$ in parallel with $r_o$, in parallel with $R_D$, in parallel with $R_L$.

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the output (drain) node:**
The total current from the dependent source equals the sum of currents through $r_o$, $R_D$, and $R_L$:
$$g_m v_{gs} = \frac{v_{ds}}{r_o} + \frac{v_{ds}}{R_D} + \frac{v_{ds}}{R_L}$$

**Step 3 — Factor $v_{ds}$ on the right-hand side:**
$$g_m v_{gs} = v_{ds}\left(\frac{1}{r_o} + \frac{1}{R_D} + \frac{1}{R_L}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Define the equivalent load resistance:**
$$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{r_o} + \frac{1}{R_D} + \frac{1}{R_L} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad R_{eq} = r_o \parallel R_D \parallel R_L$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $v_{ds}$:**
$$v_{ds} = -g_m v_{gs} \cdot R_{eq}$$

(The negative sign appears because an increase in $v_{gs}$ increases the drain current, which increases the voltage drop across $R_D$, thereby **decreasing** $v_{ds}$.)

**Step 6 — Voltage gain:**
$$A_v = \frac{v_{ds}}{v_{gs}} = -g_m (r_o \parallel R_D \parallel R_L)$$

**Step 7 — Special case (no external load, ideal current source behaviour, $r_o \to \infty$):**
$$A_v = -g_m R_D$$

This is the **inverting amplifier** behaviour — a positive $v_{gs}$ produces a negative $v_{ds}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Source Follower (Common-Drain) Voltage Gain

In a source follower, the output is taken across the source resistor $R_S$.

**Step 1 — KCL at the source node:**
$$g_m v_{gs} = \frac{v_s}{R_S} + \frac{v_s}{r_o}$$

**Step 2 — Note the input loop equation:**
$$v_{gs} = v_{in} - v_s$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the KCL:**
$$g_m (v_{in} - v_s) = v_s\left(\frac{1}{R_S} + \frac{1}{r_o}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange:**
$$g_m v_{in} = v_s\left(g_m + \frac{1}{R_S} + \frac{1}{r_o}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the voltage gain $A_v = v_s / v_{in}$:**
$$A_v = \frac{g_m}{g_m + \frac{1}{R_S} + \frac{1}{r_o}} = \frac{g_m (R_S \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m (R_S \parallel r_o)}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
- $A_v$ is **positive** (non-inverting).
- $A_v < 1$ (always less than unity).
- For $g_m (R_S \parallel r_o) \gg 1$, $A_v \to 1$ — the output **follows** the input (hence the name "source follower").

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A common-source MOSFET amplifier has $I_{D,Q} = 1\ \text{mA}$, $V_{OV} = 0.5\ \text{V}$, $R_D = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$, and $r_o = 20\ \text{k}\Omega$. A load $R_L = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ is AC-coupled at the output. Calculate (a) $g_m$, (b) $R_{in}$, (c) $A_v$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Transconductance:**
$$g_m = \frac{2 I_{D,Q}}{V_{OV}} = \frac{2 \times 1\ \text{mA}}{0.5\ \text{V}} = 4\ \text{mA/V}$$

**(b) Input resistance:**
The gate is insulated by the $\text{SiO}_2$ layer, so:
$$R_{in} \to \infty \quad (\text{practically } \geq 10^{12}\ \Omega)$$

**(c) Voltage gain:**
$$R_{eq} = r_o \parallel R_D \parallel R_L$$
$$R_{eq} = 20\ \text{k}\Omega \parallel 5\ \text{k}\Omega \parallel 10\ \text{k}\Omega$$

First, $20\ \text{k}\Omega \parallel 5\ \text{k}\Omega = \dfrac{20 \times 5}{20 + 5} = 4\ \text{k}\Omega$.

Then, $4\ \text{k}\Omega \parallel 10\ \text{k}\Omega = \dfrac{4 \times 10}{4 + 10} = 2.857\ \text{k}\Omega$.

$$A_v = -g_m \cdot R_{eq} = -(4\ \text{mA/V}) \times (2.857\ \text{k}\Omega) = -11.43\ \text{V/V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Interpretation:** The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output, which is the hallmark of the CS configuration.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: CS MOSFET Amplifier Signal Path

The following Mermaid diagram maps the signal flow through a complete common-source MOSFET amplifier stage, including biasing, small-signal processing, and load coupling.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Bias Supply VDD] --> B[Bias Network at Gate]
    C[AC Input Signal vin] --> D[Input Coupling Capacitor Cin]
    D --> E[Gate Terminal of MOSFET]
    B --> E
    E --> F[MOSFET in Saturation Region]
    F --> G[Drain Terminal]
    G --> H[Output Coupling Capacitor Cout]
    F --> I[Source Terminal Grounded]
    H --> J[External Load RL]
    J --> K[AC Output Signal vout]
    F --> L[Dependent Current Source gm vgs]
    L --> M[Output Resistance ro]
    M --> K
    G --> N[Drain Resistor RD to VDD]
    N --> K
    O[Small Signal vgs] --> P[Linear Gain Stage]
    P --> Q[Amplified Output vout]
    style F fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#BF8F00,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#E2EFDA,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Small-Signal Equivalent Model

The following diagram illustrates how the nonlinear MOSFET is replaced by its small-signal linear model for AC analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input vgs at Gate] --> B[Gate Node Open Circuit R gate to infinity]
    B --> C[Dependent Current Source gm vgs]
    C --> D[Drain Node]
    D --> E[Output Resistance ro to Source]
    D --> F[Drain Resistor RD to VDD]
    D --> G[Load Resistor RL via Coupling Cap]
    H[Source Terminal AC Grounded] --> E
    H --> C
    I[Output Voltage vout taken at Drain] --> D
    J[KCL Equation at Drain Node] --> K[g_m vgs + vds over ro + vds over RD + vds over RL equals 0]
    K --> L[Solve for Av equals negative g_m times Req]
    style C fill:#FFE699,stroke:#BF8F00,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Three Amplifier Topologies Compared

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Common Source CS] --> A1[Vin at Gate]
    A1 --> A2[Vout at Drain]
    A2 --> A3[Inverting Gain Av negative gm RD]

    B[Common Drain CD] --> B1[Vin at Gate]
    B1 --> B2[Vout at Source]
    B2 --> B3[Non inverting Gain Av less than 1]

    C[Common Gate CG] --> C1[Vin at Source]
    C1 --> C2[Vout at Drain]
    C2 --> C3[Non inverting Gain Av gm RD]

    A3 --> D[Application General Purpose Voltage Amplifier]
    B3 --> E[Application Impedance Buffer Source Follower]
    C3 --> F[Application High Frequency Low Noise Amplifier]
    style A fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF
    style B fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366
    style C fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#D79B00
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The yellow-shaded block represents the active dependent current source $g_m v_{gs}$, which is the **core amplifying element** in every MOSFET small-signal model. The green-shaded block represents the final solved voltage-gain expression that students must memorize for KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define the small-signal transconductance $g_m$ of a MOSFET. Derive an expression for $g_m$ in terms of the DC drain current $I_D$ and the overdrive voltage $V_{OV}$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The small-signal transconductance $g_m$ is defined as the rate of change of drain current $i_d$ with respect to the gate-to-source voltage $v_{gs}$, with the drain-to-source voltage held constant at the Q-point:

$$g_m = \left.\frac{\partial i_d}{\partial v_{gs}}\right|_{V_{DS} = \text{constant}}$$

**Derivation (2 marks):**
Starting from the saturation current equation:
$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}V_{OV}^2$$

Differentiating with respect to $V_{OV}$ and using $I_D = \frac{1}{2}k V_{OV}^2$:
$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}V_{OV} = \frac{2I_D}{V_{OV}}$$

where $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ is the overdrive voltage.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Compare the three basic MOSFET amplifier configurations (CS, CD, CG) in terms of voltage gain polarity, input impedance, and a typical application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Configuration | Voltage Gain Polarity | Input Impedance | Typical Application |
|---------------|----------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Common Source (CS)** | Inverting (180° phase shift) | Very High | General-purpose voltage amplifier |
| **Common Drain (CD)** | Non-inverting ($\vert A_v \vert < 1$) | Very High | Impedance buffer / source follower |
| **Common Gate (CG)** | Non-inverting (0° phase shift) | Low | High-frequency RF amplifiers |

(½ mark each for correctly filled cell; ½ mark for naming the right application.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** With the help of a small-signal equivalent circuit, derive the expression for the voltage gain of a **common-source (CS) MOSFET amplifier** with a drain resistor $R_D$ and an output resistance $r_o$ due to channel-length modulation. Discuss the role of the coupling capacitor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CS amplifier uses a MOSFET with $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} = 1\ \text{mA/V}^2$, threshold voltage $V_{TH} = 1\ \text{V}$, $\lambda = 0.02\ \text{V}^{-1}$, and is biased at $I_{D,Q} = 2\ \text{mA}$. The drain resistor is $R_D = 4\ \text{k}\Omega$ and a load $R_L = 4\ \text{k}\Omega$ is AC-coupled at the output. Calculate:
- (i) The transconductance $g_m$ and the output resistance $r_o$.
- (ii) The small-signal voltage gain $A_v$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Equivalent circuit:** Replace the MOSFET by its small-signal model — a voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{gs}$ in parallel with $r_o$, connected between drain and source. The source is grounded, the input $v_{gs}$ is applied between gate and source, and the output is taken at the drain.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — KCL at drain node:**
$$g_m v_{gs} + \frac{v_{ds}}{r_o} + \frac{v_{ds}}{R_D} = 0$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Solve for $v_{ds}$:**
$$v_{ds} = -g_m v_{gs}\left(r_o \parallel R_D\right)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Voltage gain:**
$$A_v = \frac{v_{ds}}{v_{gs}} = -g_m (r_o \parallel R_D)$$

**Step 5 (2 marks) — Role of coupling capacitor:**
- The input coupling capacitor $C_{in}$ blocks the DC bias from reaching the signal source, preventing the source from disturbing the Q-point.
- The output coupling capacitor $C_{out}$ blocks the DC drain voltage from reaching the load $R_L$, ensuring that only the amplified AC signal is delivered to the load.
- At signal frequencies, $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ act as short circuits (impedance $\to 0$).

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Overdrive voltage:**
$$V_{OV} = \sqrt{\frac{2 I_D}{k_n}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2\ \text{mA}}{1\ \text{mA/V}^2}} = \sqrt{4} = 2\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Transconductance:**
$$g_m = k_n \cdot V_{OV} = 1\ \text{mA/V}^2 \times 2\ \text{V} = 2\ \text{mA/V}$$

Alternative verification: $g_m = 2I_D / V_{OV} = 4/2 = 2\ \text{mA/V}$ ✓

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Output resistance:**
$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.02 \times 2\ \text{mA}} = \frac{1}{0.04\ \text{mA}} = 25\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Equivalent load resistance:**
$$R_{eq} = r_o \parallel R_D \parallel R_L = 25\ \text{k}\Omega \parallel 4\ \text{k}\Omega \parallel 4\ \text{k}\Omega$$

First, $4\ \text{k}\Omega \parallel 4\ \text{k}\Omega = 2\ \text{k}\Omega$.

Then, $25\ \text{k}\Omega \parallel 2\ \text{k}\Omega = \dfrac{25 \times 2}{25 + 2} = \dfrac{50}{27} = 1.852\ \text{k}\Omega$

**Step 5 (2 marks) — Voltage gain:**
$$A_v = -g_m \cdot R_{eq} = -(2\ \text{mA/V}) \times (1.852\ \text{k}\Omega) = -3.70\ \text{V/V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** — KTU evaluators specifically test whether students know the CS amplifier is **inverting**. Losing 1 mark for missing the sign is very common.
> 2. **Ignoring $r_o$** — Many students write $A_v = -g_m R_D$ and lose 1–2 marks because the question **explicitly mentions** channel-length modulation.
> 3. **Parallel combination order** — Always combine the two smallest resistances first to minimize arithmetic errors.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Derive the expression for the voltage gain of a **source follower (common-drain) MOSFET amplifier** with source resistance $R_S$ and finite output resistance $r_o$. Comment on why this configuration is used as a **buffer**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with suitable equations how **channel-length modulation** affects the small-signal model of a MOSFET. A MOSFET has $I_{D,Q} = 0.5\ \text{mA}$ and an Early voltage $V_A = 50\ \text{V}$. Find the small-signal output resistance $r_o$. If the drain resistance $R_D = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ is connected, calculate the small-signal voltage gain of the CS amplifier (assume $g_m = 2\ \text{mA/V}$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Small-signal model:** Source follower has the output taken at the source. The drain is at AC ground (DC supply $V_{DD}$). Input $v_{gs}$ is between gate and source; the dependent source $g_m v_{gs}$ flows from drain to source inside the model.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Input loop relation:**
$$v_{gs} = v_{in} - v_{out}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — KCL at source node:**
$$g_m v_{gs} = \frac{v_{out}}{R_S} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_o}$$

Substituting $v_{gs}$:
$$g_m (v_{in} - v_{out}) = v_{out}\left(\frac{1}{R_S} + \frac{1}{r_o}\right)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Solve for $A_v$:**
$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \frac{g_m (R_S \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m (R_S \parallel r_o)}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Why used as a buffer:**
- Input impedance is very high (gate is insulated), so the previous stage is not loaded.
- Output impedance is very low: $R_{out} = (R_S \parallel r_o) \parallel (1/g_m) \approx 1/g_m$, ideal for driving low-impedance loads.
- Voltage gain is approximately unity, so the **signal level is preserved** while impedance is transformed — a perfect buffer.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Effect of channel-length modulation:**
In an ideal long-channel MOSFET, $I_D$ is independent of $V_{DS}$ once the channel pinches off. However, in practice, the effective channel length decreases as $V_{DS}$ increases, causing $I_D$ to increase linearly with $V_{DS}$. The modified equation is:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$

In the small-signal model, this introduces a finite output resistance $r_o$ in parallel with the dependent current source, representing the dependence of $I_D$ on $V_{DS}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Output resistance from Early voltage:**
$$r_o = \frac{V_A}{I_{D,Q}} = \frac{50\ \text{V}}{0.5\ \text{mA}} = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Equivalent load:**
$$R_{eq} = r_o \parallel R_D = \frac{100 \times 10}{100 + 10}\ \text{k}\Omega = \frac{1000}{110}\ \text{k}\Omega = 9.09\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Voltage gain:**
$$A_v = -g_m \cdot R_{eq} = -(2\ \text{mA/V}) \times (9.09\ \text{k}\Omega) = -18.18\ \text{V/V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention confusion** — A common error is reporting a positive gain for the CS configuration. Always remember: CS is **inverting**.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in $g_m \cdot R_D$ calculation** — Use $g_m$ in **mA/V** and $R_D$ in **k$\Omega$** to get a unitless gain directly.
> 3. **Early voltage vs. $\lambda$** — Students often mix $V_A$ and $\lambda$. Recall: $\lambda = 1/V_A$. The formula $r_o = V_A / I_D$ is most reliable for KTU problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MOSFET amplifier operation requires saturation region biasing**: $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TH}$.
- **The MOSFET is a VCCS (Voltage-Controlled Current Source)** in the small-signal model, with transconductance $g_m$ and output resistance $r_o$ in parallel.
- **Transconductance $g_m = 2I_{D,Q} / V_{OV}$** is the most-used formula; always evaluate it at the Q-point.
- **Output resistance $r_o = V_A / I_{D,Q} = 1/(\lambda I_{D,Q})$** captures channel-length modulation.
- **CS amplifier is the most common** — inverting gain $A_v = -g_m(r_o \parallel R_D \parallel R_L)$, very high $R_{in}$.
- **Source follower (CD) has $A_v \approx 1$** — used as an impedance buffer.
- **Common-gate has low $R_{in}$** — used in high-frequency RF applications.
- **Coupling capacitors** block DC and pass AC — they isolate Q-point from external circuitry.
- **Phase inversion** is a defining feature of the CS configuration — KTU tests this frequently.
- **Gate current $\approx 0$** — always remember the insulated gate implies $R_{in} \to \infty$.
- **Always combine parallel resistors** when computing equivalent load $R_{eq}$.
- **Memorize the three standard topologies** with their gain expressions — this is a guaranteed KTU question.
- **Sign of $A_v$ matters** — invert for CS, positive for CD and CG.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biasing of Discrete MOSFET Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Biasing of Discrete MOSFET Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCPT304)**, **Biasing of a Discrete MOSFET Amplifier** refers to the deliberate application of external DC voltages and currents to a Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET) so that it operates at a predefined, stable, and reproducible **Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point)** — denoted by the DC coordinates $(V_{DS,Q}, I_{D,Q})$ in the Output Characteristics plane, and $(V_{GS,Q})$ in the Transfer Characteristics plane. The Q-point must lie firmly inside the **Saturation (Active/Constant-Current) Region** of the MOSFET so that the device behaves as a linear voltage-controlled current source when small AC signals are superimposed on the DC bias.

In one sentence (board-exam style):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"MOSFET biasing is the process of establishing a fixed DC drain current $I_{D,Q}$ and DC drain-to-source voltage $V_{DS,Q}$ using external resistors, independent of device parameter variations, so that the device operates in the saturation region for faithful linear amplification."**

---

## 1.2 Why is Biasing Required? — The Conceptual Foundation

A MOSFET, when used as an amplifier, is a **normally-off** (Enhancement-type) or **normally-on** (Depletion-type) voltage-controlled device. Its drain current $I_D$ is a strong (square-law) function of the gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$. Because:

1. The threshold voltage $V_{th}$ varies from device to device (±20–30% for the same part number).
2. The transconductance parameter $K = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\tfrac{W}{L}$ depends on process, temperature, and ageing.
3. The device must remain in the **Saturation Region** during the full AC swing to avoid distortion.

…it is mathematically impossible to leave all terminals unconnected. The designer must *inject* controlled DC voltages using resistor networks, and the *arrangement* of those resistors is what is called a **biasing configuration**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Knee-Jerk KTU fact:** For an n-channel Enhancement MOSFET (nEMOS) amplifier, the **required condition is $V_{GS,Q} > V_{th}$** and $V_{DS,Q} > V_{GS,Q} - V_{th}$. Violation of either condition pushes the device out of the active region, producing clipping or no amplification.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water-tap (faucet) system**:

- The **gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$** is like the *amount you turn the tap handle*.
- The **drain current $I_D$** is the *flow of water* out of the tap.
- The **threshold voltage $V_{th}$** is the *stiffness* of the handle — you must turn it beyond a certain point before any water flows.
- **Biasing** is the act of *fixing the tap at some partially-open position* (the Q-point) before you start adding a small oscillating signal (someone jiggling the handle gently to vary the flow).

If the tap is **fully closed** ($V_{GS} < V_{th}$), no current flows, and the device is **cut off** — useless for amplification.
If the tap is **fully open**, the water rushes out, the device is in the **triode/ohmic region** — also useless for linear amplification.
The *sweet spot* — where the tap is partially open and small wiggles produce clean proportional flow changes — is the **Saturation Region**, and *fixing the tap there* is exactly what biasing accomplishes.

---

## 1.4 The Four Canonical Discrete MOSFET Biasing Circuits

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly demands knowledge of the following four discrete (non-integrated) biasing arrangements:

| Sl. | Biasing Configuration | KTU-Mandated Schematic Name | Typical Application |
|-----|------------------------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Fixed Bias** | Constant-$V_{GS}$ bias | Depletion-MOSFET only |
| 2 | **Self-Bias (Source Bias)** | Single-supply source resistor bias | General-purpose, JFET/Depletion |
| 3 | **Voltage-Divider Bias** | Resistor-divider + source resistor | **Most commonly asked** in KTU ESE |
| 4 | **Drain-Feedback Bias** | Gate tied to drain through $R_F$ | Enhancement MOSFET |

Each one is analysed in Section 2 and Section 3 using **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** and the **Square-Law equation** of the MOSFET.

---

## 1.5 What is a "Q-Point" — Formal Definition for the Board

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point)** is the pair $(I_{D,Q}, V_{DS,Q})$ that the device settles to when **only DC sources are connected** and the input AC signal is identically zero. It is called *quiescent* (Latin: *quietus*, "at rest") because no time-varying signal is being processed.

The Q-point must be located **midway between cutoff ($I_D = 0$) and the edge of saturation** on the DC load line so that the **maximum symmetric AC swing** can be obtained around it without clipping — this is the design goal every biasing scheme tries to achieve.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Square-Law Equation — The Cornerstone of MOSFET Bias Analysis

For an **n-channel Enhancement MOSFET** in the **Saturation Region**, KTU 2024 expects you to recall and apply the **square-law (Shockley-type) drain current equation**:

$$
I_D \;=\; K \left( V_{GS} - V_{th} \right)^2
$$

where the **conduction parameter** $K$ is:

$$
K \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mu_n\,C_{ox}\,\tfrac{W}{L}
$$

with $\mu_n$ being the electron mobility in the channel, $C_{ox}$ the oxide capacitance per unit area, and $W/L$ the aspect ratio of the device.

For a **Depletion-type MOSFET** operated in the *enhancement-mode region* ($V_{GS} > 0$), the same Shockley form is used but with the **pinch-off voltage** $V_P$ replacing the threshold concept, and the **maximum drain current** $I_{DSS}$ at $V_{GS} = 0$:

$$
I_D \;=\; I_{DSS} \left( 1 - \tfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P} \right)^2
$$

---

## 2.2 The Four Biasing Topologies — Operating Logic

### 2.2.1 Fixed Bias (Gate-Bias Configuration)

- The gate is tied to a fixed DC voltage $V_{GG}$ (often derived from a separate bias supply or a resistive divider that is *load-independent*).
- Source is grounded: $V_S = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; V_{GS} = V_{GG}$.
- This makes $V_{GS,Q}$ **independent** of $I_D$.

**Why this is rarely used for Enhancement MOSFETs:**
Because $V_{GS,Q} = V_{GG}$ is fixed by the divider, and the square law demands $V_{GS,Q} > V_{th}$, any small variation in $V_{th}$ (process spread) will swing the device either into cutoff or deep saturation, making the Q-point highly **unstable**. The **Stability Factor** $S = \frac{\partial I_{D,Q}}{\partial V_{th}}$ is essentially *infinite* for a fixed-bias E-MOSFET, which is unacceptable.

### 2.2.2 Self-Bias / Source Bias

- A single resistor $R_S$ is placed in series with the source.
- Gate is grounded through $R_G$ (which carries **no DC current** because $I_G = 0$ in a MOSFET).
- $V_{GG} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$.

**Why this works for Depletion MOSFETs:**
Because the device conducts at $V_{GS} = 0$, the moment power is applied, current $I_D$ begins to flow, voltage drop across $R_S$ appears, making $V_{GS}$ negative, which automatically *self-regulates* the current to a stable Q-point.

### 2.2.3 Voltage-Divider Bias (The KTU Favourite)

- A pair of resistors $R_1$ (top) and $R_2$ (bottom) form a divider from $V_{DD}$ to ground.
- The midpoint sets the gate voltage: $V_G = V_{DD}\,\dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$.
- A source resistor $R_S$ is present, so $V_{GS} = V_G - I_D R_S$.

**Why it is most stable:**
The gate voltage $V_G$ is *insensitive* to $I_D$ variations (because $I_G = 0$ means no loading of the divider), and the negative feedback through $R_S$ *automatically corrects* any drift in $I_D$:

- If $I_D$ tends to increase $\Rightarrow$ $V_S$ rises $\Rightarrow$ $V_{GS} = V_G - V_S$ drops $\Rightarrow$ $I_D$ reduces (corrects the drift).
- This is the classic **negative-feedback** mechanism that yields a small **Stability Factor** $S = \dfrac{\partial I_{D,Q}}{\partial I_{D0}}$ and $\dfrac{\partial I_{D,Q}}{\partial V_{th}}$.

### 2.2.4 Drain-Feedback Bias

- A feedback resistor $R_F$ connects the **drain to the gate**.
- Because $I_G = 0$, there is **no DC current through $R_F$** $\Rightarrow$ $V_G = V_D$.
- KVL on the output loop: $V_{DD} = I_D R_D + V_{DS}$ and on the gate: $V_{GS} = V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$.

This is a **sneaky, elegant** scheme for Enhancement MOSFETs because it produces a *negative feedback* loop (more $I_D$ → more drop across $R_D$ → lower $V_{GS}$ → less $I_D$), giving a stable Q-point **without a separate bias supply**.

---

## 2.3 DC Load Line — The Geometric Backbone

The **DC load line** is the locus of all possible $(V_{DS}, I_D)$ pairs that satisfy KVL on the output loop, drawn on the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ plane:

$$
V_{DS} \;=\; V_{DD} - I_D \, R_D
$$

The two **endpoints** are:

- **Cutoff point:** $(V_{DS} = V_{DD},\; I_D = 0)$
- **Saturation-edge point:** $\left(V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_{D,sat}\,R_D,\; I_D = I_{D,sat}\right)$ where $I_{D,sat} = K(V_{GS,Q} - V_{th})^2$ is the saturation current at the Q-point $V_{GS}$.

The Q-point lies on this load line, and is found by **intersecting the load line with the $I_D = K(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ parabola** for the specific $V_{GS}$ set by the bias network.

---

## 2.4 Stability Factors — The Quality Metric of a Bias Design

KTU examiners often ask: *"Which biasing scheme is the most stable?"* The answer is quantitatively obtained using three partial-derivative stability factors:

$$
S_{I_{D0}} \;=\; \frac{\partial I_{D,Q}}{\partial I_{D0}} \quad \quad
S_{V_{th}} \;=\; \frac{\partial I_{D,Q}}{\partial V_{th}} \quad \quad
S_V \;=\; \frac{\partial I_{D,Q}}{\partial V_{DD}}
$$

- **Self-bias** for a Depletion MOSFET: $S = \dfrac{1}{1 + K' R_S}$ (a finite, less-than-unity number → good stability).
- **Voltage-divider bias** for an Enhancement MOSFET: $S_{V_{th}} = \dfrac{2 K (V_{GS,Q} - V_{th})}{1 + 2 K R_S (V_{GS,Q} - V_{th})}$ (small → good stability).

Lower the magnitude of the stability factor, *more stable* the Q-point is. A **perfectly stable design** would have $S = 0$.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorise the boxed equations. KTU values one of these per semester. All $\vert \cdot \vert$ symbols below are written using `\vert` to preserve table formatting.

| Sl. | Quantity | Symbolic Expression | Units / Notes |
|-----|-----------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Square-Law Drain Current | $I_D = K (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ | Amperes, valid in saturation |
| 2 | Conduction Parameter | $K = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mu_n C_{ox}\,W/L$ | A/V² |
| 3 | Depletion-MOSFET $I_D$ | $I_D = I_{DSS} (1 - V_{GS}/V_P)^2$ | Amperes |
| 4 | DC Load Line | $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$ | Volts |
| 5 | Source Resistor Drop | $V_S = I_D R_S$ | Volts |
| 6 | Self-Bias Gate-Source Voltage | $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$ | Volts (n-channel Depletion) |
| 7 | Voltage-Divider Gate Voltage | $V_G = V_{DD}\,\dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Volts |
| 8 | Voltage-Divider $V_{GS}$ | $V_{GS} = V_G - I_D R_S$ | Volts |
| 9 | Drain-Feedback $V_{GS}$ | $V_{GS} = V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$ | Volts |
| 10 | Saturation-Region Condition | $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | Inequality |
| 11 | Conduction-Region Condition | $V_{GS} \geq V_{th}$ | Inequality (Enhancement) |
| 12 | Stability Factor (Self-Bias) | $S = \dfrac{1}{1 + K R_S}$ | Dimensionless |
| 13 | Stability Factor (V-Divider) | $S_{V_{th}} = \dfrac{2K(V_{GS,Q}-V_{th})}{1 + 2KR_S(V_{GS,Q}-V_{th})}$ | Dimensionless |
| 14 | Drain Dissipation Limit | $P_D = V_{DS}\,I_D \leq P_{D,\max}$ | Watts |
| 15 | Midpoint Q-Point Rule | $V_{DS,Q} \approx \tfrac{1}{2} V_{DD}$ | Maximum-swing design |

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility — Where This Topic Lives in the Real World

- **Audio pre-amplifiers** (e.g., microphone pre-amps) use the **voltage-divider bias** for low-noise, thermally-stable operation.
- **RF front-end LNAs** in mobile phones use **drain-feedback bias** for compact, single-supply operation.
- **Power MOSFETs in SMPS** use **constant-current (active) biasing** — an advanced version of the discrete schemes listed here.
- **Cascade amplifiers in instrumentation** often require the Q-point of every stage to be temperature-compensated — and the *stability factor* you calculate in this module is precisely the parameter that determines whether your design will drift under heat.

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section is the **analytical heart** of the topic. Each biasing scheme is derived in full, with every algebraic transition written explicitly. No step is skipped, no equation is left unjustified.

---

## 3.1 Common Framework — The Three KVL Loops

For **every** MOSFET biasing circuit, you must write **two KVL equations** (one for the input/gate loop, one for the output/drain loop) and **one device equation** (the square law). This gives a system of three equations in three unknowns: $I_{D,Q}, V_{GS,Q}, V_{DS,Q}$.

> [!NOTE]
> The standard analytical recipe is:
> **Step A:** Write KVL on the *gate* loop → obtain $V_{GS}$ in terms of $I_D$.
> **Step B:** Write KVL on the *drain* loop → obtain $V_{DS}$ in terms of $I_D$.
> **Step C:** Use the square law $I_D = K(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ to solve for $I_{D,Q}$ (this is generally a **quadratic** in $I_D$).
> **Step D:** Back-substitute to find $V_{GS,Q}$ and $V_{DS,Q}$.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Self-Bias (Source Bias) Configuration

### Circuit Assumptions
- n-channel Depletion MOSFET.
- $R_G$ connects gate to ground (carries zero DC).
- $R_S$ in series with source.
- $R_D$ in series with drain, between drain and $V_{DD}$.

### Step A — Gate Loop KVL
The gate is connected to ground only through $R_G$, with no current:

$$
I_G = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_G = 0
$$

Applying KVL from gate to source through the device:

$$
V_{GS} \;=\; V_G - V_S \;=\; 0 - I_D R_S \;=\; - I_D R_S
$$

### Step B — Drain Loop KVL
Walking from $V_{DD}$ down to ground through $R_D$, the device, and $R_S$:

$$
V_{DD} \;=\; I_D R_D + V_{DS} + I_D R_S
$$

Rearranged:

$$
V_{DS} \;=\; V_{DD} - I_D (R_D + R_S)
$$

### Step C — Square-Law Equation (Depletion Form)
For a Depletion MOSFET:

$$
I_D \;=\; I_{DSS} \left( 1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P} \right)^2
$$

Substituting $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$:

$$
I_D \;=\; I_{DSS} \left( 1 - \frac{-I_D R_S}{V_P} \right)^2 \;=\; I_{DSS} \left( 1 + \frac{I_D R_S}{V_P} \right)^2
$$

### Step D — Quadratic in $I_D$
Take the square root of both sides (note: $I_D \geq 0$ and $V_P < 0$ for n-channel Depletion, so the sign is preserved):

$$
\sqrt{I_D} \;=\; \sqrt{I_{DSS}} \left( 1 + \frac{I_D R_S}{V_P} \right)
$$

Let $x = \sqrt{I_D}$ and rearrange to a standard quadratic in $x$:

$$
x^2 - \frac{V_P}{R_S \sqrt{I_{DSS}}}\,x + \frac{V_P^2}{R_S^2} \;=\; 0
$$

The **two solutions** are:

$$
x \;=\; \frac{V_P}{2 R_S \sqrt{I_{DSS}}} \,\left[\, 1 \pm \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4 I_{DSS} R_S^2}{V_P^2}} \,\right]
$$

The **physically valid** root is the one that gives $0 \leq I_D \leq I_{DSS}$:

$$
\boxed{\, I_{D,Q} \;=\; \frac{V_P^2}{4 R_S^2 I_{DSS}} \left[\, 1 \pm \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4 I_{DSS} R_S^2}{V_P^2}} \,\right]^2 \,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "$+$" sign gives the *correct* operating point in the **saturation region**. The "$-$" sign gives a point in the **cutoff** or **non-physical** region — discard it.

### Step E — Final Q-Point Coordinates

$$
V_{GS,Q} = -I_{D,Q}\,R_S
\quad\quad
V_{DS,Q} = V_{DD} - I_{D,Q}\,(R_D + R_S)
$$

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Voltage-Divider Bias (Enhancement MOSFET)

### Circuit Assumptions
- n-channel Enhancement MOSFET.
- $R_1$ from $V_{DD}$ to gate, $R_2$ from gate to ground.
- $R_S$ in source, $R_D$ in drain.

### Step A — Gate Voltage (Divider)
Because $I_G = 0$, no current flows through $R_1$ or $R_2$, so the divider is **unloaded**:

$$
V_G \;=\; V_{DD}\,\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

### Step B — Gate-Source Voltage
KVL gate-to-source:

$$
V_{GS} \;=\; V_G - V_S \;=\; V_{DD}\,\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - I_D R_S
$$

### Step C — Drain-Source Voltage (Drain Loop KVL)

$$
V_{DD} \;=\; I_D R_D + V_{DS} + I_D R_S
\;\;\Rightarrow\;\;
V_{DS} \;=\; V_{DD} - I_D (R_D + R_S)
$$

### Step D — Square-Law Equation (Enhancement Form)
For Enhancement MOSFET:

$$
I_D \;=\; K (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2
$$

Substitute $V_{GS}$:

$$
I_D \;=\; K \left( V_{DD}\,\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - I_D R_S - V_{th} \right)^2
$$

### Step E — Quadratic in $I_D$
Let:

$$
A \;=\; K R_S^2
\quad\quad
B \;=\; - 2 K R_S \left( V_{DD}\,\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - V_{th} \right)
\quad\quad
C \;=\; K \left( V_{DD}\,\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - V_{th} \right)^2
$$

Then the equation $I_D = A I_D^2 + B I_D + C$ becomes:

$$
\boxed{\, A\,I_D^2 + (B - 1)\,I_D + C \;=\; 0 \,}
$$

Solving via the quadratic formula:

$$
\boxed{\, I_{D,Q} \;=\; \frac{1 - B \pm \sqrt{(1 - B)^2 - 4 A C}}{2 A} \,}
$$

Again, retain only the root that yields $V_{GS,Q} > V_{th}$ and $V_{DS,Q} > V_{GS,Q} - V_{th}$.

### Step F — Numerical Worked Example (Board-Style)

Given: $V_{DD} = 12\,\text{V}$, $R_1 = 1.5\,\text{M}\Omega$, $R_2 = 1\,\text{M}\Omega$, $R_S = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_D = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $K = 0.5\,\text{mA/V}^2$, $V_{th} = 2\,\text{V}$.

**Compute $V_G$:**

$$
V_G \;=\; 12 \times \frac{1}{1.5 + 1} \;=\; 12 \times 0.4 \;=\; 4.8\;\text{V}
$$

**Assume initially $I_D = 0$ to estimate $V_{GS}$:**

$$
V_{GS,0} \;=\; V_G - 0 = 4.8\;\text{V}
$$

**First-iteration drain current:**

$$
I_{D,1} \;=\; 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times (4.8 - 2)^2 \;=\; 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.84 \;=\; 3.92\;\text{mA}
$$

**Iterate: update $V_{GS}$:**

$$
V_{GS,1} \;=\; 4.8 - 3.92 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{3} \;=\; 4.8 - 7.84 \;=\; -3.04\;\text{V}
$$

**Note:** $V_{GS,1} < V_{th}$ — the device is in cutoff. The numerical iteration did not converge to a valid Q-point because the *initial estimate* of $I_D = 0$ was poor. In such cases, the **direct quadratic solution** in Step E must be used.

**Use the quadratic formula directly:**

$$
V_{GS} - V_{th} = 4.8 - I_D R_S - 2 = 2.8 - 2 I_D \quad (\text{with } I_D \text{ in mA})
$$

$$
I_D = 0.5 (2.8 - 2 I_D)^2
$$

$$
I_D = 0.5 (7.84 - 11.2 I_D + 4 I_D^2) = 3.92 - 5.6 I_D + 2 I_D^2
$$

$$
2 I_D^2 - 6.6 I_D + 3.92 = 0
$$

$$
I_D = \frac{6.6 \pm \sqrt{43.56 - 31.36}}{4} = \frac{6.6 \pm \sqrt{12.2}}{4} = \frac{6.6 \pm 3.49}{4}
$$

$$
I_{D,1} = \frac{6.6 - 3.49}{4} = 0.78\;\text{mA}
\quad\quad
I_{D,2} = \frac{6.6 + 3.49}{4} = 2.52\;\text{mA}
$$

**Check $V_{GS}$ for each:**

- For $I_D = 0.78\,\text{mA}$: $V_{GS} = 4.8 - 1.56 = 3.24\,\text{V} > V_{th}$ ✓
- For $I_D = 2.52\,\text{mA}$: $V_{GS} = 4.8 - 5.04 = -0.24\,\text{V} < V_{th}$ ✗ (rejected)

**Therefore, the valid Q-point is:**

$$
\boxed{\, I_{D,Q} = 0.78\;\text{mA}, \quad V_{GS,Q} = 3.24\;\text{V} \,}
$$

**Drain-source voltage:**

$$
V_{DS,Q} = 12 - 0.78 \times (4.7 + 2) = 12 - 5.226 = 6.77\;\text{V}
$$

**Saturation check:**

$$
V_{GS,Q} - V_{th} = 1.24\;\text{V}, \quad V_{DS,Q} = 6.77\;\text{V} \;\Rightarrow\; V_{DS,Q} > V_{GS,Q} - V_{th} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Midpoint check:** $\tfrac{V_{DD}}{2} = 6\,\text{V}$ vs $V_{DS,Q} = 6.77\,\text{V}$ — Q-point is *close to the midpoint*, indicating a well-designed bias for maximum symmetric swing.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation — Drain-Feedback Bias

### Circuit Assumptions
- n-channel Enhancement MOSFET.
- $R_F$ from drain to gate (large, e.g., 1–10 M$\Omega$).
- $R_D$ in drain, source grounded.

### Step A — Gate-Drain Coupling
Because $I_G = 0$, no current flows through $R_F$:

$$
V_G \;=\; V_D
$$

### Step B — Gate-Source Voltage
Source is grounded ($V_S = 0$), so:

$$
V_{GS} \;=\; V_G - V_S \;=\; V_D
$$

### Step C — Drain Loop KVL

$$
V_{DD} \;=\; I_D R_D + V_{DS} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V_{DS} \;=\; V_{DD} - I_D R_D
$$

### Step D — Combine

$$
V_{GS} \;=\; V_{DS} \;=\; V_{DD} - I_D R_D
$$

### Step E — Square-Law Substitution

$$
I_D \;=\; K (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 \;=\; K (V_{DD} - I_D R_D - V_{th})^2
$$

This is again a quadratic in $I_D$ of the form:

$$
K R_D^2 I_D^2 - \left[ 1 + 2 K R_D (V_{DD} - V_{th}) \right] I_D + K (V_{DD} - V_{th})^2 \;=\; 0
$$

Apply the quadratic formula and select the root that satisfies $V_{GS,Q} > V_{th}$.

> [!NOTE]
> The negative-feedback nature is now explicit: a **larger $I_D$** produces a **larger drop across $R_D$**, which **reduces $V_{DS}$**, which **reduces $V_{GS}$** (since $V_{GS} = V_{DS}$), which **reduces $I_D$** — a self-correcting loop.

---

## 3.5 Stability-Factor Derivation (Self-Bias, Depletion)

Starting from the self-bias equations:

$$
V_{GS} = -I_D R_S
\quad\quad
I_D = I_{DSS} \left( 1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P} \right)^2
$$

Implicitly differentiate $I_D$ with respect to $V_{th}$ (or in the BJT analog, $I_{D0}$). The **stability factor** for self-bias is derived as:

$$
S \;=\; \frac{\partial I_{D,Q}}{\partial I_{D0}} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + K R_S}
$$

where $K$ is the conduction parameter evaluated at the Q-point. The **larger $R_S$ is**, the **smaller $S$ becomes**, and the **more stable** the design — but at the cost of a *smaller $V_{DS,Q}$* (smaller headroom for the AC swing).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix of the Four Biasing Schemes

| Property | Fixed Bias | Self-Bias | Voltage-Divider Bias | Drain-Feedback |
|----------|------------|-----------|----------------------|-----------------|
| Independent supply? | Yes (sometimes) | No | No | No |
| Works for Enhancement MOSFET? | Difficult | No (yes for D-MOSFET) | **Yes — preferred** | Yes |
| Q-point stability | Poor | Good | **Excellent** | Good |
| Negative feedback present? | No | Yes (through $R_S$) | Yes (through $R_S$) | Yes (through $R_F$) |
| Typical Stability Factor $\vert S \vert$ | $\infty$ | $\tfrac{1}{1+KR_S}$ | $\ll 1$ | $\approx \tfrac{1}{1+KR_D}$ |
| Number of resistors | 2 | 3 | 4 | 2 |
| Power consumption | Lowest | Low | Moderate | Moderate |
| **KTU 2024 Exam Weightage** | Low | Medium | **Highest** | Medium |
