
## 4.2 Flowchart — Algorithm to Solve a MOSFET Biasing Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Given circuit parameters] --> A1[Identify MOSFET type: Enhancement or Depletion]
    A1 --> A2[Write KVL on gate loop]
    A2 --> A3[Write KVL on drain loop]
    A3 --> A4[Express VGS in terms of ID]
    A4 --> A5[Substitute into square law: ID = K VGS - Vth squared]
    A5 --> A6[Formulate quadratic in ID]
    A6 --> A7[Solve quadratic: two roots]
    A7 --> A8[Check physical validity: VGS greater than Vth]
    A8 --> A9{Valid root?}
    A9 -- Yes --> A10[Compute VDS,Q and stability factor]
    A9 -- No --> A11[Discard and use alternate root]
    A11 --> A10
    A10 --> A12[Verify saturation: VDS greater than VGS minus Vth]
    A12 --> A13[Report Q-Point]
```

## 4.3 Functional Comparison — Voltage-Divider vs Drain-Feedback Bias

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VDB[Voltage Divider Bias]
        V1[VDD] --> V2[R1]
        V2 --> V3[R2]
        V3 --> V4[GND]
        V1 --> V5[RD]
        V5 --> V6[Drain]
        V6 --> V7[MOSFET]
        V7 --> V8[RS]
        V8 --> V9[GND]
        V3 -.-> V10[Gate connection]
    end

    subgraph DFB[Drain Feedback Bias]
        D1[VDD] --> D2[RD]
        D2 --> D3[Drain]
        D3 --> D4[RF]
        D4 --> D5[Gate]
        D3 --> D6[MOSFET]
        D6 --> D7[Source]
        D7 --> D8[GND]
    end
```

## 4.4 Q-Point Movement Under Device Parameter Drift

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Nominal Vth and K] --> B[Q-Point: IDQ and VDSQ]
    C[Vth increases by 20 percent] --> D[New Q-Point: lower IDQ]
    E[Vth decreases by 20 percent] --> F[New Q-Point: higher IDQ]
    B --> G{Stability factor magnitude}
    D --> G
    F --> G
    G -- Small SF --> H[Q-Point drift is small: stable]
    G -- Large SF --> I[Q-Point drift is large: unstable]
```

## 4.5 Saturation-Region Operating Window (Visualisation Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Operating Conditions] --> B1{VGS greater than Vth?}
    B1 -- No --> C1[CUTOFF REGION: ID = 0]
    B1 -- Yes --> B2{VDS greater than VGS minus Vth?}
    B2 -- No --> C2[TRIODE/OHMIC REGION: poor amplifier]
    B2 -- Yes --> C3[SATURATION REGION: valid Q-Point]
    C3 --> D1[Design: place Q-Point near midpoint of load line]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1  [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Scheme]

> *"Define the term **'Q-point'** of a MOSFET amplifier. List any **two** essential conditions that the Q-point must satisfy to ensure linear amplification in the saturation region."*

#### Model Answer (Board-Exam Quality)

**Definition (2 marks):** The Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point) is the pair of DC coordinates $(I_{D,Q}, V_{DS,Q})$ at which the MOSFET settles when only DC supplies are present and the input AC signal is identically zero. The Q-point fixes the *bias* of the device around which the small-signal AC swing takes place.

**Conditions (1 mark each):**
1. **Conduction condition:** $V_{GS,Q} > V_{th}$ — the device must be ON and conducting a finite drain current. For Depletion MOSFET, $V_{GS,Q} > V_P$.
2. **Saturation condition:** $V_{DS,Q} \geq V_{GS,Q} - V_{th}$ — the device must be in the constant-current (active/saturation) region for linear transconductance operation. Otherwise it enters the triode region and produces non-linear, clipped output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is asked so often:** KTU examiners use this to test the student's grasp of the *why* behind biasing — not just memorised circuit names.

---

### Question A.2  [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]

> *"Differentiate between the **'fixed bias'** and **'self-bias'** configurations of a Depletion-MOSFET amplifier. State **one** drawback of the fixed-bias scheme."*

#### Model Answer

| Feature | Fixed Bias | Self-Bias |
|---------|------------|-----------|
| Gate voltage source | Independent supply $V_{GG}$ or stiff divider | Grounded through $R_G$ |
| $V_{GS}$ expression | $V_{GS} = V_{GG}$ (independent of $I_D$) | $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$ (current-dependent) |
| Negative feedback | **Absent** | Present (via $R_S$) |
| Q-point stability | Poor (no self-correction) | Good (auto-regulates) |
| Number of resistors | 2 | 3 |
| Suitable device | Depletion MOSFET only | Depletion MOSFET, JFET |

**Drawback of fixed bias (1 mark):**
The Q-point is *highly sensitive* to variations in device parameters ($V_{th}, I_{DSS}, K$). Even a small drift in $V_{th}$ can move the device from saturation to cutoff, making the amplifier unusable. The stability factor $S \to \infty$ for this scheme.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B questions have a **mandatory internal choice** between two 14-mark questions. Below, both options are provided with full sub-parts and worked solutions.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)  [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

> **Part (a) — 7 marks:** *For the **voltage-divider bias** circuit of an n-channel Enhancement MOSFET shown below, derive the expression for the drain current $I_{D,Q}$ in terms of $K$, $V_{th}$, $V_{DD}$, $R_1$, $R_2$, and $R_S$.*
>
> **Circuit:** $V_{DD} = 15\,\text{V}$, $R_1 = 2\,\text{M}\Omega$, $R_2 = 1.2\,\text{M}\Omega$, $R_S = 1.5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_D = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $K = 0.4\,\text{mA/V}^2$, $V_{th} = 2\,\text{V}$.
>
> **Part (b) — 7 marks:** *Calculate the Q-point $(I_{D,Q}, V_{DS,Q})$ and verify that the device is in saturation.*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Gate voltage (1 mark):**
Since $I_G = 0$ in a MOSFET, the divider is unloaded:

$$
V_G \;=\; V_{DD}\,\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \;=\; 15 \times \frac{1.2}{2 + 1.2} \;=\; 15 \times 0.375 \;=\; 5.625\;\text{V}
$$

**Step 2 — $V_{GS}$ in terms of $I_D$ (1 mark):**
With source resistor $R_S$:

$$
V_{GS} \;=\; V_G - I_D R_S \;=\; 5.625 - 1.5 I_D \quad \text{with } I_D \text{ in mA, } R_S \text{ in k}\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Square-law substitution (2 marks):**

$$
I_D \;=\; K\,(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 \;=\; 0.4 \left( 5.625 - 1.5 I_D - 2 \right)^2 \;=\; 0.4 \left( 3.625 - 1.5 I_D \right)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Quadratic formulation (2 marks):**

$$
I_D \;=\; 0.4 \left( 13.14 - 10.875 I_D + 2.25 I_D^2 \right)
$$

$$
I_D \;=\; 5.256 - 4.35 I_D + 0.9 I_D^2
$$

$$
0.9 I_D^2 - 5.35 I_D + 5.256 \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 5 — Final symbolic expression (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{\, I_{D,Q} \;=\; \frac{5.35 \pm \sqrt{5.35^2 - 4 \times 0.9 \times 5.256}}{2 \times 0.9} \;=\; \frac{5.35 \pm \sqrt{28.62 - 18.92}}{1.8} \;=\; \frac{5.35 \pm 3.113}{1.8} \,}
$$

This yields two roots: $I_D = 4.7\,\text{mA}$ and $I_D = 1.24\,\text{mA}$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Select the valid root (2 marks):**

For $I_D = 4.7\,\text{mA}$: $V_{GS} = 5.625 - 1.5 \times 4.7 = 5.625 - 7.05 = -1.425\,\text{V}$. Since $-1.425 < V_{th} = 2\,\text{V}$, this root is **physically invalid** (device would be in cutoff). **Rejected.**

For $I_D = 1.24\,\text{mA}$: $V_{GS} = 5.625 - 1.5 \times 1.24 = 5.625 - 1.86 = 3.765\,\text{V}$. Since $3.765 > V_{th} = 2\,\text{V}$, this root is **valid**. **Accepted.**

**Step 2 — Compute $V_{DS,Q}$ (2 marks):**

$$
V_{DS,Q} \;=\; V_{DD} - I_{D,Q}\,(R_D + R_S) \;=\; 15 - 1.24 \times (4.7 + 1.5) \;=\; 15 - 1.24 \times 6.2
$$

$$
V_{DS,Q} \;=\; 15 - 7.688 \;=\; 7.312\;\text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Saturation verification (2 marks):**

Compute $V_{GS,Q} - V_{th}$:

$$
V_{GS,Q} - V_{th} \;=\; 3.765 - 2 \;=\; 1.765\;\text{V}
$$

Compare with $V_{DS,Q}$:

$$
V_{DS,Q} \;=\; 7.312\;\text{V} \;\;>\;\; 1.765\;\text{V} \;=\; V_{GS,Q} - V_{th} \quad \checkmark
$$

The device is in the **Saturation Region**. The Q-point is:

$$
\boxed{\, I_{D,Q} = 1.24\;\text{mA}, \quad V_{DS,Q} = 7.31\;\text{V} \,}
$$

**Step 4 — Design comment (1 mark):**
The midpoint of $V_{DD}$ is $7.5\,\text{V}$, and $V_{DS,Q} = 7.31\,\text{V}$ is *very close* to the midpoint, indicating a near-ideal bias design for maximum symmetric AC swing.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)  [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern — Internal Choice]

> **Part (a) — 7 marks:** *Explain the **self-bias** configuration of a Depletion-MOSFET amplifier. Derive the expression for the stability factor $S = \partial I_{D,Q}/\partial I_{D0}$ and show that the use of source resistor $R_S$ improves Q-point stability.*
>
> **Part (b) — 7 marks:** *For a Depletion MOSFET with $I_{DSS} = 10\,\text{mA}$, $V_P = -4\,\text{V}$, biased with $R_S = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_D = 2.2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{DD} = 20\,\text{V}$, find the Q-point $(I_{D,Q}, V_{DS,Q})$ and the stability factor $S$.*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Self-bias description (2 marks):**
In the self-bias (or source-bias) configuration of a Depletion MOSFET, the gate is grounded through a large resistor $R_G$ (carrying no DC current), the source is connected to ground through a resistor $R_S$, and the drain is connected to $V_{DD}$ through $R_D$. The circuit is **single-supply** and self-regulating: the drain current flowing through $R_S$ develops a voltage that biases the gate negatively, automatically stabilising the operating point.

**Step 2 — Derive $V_{GS}$ and $I_D$ equations (2 marks):**

$$
V_{GS} \;=\; -I_D R_S
$$

$$
I_D \;=\; I_{DSS} \left( 1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P} \right)^2 \;=\; I_{DSS} \left( 1 + \frac{I_D R_S}{V_P} \right)^2
$$

Differentiating implicitly with respect to $I_{D0}$ (where $I_{D0}$ represents any drift in $I_{DSS}$):

$$
\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial I_{D0}} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + 2 I_{DSS} R_S / V_P \cdot \left(1 + I_D R_S / V_P\right) \cdot \text{etc.}}
$$

A cleaner form is obtained by working in terms of the small-signal transconductance parameter $g_{m0} = 2 I_{DSS} / \vert V_P \vert$ at the origin. The stability factor is:

$$
\boxed{\, S \;=\; \frac{\partial I_{D,Q}}{\partial I_{D0}} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + g_{m0} R_S} \,}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (2 marks):**
As $R_S$ increases, the denominator grows, and $S$ decreases — meaning a *unit* change in $I_{D0}$ produces a *smaller* change in $I_{D,Q}$. The **negative feedback** action through $R_S$ is responsible: any tendency of $I_D$ to increase causes a larger voltage drop across $R_S$, reducing $V_{GS}$, which in turn suppresses the increase in $I_D$. Hence, the self-bias scheme provides **inherent thermal stability** that fixed-bias lacks.

**Step 4 — Limitation (1 mark):**
Increasing $R_S$ excessively reduces $V_{DS,Q}$ (headroom for AC swing) and may push the device toward the triode region. A **compromise** between stability and signal swing must be made by the designer.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Set up the self-bias quadratic (2 marks):**
With $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$:

$$
I_D \;=\; I_{DSS} \left( 1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P} \right)^2 \;=\; 10 \left( 1 + \frac{I_D \times 1}{-4} \right)^2 \;=\; 10 \left( 1 - 0.25 I_D \right)^2
$$

(Here $I_D$ is in mA and $R_S$ in k$\Omega$.)

**Step 2 — Expand and solve (2 marks):**

$$
I_D \;=\; 10 (1 - 0.5 I_D + 0.0625 I_D^2) \;=\; 10 - 5 I_D + 0.625 I_D^2
$$

$$
0.625 I_D^2 - 6 I_D + 10 \;=\; 0
$$

Apply the quadratic formula:

$$
I_D \;=\; \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{36 - 25}}{1.25} \;=\; \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{11}}{1.25} \;=\; \frac{6 \pm 3.317}{1.25}
$$

$$
I_{D,1} \;=\; \frac{6 - 3.317}{1.25} \;=\; 2.147\;\text{mA}
\quad\quad
I_{D,2} \;=\; \frac{6 + 3.317}{1.25} \;=\; 7.454\;\text{mA}
$$

**Step 3 — Select valid root (1 mark):**
$I_{D,2} = 7.454\,\text{mA} < I_{DSS} = 10\,\text{mA}$ ✓ but gives $V_{GS} = -7.454 < V_P = -4$, which is **beyond pinch-off** → cutoff region, **invalid**.

$I_{D,1} = 2.147\,\text{mA}$ gives $V_{GS} = -2.147\,\text{V}$, which satisfies $V_P < V_{GS} < 0$ → **valid Q-point**.

**Step 4 — Compute Q-point coordinates (1 mark):**

$$
V_{DS,Q} \;=\; V_{DD} - I_{D,Q}(R_D + R_S) \;=\; 20 - 2.147 \times (2.2 + 1) \;=\; 20 - 6.872 \;=\; 13.13\;\text{V}
$$

**Step 5 — Stability factor (1 mark):**

$$
g_{m0} \;=\; \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 10}{4} \;=\; 5\;\text{mA/V}
$$

$$
S \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + g_{m0} R_S} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + 5 \times 1} \;=\; \frac{1}{6} \;=\; 0.167
$$

$$
\boxed{\, I_{D,Q} = 2.15\;\text{mA}, \quad V_{DS,Q} = 13.13\;\text{V}, \quad S = 0.167 \,}
$$

The stability factor $S = 0.167$ is small (well below 1), confirming the Q-point is well stabilised against drift in $I_{DSS}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Discarding the wrong root of the quadratic (–2 marks):** Almost every biasing problem yields two roots. Students often pick the larger one (e.g., $I_D = 4.7\,\text{mA}$ in the example above) and forget to check the validity condition $V_{GS,Q} > V_{th}$. Always verify *both* roots.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the saturation check (–2 marks):** Computing $I_{D,Q}$ and $V_{DS,Q}$ without verifying $V_{DS,Q} \geq V_{GS,Q} - V_{th}$ is treated as an *incomplete* answer. The examiner expects a one-line statement: *"Since $V_{DS,Q} > V_{GS,Q} - V_{th}$, the device is in saturation."*
>
> 3. **Confusing $V_{th}$ with $V_P$ (–1 mark):** $V_{th}$ is for Enhancement MOSFETs, $V_P$ (pinch-off) is for Depletion MOSFETs and JFETs. Mixing them in the same equation is a common error.
>
> 4. **Loading the voltage divider (–2 marks):** When the gate is connected to a voltage divider, students sometimes include $R_G$ in the divider calculation. Since $I_G = 0$, $R_G$ does *not* load the divider — a frequent KTU trap.
>
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer (–1 mark):** KTU has a strict "always quote units" rule. $I_D$ in mA without "mA" loses one mark. $V_{DS}$ in V without "V" loses one mark.
>
> 6. **Using $V_{DD}$ instead of $V_{DD} - I_D R_D$ for $V_{DS}$ (–1 mark):** $V_{DS}$ is the voltage *across* the device, not the supply voltage. The drain-loop KVL is mandatory.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-second revision before the exam:**

- [x] **Biasing** = fixing the DC operating point $(I_{D,Q}, V_{DS,Q})$ of a MOSFET for linear amplification.
- [x] **Saturation region conditions:** $V_{GS,Q} > V_{th}$ **AND** $V_{DS,Q} \geq V_{GS,Q} - V_{th}$.
- [x] **Square law (Enhancement):** $I_D = K (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$, with $K = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} W/L$.
- [x] **Square law (Depletion):** $I_D = I_{DSS} (1 - V_{GS}/V_P)^2$.
- [x] **Four canonical schemes:** Fixed bias, Self-bias, Voltage-divider bias, Drain-feedback bias.
- [x] **Voltage-divider bias** is the **most stable** for Enhancement MOSFETs → most-examined configuration.
- [x] **Self-bias** is preferred for Depletion MOSFETs and JFETs (single supply, no $V_{GG}$).
- [x] **Drain-feedback bias** uses $I_G = 0$ to set $V_G = V_D$, creating automatic negative feedback.
- [x] **Stability factor** $S = \partial I_{D,Q}/\partial I_{D0}$ — *smaller* $\vert S \vert$ means *more stable* Q-point.
- [x] **Q-point design rule:** Place $V_{DS,Q}$ near $V_{DD}/2$ for maximum symmetric AC swing.
- [x] **Two-root trap:** Every biasing quadratic gives two roots — always check $V_{GS,Q} > V_{th}$ to pick the *valid* one.
- [x] **Divider rule:** Because $I_G = 0$, the gate divider is *unloaded*; the gate voltage is exactly $V_G = V_{DD}\,R_2/(R_1 + R_2)$.
- [x] **Negative feedback** through $R_S$ (or $R_F$) is the *physical mechanism* that gives the bias its stability.
- [x] **DC load line** is the line $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$ drawn on the output characteristics; the Q-point is the intersection with the $I_D = K(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ parabola.
- [x] **Midpoint Q-point** minimises both cutoff and saturation-region clipping for a sine-wave input.
- [x] **Larger $R_S$** → more stability but less $V_{DS,Q}$ headroom; design is a trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oscillator Circuits: Effect of Positive and Negative Feedback on Gain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Oscillator Circuits: Effect of Positive and Negative Feedback on Gain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Feedback

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feedback** is the process of transferring a portion of the output signal back to the input of an amplifier. It is a fundamental concept that classifies all practical amplifier and oscillator designs into two broad families based on the **relative phase** of the fed-back signal.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCPT304)**, the effect of feedback on the closed-loop gain of an amplifier is the foundational concept that connects linear amplifiers to sinusoidal oscillators. The same mathematical framework governs both — only the sign of the feedback term changes.

### 1.2 The Two Families of Feedback

**A. Negative Feedback (Degenerative Feedback)**
> [!NOTE]
> The portion of the output that is returned to the input is **180° out of phase** with the applied input signal. It *opposes* the input, thereby **reducing the net effective gain** but simultaneously improving almost every other amplifier parameter.

**B. Positive Feedback (Regenerative Feedback)**
> [!NOTE]
> The portion of the output that is returned to the input is **in phase** (0° or a multiple of 360°) with the applied input signal. It *aids* the input, thereby **increasing the net effective gain**. When the loop gain reaches unity, the amplifier becomes an **oscillator**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of steering a car on a highway:**
>
> - **Negative Feedback** is like **cruise control with lane-keep assist** — the system continuously observes the deviation from the desired path and applies a *correcting* force in the opposite direction. The car is stable, smooth, and follows the set speed precisely, even if the wind pushes it sideways. The "gain" (responsiveness) reduces a little, but accuracy and stability skyrocket.
>
> - **Positive Feedback** is like **placing a microphone in front of its own speaker** — the speaker output is picked up by the mic, amplified again, sent to the speaker, picked up again, and so on. The amplitude keeps growing *exponentially* until the system either saturates (clipping) or finds a new equilibrium — the **oscillation** that every audio engineer has unfortunately experienced at some point!

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- The **feedback factor (β)** is dimensionless and lies between **0 and 1** for most practical designs.
- The **loop gain (Aβ)** is the most important metric — it determines whether the circuit amplifies cleanly (negative feedback) or oscillates (positive feedback).
- The **unity-gain crossover frequency (f_T)** marks the upper limit where the open-loop gain A drops to 1.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Feedback Topology

Every feedback amplifier (and oscillator) can be reduced to a canonical block diagram consisting of two fundamental blocks:

1. **The Basic Amplifier (A-block)** — provides open-loop gain A.
2. **The Feedback Network (β-block)** — samples the output and produces a feedback signal that is summed (or subtracted) at the input.

The **mixing node** (summing junction) is where the feedback signal combines with the external source. The **sampling node** is where the output is tapped (either as voltage or current).

## 2.2 The Two Sign Conventions

| Convention | Sign in Denominator | Effect on Gain | Stability |
|------------|---------------------|----------------|-----------|
| **Negative Feedback** | $(1 + \beta A)$ | Gain **decreases** by factor $(1 + \beta A)$ | System is **stabilized** |
| **Positive Feedback** | $(1 - \beta A)$ | Gain **increases** as $\beta A \rightarrow 1$ | System **oscillates** when $\beta A = 1$ |

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Feedback Action

- **Step 1:** An external source signal $X_s$ is applied to the mixing junction.
- **Step 2:** A portion $\beta X_o$ of the output $X_o$ is sampled and returned.
- **Step 3:** The error signal $X_d = X_s \pm \beta X_o$ drives the basic amplifier.
- **Step 4:** The amplifier output becomes $X_o = A X_d$.
- **Step 5:** Combining the above relations yields the **closed-loop transfer function** (see Section 3 for derivation).

## 2.4 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Effects

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does negative feedback improve so many parameters simultaneously?**
> Because any change in the output is *automatically corrected* by the feedback loop. The system is forced to settle at a value dictated by the precise, passive feedback network β rather than the nonlinear, temperature-dependent, active amplifier A.

> [!NOTE]
> **How does positive feedback cause oscillation?**
> The amplifier is no longer "amplifying" a controlled input — it is *feeding its own output back into itself*. Each round trip multiplies the signal by Aβ. If Aβ > 1, the amplitude grows without bound (limited by device non-linearity). If Aβ = 1 exactly, a sustained sinusoidal oscillation is produced — this is precisely the **Barkhausen Criterion**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Negative Feedback | Positive Feedback | Units |
|---|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Closed-loop gain | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + \beta A}$ | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 - \beta A}$ | dimensionless (V/V or A/A) |
| 2 | Loop gain | $T = \beta A$ | $T = \beta A$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Desensitivity factor | $D = 1 + \beta A$ | $D = 1 - \beta A$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Bandwidth | $BW_f = BW \cdot (1 + \beta A)$ | $BW_f = \dfrac{BW}{1 - \beta A}$ | Hz |
| 5 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | Constant (= $A \cdot BW$) | Increases | Hz |
| 6 | Input Impedance (Series mix) | $Z_{if} = Z_i(1 + \beta A)$ | $Z_{if} = Z_i(1 - \beta A)$ | Ω |
| 7 | Input Impedance (Shunt mix) | $Z_{if} = \dfrac{Z_i}{1 + \beta A}$ | $Z_{if} = \dfrac{Z_i}{1 - \beta A}$ | Ω |
| 8 | Output Impedance (Voltage samp.) | $Z_{of} = \dfrac{Z_o}{1 + \beta A}$ | $Z_{of} = \dfrac{Z_o}{1 - \beta A}$ | Ω |
| 9 | Output Impedance (Current samp.) | $Z_{of} = Z_o(1 + \beta A)$ | $Z_{of} = Z_o(1 - \beta A)$ | Ω |
| 10 | Distortion (Harmonic) | $D_f = \dfrac{D}{1 + \beta A}$ | $D_f = \dfrac{D}{1 - \beta A}$ | % or dB |
| 11 | Noise figure improvement | $NF_f = NF \cdot (1 + \beta A)$ | (Not used in design) | dB |
| 12 | Barkhausen (Oscillation) | N/A | $\vert \beta A \vert = 1$, $\angle \beta A = 0°$ | — |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Negative feedback** is the cornerstone of every op-amp, audio amplifier, instrumentation amplifier, voltage regulator (LM7805, LM317), and phase-locked loop. The **μA741 op-amp**, the **TL072**, and even modern **Class-D audio amplifiers** rely on heavy negative feedback to achieve their published specifications.
- **Positive feedback** is the heart of every **sinusoidal oscillator** (Wien-bridge, RC phase-shift, Colpitts, Hartley, Crystal), **Schmitt trigger**, **comparator with hysteresis**, and **bi-stable multivibrator**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The closed-loop gain formula $A_f = \frac{A}{1 + \beta A}$ is asked almost every year. Always write the *negative* feedback version when the question mentions stability, bandwidth, or distortion, and the *positive* feedback version (with minus sign in the denominator) when the question is about oscillators.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closed-Loop Gain of a Basic Feedback Amplifier

Consider a basic amplifier of open-loop gain $A$ cascaded with a feedback network of transfer ratio $\beta$. The input source $X_s$ and the feedback signal $\beta X_o$ are combined at the summing junction to produce the difference signal $X_d$.

$$
X_d = X_s - \beta X_o \quad \text{(negative feedback convention)}
$$

The output of the basic amplifier is the difference signal multiplied by its gain:

$$
X_o = A \cdot X_d
$$

Substituting the first equation into the second:

$$
X_o = A(X_s - \beta X_o)
$$

$$
X_o = A X_s - A \beta X_o
$$

Collecting the $X_o$ terms on the left-hand side:

$$
X_o + A \beta X_o = A X_s
$$

$$
X_o(1 + \beta A) = A X_s
$$

The closed-loop gain is therefore:

$$
\boxed{A_f = \frac{X_o}{X_s} = \frac{A}{1 + \beta A}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The term $(1 + \beta A)$ is called the **Desensitivity Factor** or **Return Difference**. It is the single most important quantity in feedback theory. If $\beta A \gg 1$, then $A_f \approx \frac{1}{\beta}$, which means the closed-loop gain is determined *entirely* by the passive feedback network.

For the **positive feedback** case, the sign in the summing junction reverses:

$$
\boxed{A_f^{+} = \frac{A}{1 - \beta A}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effect of Negative Feedback on Gain Sensitivity

Suppose the open-loop gain $A$ changes by a small amount $dA$ due to temperature, ageing, or supply variation. We want to find the resulting change $dA_f$ in the closed-loop gain.

Starting with $A_f = \frac{A}{1 + \beta A}$, take the differential with respect to $A$:

$$
\frac{dA_f}{dA} = \frac{(1 + \beta A)(1) - A(\beta)}{(1 + \beta A)^2}
$$

$$
\frac{dA_f}{dA} = \frac{1 + \beta A - \beta A}{(1 + \beta A)^2}
$$

$$
\frac{dA_f}{dA} = \frac{1}{(1 + \beta A)^2}
$$

Divide both sides by $A_f$ to obtain the **fractional sensitivity**:

$$
\frac{dA_f}{A_f} = \frac{1}{(1 + \beta A)^2} \cdot \frac{dA}{A_f}
$$

But $A_f = \frac{A}{1 + \beta A}$, so $\frac{1}{A_f} = \frac{1 + \beta A}{A}$. Substituting:

$$
\frac{dA_f}{A_f} = \frac{1}{(1 + \beta A)^2} \cdot \frac{1 + \beta A}{A} \cdot dA
$$

$$
\frac{dA_f}{A_f} = \frac{1}{1 + \beta A} \cdot \frac{dA}{A}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The percentage change in closed-loop gain is $(1 + \beta A)$ times *smaller* than the percentage change in open-loop gain. This is why negative feedback stabilises the amplifier.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effect on Input Impedance (Series Mixing)

When the feedback signal is mixed in **series** with the source (voltage-series or series-series feedback), the input sees an opposing voltage $\beta X_o$. The voltage drop across the input impedance $Z_i$ is:

$$
V_i = I_i \cdot Z_i = X_s - \beta X_o
$$

But $X_o = A X_d \approx A X_i$ (when source impedance is small). Substituting:

$$
I_i \cdot Z_i = X_s - \beta A \cdot I_i Z_i
$$

$$
X_s = I_i Z_i (1 + \beta A)
$$

The effective input impedance seen by the source is $Z_{if} = \dfrac{X_s}{I_i}$:

$$
\boxed{Z_{if} = Z_i (1 + \beta A)}
$$

For **shunt mixing** (voltage-shunt or current-shunt feedback), the current $I_i$ is divided:

$$
\boxed{Z_{if} = \frac{Z_i}{1 + \beta A}}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Effect on Output Impedance (Voltage Sampling)

For voltage sampling, the output voltage is forced to be $X_o = A X_d$ regardless of load. Looking back into the output port, the feedback network applies a voltage $\beta X_o$ that *opposes* any change in $X_o$. The Thévenin source $V_{th}$ driving the output sees an effective impedance:

$$
\boxed{Z_{of} = \frac{Z_o}{1 + \beta A}}
$$

For **current sampling**, the output current is sensed, and feedback acts to *maintain* it:

$$
\boxed{Z_{of} = Z_o (1 + \beta A)}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Effect on Bandwidth

The open-loop gain of a typical amplifier rolls off at high frequency as:

$$
A(f) = \frac{A_{mid}}{1 + j(f/f_H)}
$$

With negative feedback, the closed-loop gain becomes:

$$
A_f(f) = \frac{A(f)}{1 + \beta A(f)} = \frac{\frac{A_{mid}}{1 + j(f/f_H)}}{1 + \beta \cdot \frac{A_{mid}}{1 + j(f/f_H)}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(1 + j(f/f_H))$:

$$
A_f(f) = \frac{A_{mid}}{1 + j(f/f_H) + \beta A_{mid}} = \frac{\frac{A_{mid}}{1 + \beta A_{mid}}}{1 + j\dfrac{f}{f_H(1 + \beta A_{mid})}}
$$

The new midband gain and the new upper cutoff frequency are therefore:

$$
\boxed{A_{f,mid} = \frac{A_{mid}}{1 + \beta A_{mid}}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{H,f} = f_H(1 + \beta A_{mid})}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)** is invariant: $A_{f,mid} \cdot f_{H,f} = A_{mid} \cdot f_H$. This is the famous trade-off that allows op-amp designers to "trade" gain for bandwidth at a constant product.

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Distortion and Noise Reduction

Let the open-loop amplifier introduce a distortion component $D$ at the output. With negative feedback, a fraction $\beta$ of this distortion is fed back and subtracted from the input:

$$
D_f = D - \beta A D_f
$$

$$
D_f (1 + \beta A) = D
$$

$$
\boxed{D_f = \frac{D}{1 + \beta A}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Distortion, noise, and any internal non-linearity are all reduced by the same factor $(1 + \beta A)$ — a powerful benefit of negative feedback.

## 3.7 Derivation 7 — The Barkhausen Criterion (Positive Feedback / Oscillation)

For the circuit to oscillate *without* an external input, the closed-loop gain must become infinite (i.e., the denominator must vanish):

$$
1 - \beta A = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\beta A = 1}
$$

Including the complex (phasor) nature of A and β:

$$
\boxed{\vert \beta A \vert = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \angle(\beta A) = 0° \text{ or } 360° \text{ (or } 2n\pi \text{)} }
$$

This is the **Barkhausen Criterion** for sustained sinusoidal oscillation. The first condition is the **magnitude condition**; the second is the **phase condition** (also called the loop phase condition).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical Block Diagram of a Feedback Amplifier

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the universal feedback topology. It is applicable to *both* negative feedback amplifiers and positive feedback oscillators — only the sign at the summing junction differs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    source[Source Signal Xs] --> mixNode{Summing Junction}
    fbSig[Feedback Signal betaXo] --> mixNode
    mixNode --> errSig[Difference Signal Xd]
    errSig --> ampBlock[Basic Amplifier with Gain A]
    ampBlock --> outPort[Output Signal Xo]
    outPort --> outNode((Output Port))
    outPort --> fbNet[Feedback Network beta]
    fbNet --> fbSig
    style source fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style ampBlock fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style fbNet fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style outNode fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

> [!NOTE]
> The summing junction produces $X_d = X_s \pm \beta X_o$. The **+** sign gives negative feedback (amplifier); the **−** sign (i.e., $X_d = X_s - \beta X_o$) with $\beta A = 1$ gives positive feedback (oscillator).

## 4.2 Comparative Architecture: Negative vs Positive Feedback

The block-level functional architecture below contrasts the two regimes. Notice that the topology is **identical**; the only difference is the sign at the summer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NegPath[Negative Feedback Amplifier]
        n1[Input Xs] --> n2[Summing Junction - sign]
        n3[betaXo is subtracted] --> n2
        n2 --> n4[Xd is smaller than Xs]
        n4 --> n5[Amplifier A]
        n5 --> n6[Output Xo = A Xd]
        n6 --> n7[Feedback beta]
        n7 --> n3
    end

    subgraph PosPath[Positive Feedback Oscillator]
        p1[No external source] --> p2[Summing Junction + sign]
        p3[betaXo is added] --> p2
        p2 --> p4[Xd is reinforced]
        p4 --> p5[Amplifier A]
        p5 --> p6[Output Xo grows]
        p6 --> p7[Feedback beta]
        p7 --> p3
    end
    style n6 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style p6 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

## 4.3 Effect-Map: How Each Parameter Changes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    central([Feedback Type]) --> nfb[Negative Feedback]
    central --> pfb[Positive Feedback]
    nfb --> nGain[Gain reduced by 1+betaA]
    nfb --> nBW[Bandwidth increased by 1+betaA]
    nfb --> nDist[Distortion reduced by 1+betaA]
    nfb --> nStable[Stability increased]
    pfb --> pGain[Gain increased as betaA approaches 1]
    pfb --> pBW[Bandwidth reduced]
    pfb --> pDist[Distortion amplified]
    pfb --> pOsc[Produces oscillation when betaA = 1]
    style central fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style nfb fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style pfb fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram of a Practical Oscillator (RC Phase-Shift)

When negative feedback is deliberately converted to positive feedback at one specific frequency $f_o$, the amplifier becomes an oscillator. The Mermaid architecture below shows how a generic RC phase-shift oscillator uses three RC sections to provide the required $180°$ phase shift plus the inverting amplifier's $180°$ for a total loop phase of $360°$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    oscAmp[Inverting Amplifier Gain A] --> outOsc[Output Sine Wave]
    outOsc --> rc1[RC Section 1 - 60 deg]
    rc1 --> rc2[RC Section 2 - 60 deg]
    rc2 --> rc3[RC Section 3 - 60 deg]
    rc3 --> fbRet[Total 180 deg phase shift]
    fbRet --> oscAmp
    style oscAmp fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style outOsc fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style fbRet fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The amplifier inverts ($180°$ phase shift) and the RC ladder provides an *additional* $180°$ at the oscillation frequency $f_o$, giving a net loop phase of $360° \equiv 0°$. The Barkhausen magnitude condition $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ is then used to determine the *minimum* amplifier gain required to start the oscillation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define negative feedback in an amplifier. Mention any two advantages.**

**Model Answer:**
> Negative feedback is the process of feeding back a portion of the output signal to the input in **antiphase** (180° phase difference) with the input signal, such that the feedback signal **opposes** the input. The closed-loop gain becomes $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + \beta A}$, which is smaller than the open-loop gain $A$.

**Advantages (any two):**
- **Gain stability:** Variations in open-loop gain $A$ due to temperature, ageing, or supply changes are reduced by a factor of $(1 + \beta A)$ at the output.
- **Reduced distortion and noise:** Harmonic distortion and internally generated noise are reduced by the same factor $(1 + \beta A)$.
- **Increased bandwidth:** The upper cutoff frequency is extended by $(1 + \beta A)$.
- **Improved input/output impedance:** Series mixing raises $Z_{if}$; voltage sampling lowers $Z_{of}$.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**State the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillation.**

**Model Answer:**
> The Barkhausen criterion states two conditions that must *simultaneously* be satisfied for a feedback circuit to produce sustained sinusoidal oscillations:
>
> 1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must be unity. $\quad \vert \beta A \vert = 1$
> 2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the loop must be zero (or an integer multiple of $360°$). $\quad \angle(\beta A) = 0° \text{ or } 2n\pi$

> If $\vert \beta A \vert < 1$, the oscillation dies out (damped). If $\vert \beta A \vert > 1$, the oscillation grows in amplitude until limited by device non-linearity (clipped waveform).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 — Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the closed-loop gain of an amplifier with negative feedback. Explain how negative feedback affects the gain, bandwidth, and distortion of the amplifier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An amplifier has an open-loop gain of $A = 1000$ and a feedback factor of $\beta = 0.04$. Determine the closed-loop gain with negative feedback. If the open-loop gain changes by $\pm 10\%$, calculate the corresponding percentage change in the closed-loop gain. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a):

**Derivation of Closed-Loop Gain:**
Let the source signal be $X_s$, the difference signal be $X_d$, the output be $X_o$, and the feedback signal be $\beta X_o$. At the summing junction:

$$
X_d = X_s - \beta X_o
$$

The amplifier output is $X_o = A X_d$. Substituting:

$$
X_o = A(X_s - \beta X_o) \quad \Rightarrow \quad X_o(1 + \beta A) = A X_s
$$

$$
\boxed{A_f = \frac{A}{1 + \beta A}}
$$

**Effect on Gain:** The closed-loop gain is reduced by the factor $(1 + \beta A)$.

**Effect on Bandwidth:** The upper cutoff frequency increases to $f_{Hf} = f_H(1 + \beta A)$. The **Gain-Bandwidth Product** remains constant.

**Effect on Distortion:** Distortion is reduced from $D$ to $D_f = \dfrac{D}{1 + \beta A}$. Noise and internally generated harmonics behave identically.

---

#### Solution to A(b):

**Given:** $A = 1000$, $\beta = 0.04$

**Step 1 — Closed-loop gain with negative feedback:**

$$
A_f = \frac{A}{1 + \beta A} = \frac{1000}{1 + (0.04)(1000)} = \frac{1000}{1 + 40} = \frac{1000}{41} \approx 24.39
$$

**Step 2 — Percentage change in closed-loop gain for a 10% change in $A$:**

The desensitivity factor is $D = 1 + \beta A = 41$. The fractional sensitivity relation is:

$$
\frac{dA_f}{A_f} = \frac{1}{1 + \beta A} \cdot \frac{dA}{A} = \frac{1}{41} \cdot 10\% \approx 0.244\%
$$

**Step 3 — Comment:**
Even though the open-loop gain changes by $\pm 10\%$, the closed-loop gain changes by only about $\pm 0.244\%$. This demonstrates the **stabilising effect of negative feedback** — the closed-loop amplifier is 41 times less sensitive to variations in the open-loop gain.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the concept of positive feedback. How does it lead to oscillation? State the Barkhausen criterion and explain the significance of the magnitude and phase conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An RC phase-shift oscillator uses an inverting amplifier of gain $A$ and three identical RC sections each having $R = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$. Calculate (i) the frequency of oscillation, and (ii) the minimum gain required to sustain oscillation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a):

**Block Diagram and Concept:**
A portion of the output $X_o$ is sampled by the feedback network (transfer ratio $\beta$) and returned to the input. If the fed-back signal is **in phase** with the input, the feedback is **positive** (regenerative). The summing junction adds the two:

$$
X_d = X_s + \beta X_o \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_f^{+} = \frac{A}{1 - \beta A}
$$

As $\beta A \to 1$, the closed-loop gain tends to **infinity**, meaning the circuit can produce a finite output with **zero** external input — this is **self-sustained oscillation**.

**Barkhausen Criterion:**
For sustained sinusoidal oscillation, two conditions must be satisfied:

1. **Magnitude condition:** $\vert \beta A \vert = 1$ — the loop gain must be exactly unity; otherwise, the oscillation either decays ($\vert \beta A \vert < 1$) or grows into a clipped, non-sinusoidal waveform ($\vert \beta A \vert > 1$).
2. **Phase condition:** $\angle(\beta A) = 0° \text{ or } 360°$ — the total phase shift around the loop must be zero, so that the fed-back signal reinforces the input at that exact frequency.

**Significance:** The Barkhausen criterion is a *necessary* condition for sustained sinusoidal oscillation. The amplifier must compensate for the phase shift introduced by the frequency-selective feedback network, and the overall loop gain must be unity at that frequency.

---

#### Solution to B(b):

**Given:** $R = 10\text{ k}\Omega = 10^4\,\Omega$, $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F} = 10^{-8}\,\text{F}$, three identical RC sections.

**(i) Frequency of oscillation:**

The standard formula for an RC phase-shift oscillator with three identical RC sections is:

$$
\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi R C \sqrt{6}}}
$$

Computing $RC$:

$$
RC = (10^4)(10^{-8}) = 10^{-4}\,\text{s}
$$

$$
\sqrt{6} \approx 2.449
$$

$$
2\pi RC \sqrt{6} = 2\pi \times 10^{-4} \times 2.449 \approx 1.539 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{1.539 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 649.7\,\text{Hz} \approx 650\,\text{Hz}
$$

**(ii) Minimum gain required:**

The minimum gain for a three-section RC phase-shift oscillator is:

$$
\boxed{A_{min} = 29}
$$

(Independent of $R$ and $C$ — it depends only on the number of RC sections.) Therefore, the amplifier must provide a gain of **at least 29** to satisfy the Barkhausen magnitude condition $\vert \beta A \vert \ge 1$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in the Denominator:** The single most common error is writing $A_f = \frac{A}{1 + \beta A}$ for *positive* feedback. The correct expression for positive feedback is $A_f = \frac{A}{1 - \beta A}$. Examiners *will* deduct **1 to 2 marks** for this mistake.
> 2. **Forgetting Units in RC Oscillators:** Always carry the units of $R$ (Ω) and $C$ (F) and produce $f_o$ in **Hz**. A numerical answer without units loses ½ mark.
> 3. **Mixing Up the Four Feedback Topologies:** The four topologies (voltage-series, voltage-shunt, current-series, current-shunt) affect $Z_{if}$ and $Z_{of}$ *differently*. Do not blindly multiply or divide $Z_i$ and $Z_o$ by $(1+\beta A)$ without identifying the topology first.
> 4. **Barkhausen Condition Statement:** Many students write "$\beta A = 1$" without mentioning the phase condition. The phase condition $\angle(\beta A) = 0°$ is **equally important** and is usually worth 1 mark in a 7-mark question.
> 5. **Desensitivity vs Sensitivity:** Do not write $A_f = A(1 + \beta A)$. This is a classic guess that gains **zero** marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Negative feedback** *reduces* gain but improves stability, bandwidth, input/output impedance, linearity, and noise performance.
- **Positive feedback** *increases* gain and is the basis of every sinusoidal oscillator.
- The closed-loop gain for **negative feedback** is $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + \beta A}$.
- The closed-loop gain for **positive feedback** is $A_f^{+} = \dfrac{A}{1 - \beta A}$.
- The term $(1 + \beta A)$ is the **desensitivity factor** — it appears in *every* negative-feedback effect formula.
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product** is *constant* for a given amplifier — you cannot increase both simultaneously.
- The **Barkhausen Criterion** has two parts: $\vert \beta A \vert = 1$ *and* $\angle(\beta A) = 0°$ (or $2n\pi$).
- The **RC phase-shift oscillator** has $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ and requires $A_{min} = 29$ for three RC sections.
- **Distortion** with negative feedback is reduced to $D_f = \dfrac{D}{1 + \beta A}$.
- **Sensitivity** with negative feedback is $\dfrac{dA_f}{A_f} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \beta A} \cdot \dfrac{dA}{A}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency Response and Distortion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Frequency Response and Distortion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Frequency Response — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequency Response** of an electronic amplifier is defined as the variation of the **magnitude of the voltage gain (|A_v|)** and the **phase shift (φ)** of the output signal with respect to the input signal, measured as a continuous function of the operating signal frequency.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context (PBCPT304 – Analog and Digital Electronics), frequency response describes how faithfully an amplifier reproduces input signals across a spectrum of frequencies — from very low audio frequencies (e.g., **20 Hz**) to very high radio frequencies (e.g., **MHz** range). The graph of **|A_v| (in dB) vs. log f** is called the **Bode Plot** (or frequency response curve).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Hose" Picture

Imagine an amplifier as a **water hose** carrying sound (water). 
- At **low frequencies** (gentle water flow), the hose feels heavy and resistant — water doesn't pass freely because of large coupling capacitors (like big stiff valves). This is the **low-frequency response problem**.
- In the **mid-band** (normal water pressure), everything flows smoothly and the gain is at its maximum — this is the **flat, useful range**.
- At **very high frequencies** (turbo pressure), the water sprays out chaotically, and internal wire capacitance (like small leaks) siphons energy away. This is the **high-frequency response problem**.

## 1.3 Distortion — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Distortion** is any unwanted change in the waveform shape of the output signal compared to the input signal. When the output is not a faithful scaled replica of the input, the amplifier is said to introduce **distortion**.

There are three principal categories of distortion studied in this module:
1. **Amplitude (Non-linear) Distortion** — Caused by non-linear devices (transistors, diodes).
2. **Frequency (Linear) Distortion** — Caused by reactive components (C, L) having frequency-dependent impedance.
3. **Phase Distortion** — Different frequency components experience different time delays.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module explicitly covers *amplitude frequency response, phase response, and distortion in amplifiers* — the relationship between gain and frequency, the concept of bandwidth, lower/upper cutoff frequencies, and the various types of distortion introduced by non-ideal amplifier behavior.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Frequency Regions of an Amplifier

A practical RC-coupled amplifier exhibits three distinct operating regions when analyzed over a wide frequency spectrum:

| Region | Frequency Range | Dominant Reactive Element | Gain Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-Frequency Region** | $f < f_1$ | Coupling Capacitors ($C_C$), Emitter Bypass Capacitor ($C_E$) | Gain rolls off at **+20 dB/decade** slope |
| **Mid-Frequency Region** | $f_1 \le f \le f_2$ | All capacitors act as ideal shorts; stray capacitances open | Gain is **constant and maximum** ($A_{mid}$) |
| **High-Frequency Region** | $f > f_2$ | Internal junction capacitances ($C_{bc}$, $C_{be}$, Miller capacitance) | Gain rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** slope |

## 2.2 Cutoff Frequencies and Bandwidth

> [!NOTE]
> The **lower cutoff frequency ($f_L$)** and **upper cutoff frequency ($f_H$)** are the frequencies at which the power gain drops to **half (½)** of its mid-band value, which corresponds to the **voltage gain dropping to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707$** of the mid-band value. This represents a **−3 dB** point.

The **Bandwidth (BW)** of the amplifier is defined as:

$$BW = f_H - f_L$$

For a typical audio amplifier, $f_L$ is in the range of **20–100 Hz**, and $f_H$ is in the range of **20 kHz–several MHz**.

## 2.3 The Decibel (dB) Scale

The decibel scale is used universally in frequency response analysis because the human ear responds logarithmically to sound intensity.

$$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \right) = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$$

| dB Value | Voltage Ratio |
|---|---|
| **+20 dB** | Gain of 10× |
| **−3 dB** | Gain of $0.707 \times$ (cutoff point) |
| **−20 dB** | Gain of $0.1 \times$ (10% of input) |
| **−40 dB** | Gain of $0.01 \times$ (1% of input) |

## 2.4 Distortion in Amplifiers — Theoretical Framework

### A. Amplitude (Non-linear) Distortion
This occurs when the amplifier's transfer characteristic is non-linear. When a pure sine wave passes through a non-linear system, **harmonics** (multiples of the fundamental frequency $f$) are generated.

**Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** is the standard metric:

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$

Where $V_1$ is the RMS voltage of the fundamental frequency, and $V_2, V_3, \ldots, V_n$ are RMS voltages of the 2nd, 3rd, ..., nth harmonics.

### B. Frequency (Linear) Distortion
Linear distortion occurs when the amplifier's gain is **not constant across the signal's frequency spectrum**. The waveform shape is altered because different harmonics are amplified by different amounts. This is a **linear phenomenon** (no new frequencies are generated).

### C. Phase Distortion
If different frequency components of a complex signal suffer different phase shifts, the relative timing between them is altered, distorting the output waveform shape even though the amplitude is preserved.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voltage Gain in dB | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ | dB |
| 2 | Power Gain in dB | $A_{p(dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{out}/P_{in})$ | dB |
| 3 | Cutoff Frequency | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Hz |
| 4 | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L$ | Hz |
| 5 | Low-freq. cutoff (CE amp) | $f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_i) C_C}$ | Hz |
| 6 | THD | $THD = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 7 | SNR | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}(V_{signal}/V_{noise})$ | dB |
| 8 | Miller Capacitance | $C_{M} = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | Farads |
| 9 | High-freq. cutoff (approx.) | $f_H \approx \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{in(total)}}$ | Hz |
| 10 | Phase shift at cutoff | $\phi = \pm 45^{\circ}$ | Degrees |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Amplifiers**: Need bandwidth of 20 Hz – 20 kHz to faithfully reproduce human hearing range.
- **RF Communication Systems**: Need sharp frequency response for channel selectivity.
- **Medical Equipment (ECG, EEG amplifiers)**: Need flat response within narrow low-frequency bands.
- **Oscilloscope Probes**: Calibrated frequency response ensures accurate signal measurement.
- **Hi-Fi Music Systems**: Designers minimize THD to below **0.1%** for high fidelity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lower Cutoff Frequency ($f_L$)

Consider a **Common-Emitter RC-coupled amplifier**. The coupling capacitor $C_C$ and the bypass capacitor $C_E$ form **High-Pass RC filter** networks with the surrounding resistances.

### Step 1: Equivalent Circuit at Low Frequencies

At low frequencies, coupling capacitor $C_C$ has significant impedance. The Thévenin equivalent resistance seen by $C_C$ is:

$$R_{eq, C_C} = R_C + R_i$$

Where $R_i$ is the input resistance of the next stage (or the load), and $R_C$ is the output resistance of the previous stage.

### Step 2: Apply the Standard Cutoff Formula for an RC High-Pass Filter

A first-order RC high-pass filter has a cutoff (corner) frequency defined as the frequency where the magnitude of the transfer function equals $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of the passband value:

$$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_C}$$

### Step 3: Including the Bypass Capacitor

When the emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$ is also considered, it introduces another high-pass filter. The combined low-frequency cutoff can be approximated as the **dominant pole** (the highest of the individual cutoff frequencies):

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{L1} &= \frac{1}{2\pi (R_{out} + R_i) C_C} \\
f_{L2} &= \frac{1}{2\pi (R_E \Vert \frac{R_S + r_{\pi}}{\beta + 1}) C_E}
\end{aligned}$$

The dominant lower cutoff frequency is:

$$f_L \approx \max(f_{L1}, f_{L2})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "max"?** Because the overall transfer function is the **product** of two high-pass filter responses. The highest individual cutoff frequency dominates and determines where the overall gain starts to fall.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Upper Cutoff Frequency ($f_H$)

At high frequencies, **Miller's Theorem** is crucial.

### Step 1: Miller's Theorem

If an impedance $Z$ connects input node (voltage $V_i$) to output node (voltage $V_o = A_v V_i$), it can be replaced by two grounded impedances:
- **Input side Miller impedance:** $Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - A_v}$
- **Output side Miller impedance:** $Z_2 = \frac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}$

### Step 2: Miller Capacitance

For a CE amplifier, the base-collector junction capacitance $C_{bc}$ is the dominant high-frequency culprit. The voltage gain is large and negative ($A_v \approx -g_m R_C$). Applying Miller's Theorem:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{M} &= C_{bc} (1 - A_v) \\
      &\approx C_{bc} (1 + \vert A_v \vert)
\end{aligned}$$

This **Miller Capacitance** multiplies the small $C_{bc}$ (typically a few pF) into a **large effective input capacitance** (tens to hundreds of pF).

### Step 3: Total Input Capacitance

$$C_{in(total)} = C_{be} + C_{M} = C_{be} + C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$

### Step 4: Upper Cutoff Frequency

The effective source resistance $R_{eq}$ driving this capacitance forms a low-pass filter:

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{in(total)}}$$

Where $R_{eq} = R_S \Vert R_B \Vert r_{\pi}$.

## 3.3 Complete Gain Expression

Combining both regions, the **complete normalized gain** of an amplifier can be expressed as:

$$A_v(j\omega) = \frac{A_{mid}}{\left(1 + \frac{f_{L1}}{jf}\right)\left(1 + \frac{f_{L2}}{jf}\right) \left(1 + \frac{jf}{f_H}\right)}$$

In magnitude form:

$$\vert A_v(f) \vert = \frac{A_{mid}}{\sqrt{\left(1 + \left(\frac{f_{L1}}{f}\right)^2\right)\left(1 + \left(\frac{f_{L2}}{f}\right)^2\right)\left(1 + \left(\frac{f}{f_H}\right)^2\right)}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)

Consider a non-linear device whose transfer characteristic can be expressed as a power series:

$$v_{out}(t) = a_0 + a_1 v_{in}(t) + a_2 v_{in}^2(t) + a_3 v_{in}^3(t) + \cdots$$

### Step 1: Apply a Pure Sinusoidal Input

Let $v_{in}(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t)$

### Step 2: Expand Using Trigonometric Identities

Using $\cos^2(\omega t) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega t)}{2}$ and $\cos^3(\omega t) = \frac{3\cos(\omega t) + \cos(3\omega t)}{4}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{out}(t) &= a_0 + a_1 V_m \cos(\omega t) \\
           &+ a_2 V_m^2 \cdot \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \\
           &+ a_3 V_m^3 \cdot \frac{3\cos(\omega t) + \cos(3\omega t)}{4} + \cdots
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Identify the Harmonic Components

- **DC Component:** $a_0 + \frac{a_2 V_m^2}{2} + \cdots$
- **Fundamental (ω):** $a_1 V_m + \frac{3 a_3 V_m^3}{4} + \cdots$ — Amplitude depends on $V_m$, indicating non-linearity.
- **2nd Harmonic (2ω):** $\frac{a_2 V_m^2}{2}$
- **3rd Harmonic (3ω):** $\frac{a_3 V_m^3}{4}$

### Step 4: Calculate THD

The fundamental RMS amplitude: $V_1 = a_1 V_m$ (first order)
The total harmonic RMS amplitude: $V_h = \sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots}$

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Frequency Response Calculation

**Problem:** An amplifier has $f_L = 100$ Hz, $f_H = 20$ kHz, and mid-band gain $A_{mid} = 1000$. Calculate:
(i) The bandwidth.
(ii) The voltage gain at $f = 50$ Hz.
(iii) The voltage gain in dB at $f = 10$ kHz.

### Solution:

**Part (i) — Bandwidth:**
$$BW = f_H - f_L = 20{,}000 - 100 = 19{,}900 \text{ Hz} \approx 19.9 \text{ kHz}$$

**Part (ii) — Gain at 50 Hz (low-frequency region):**

The frequency ratio: $\frac{f}{f_L} = \frac{50}{100} = 0.5$

Since we are below $f_L$, the gain is reduced. For a single-pole response:

$$\vert A_v \vert = \frac{A_{mid}}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{f_L}{f}\right)^2}} = \frac{1000}{\sqrt{1 + (100/50)^2}} = \frac{1000}{\sqrt{1 + 4}} = \frac{1000}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 447.2$$

**Part (iii) — Gain in dB at 10 kHz (mid-band):**

Since 10 kHz lies between $f_L$ (100 Hz) and $f_H$ (20 kHz), the amplifier is in the flat mid-band region:

$$\vert A_v \vert \approx A_{mid} = 1000$$

Converting to dB:

$$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(1000) = 20 \times 3 = 60 \text{ dB}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Frequency Response Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how an input signal traverses the amplifier and undergoes frequency-dependent processing at each internal stage:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT SIGNAL SOURCE Vs Rs"] --> nodeB["COUPLING CAPACITOR CC STAGE 1"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["CE AMPLIFIER STAGE TRANSISTOR WITH C_be AND C_bc"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["MILLER MULTIPLICATION BLOCK C_M = C_bc x 1 PLUS A_v"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["BYPASS CAPACITOR C_E EMITTER NETWORK"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["COUPLING CAPACITOR CC STAGE 2 INTERSTAGE"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["OUTPUT LOAD RL"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["FINAL AMPLIFIED OUTPUT V_out"]

    nodeB -.->|"Low-freq HPF: f_L1 = 1 over 2piR1C1"| nodeI["LOW-FREQUENCY POLES DOMINATE f_L"]
    nodeE -.->|"Low-freq HPF: f_L2 = 1 over 2piR2C2"| nodeI
    nodeD -.->|"High-freq LPF: f_H = 1 over 2piR3C3"| nodeJ["HIGH-FREQUENCY POLE DOMINATES f_H"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["BANDWIDTH BW = f_H minus f_L"]
    nodeI --> nodeK
```

## 4.2 Bode Plot — Magnitude Response Architecture

This Mermaid block represents the canonical three-region Bode magnitude plot showing how gain varies with frequency:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeL["REGION 1 LOW FREQUENCY f LESS THAN f_L"] -->|"ROLL-OFF +20 dB per decade"| nodeM["REGION 2 MID BAND FLAT GAIN A_mid in dB f_L to f_H"]
    nodeM -->|"ROLL-OFF MINUS 20 dB per decade"| nodeN["REGION 3 HIGH FREQUENCY f GREATER THAN f_H"]
    nodeM -->|"BANDWIDTH BW = f_H minus f_L"| nodeO["USEFUL PASSBAND REGION"]

    nodeP["AT f_L: GAIN IS MINUS 3 dB BELOW A_mid"] -.-> nodeL
    nodeQ["AT f_H: GAIN IS MINUS 3 dB BELOW A_mid"] -.-> nodeN
```

## 4.3 Distortion Generation — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeR["PURE SINE INPUT V_in = V_m cos wt"] --> nodeS["NON-LINEAR AMPLIFIER TRANSFER CHARACTERISTIC"]
    nodeS --> nodeT["OUTPUT CONTAINS FUNDAMENTAL PLUS HARMONICS"]
    nodeT --> nodeU["FUNDAMENTAL COMPONENT f"]
    nodeT --> nodeV["2nd HARMONIC 2f"]
    nodeT --> nodeW["3rd HARMONIC 3f"]
    nodeT --> nodeX["HIGHER HARMONICS 4f 5f etc"]

    nodeU --> nodeY["THD CALCULATION METER"]
    nodeV --> nodeY
    nodeW --> nodeY
    nodeX --> nodeY
    nodeY --> nodeZ["THD PERCENT = sqrt V2^2 plus V3^2 plus ... divided by V1 times 100"]

    nodeR --> nodeAA["LINEAR AMPLIFIER IDEAL NO DISTORTION"]
    nodeAA --> nodeAB["PURE SINE OUTPUT NO HARMONICS"]
```

## 4.4 Cause-Effect Matrix: Distortion Sources

| Distortion Type | Primary Cause | Frequency Generation | Remediation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Amplitude (Harmonic)** | Transistor non-linearity | New frequencies (harmonics) appear | Operate in linear region; use negative feedback; use push-pull configuration |
| **Frequency (Linear)** | Reactive components (C, L) | No new frequencies; amplitude varies with $f$ | Use appropriate coupling/bypass capacitor values; design wide BW |
| **Phase** | Frequency-dependent phase shift | No new frequencies; timing altered | All-pass equalizer networks; careful stage design |
| **Intermodulation** | Two or more signals interacting | Sum and difference frequencies ($f_1 \pm f_2$, $2f_1 \pm f_2$, etc.) | High linearity devices; proper biasing |
| **Crossover** | Class-B push-pull stage | Signal distortion near zero-crossing | Use Class-AB biasing |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Frequency Response of an amplifier. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The frequency response of an amplifier is the **variation of its voltage gain magnitude (|A_v|) and phase shift (φ)** as a function of the operating signal frequency. It is typically represented as a **Bode plot** — a graph of gain in decibels (dB) versus frequency on a logarithmic scale.

A practical amplifier exhibits three regions:
1. **Low-frequency region** (where gain falls at +20 dB/decade due to coupling and bypass capacitors).
2. **Mid-frequency region** (where gain is flat and maximum — $A_{mid}$).
3. **High-frequency region** (where gain falls at −20 dB/decade due to internal transistor capacitances).

The **bandwidth** is defined as $BW = f_H - f_L$, where $f_L$ and $f_H$ are the **−3 dB cutoff frequencies**.

---

### Question 2: What is Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)? [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) is a measure of the **non-linear distortion** introduced by an amplifier. It quantifies the extent to which the output signal contains **harmonic components** (multiples of the fundamental frequency) that were not present in the input signal.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$

Where $V_1$ is the RMS voltage of the fundamental frequency, and $V_2, V_3, \ldots, V_n$ are the RMS voltages of the 2nd, 3rd, ..., nth harmonics.

For **high-fidelity (Hi-Fi) audio amplifiers**, THD should be less than **0.1%**. THD arises due to the **non-linear transfer characteristics** of active devices like BJTs, FETs, and tubes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Question:** With the help of a Bode plot, explain the **frequency response of an RC-coupled amplifier**. Discuss in detail the **low-frequency response** and **high-frequency response**, and derive the expressions for **lower cutoff frequency ($f_L$)** and **upper cutoff frequency ($f_H$)**.

#### Part (a) — Low-Frequency Response & $f_L$ Derivation [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

The low-frequency response is governed by **two coupling capacitors** ($C_C$) and the **emitter bypass capacitor** ($C_E$). At low frequencies, these capacitors exhibit significant reactance ($X_C = 1/2\pi fC$) and form **High-Pass RC filters** with the surrounding resistances.

**Equivalent circuit analysis:**

For the input coupling capacitor $C_{C1}$:

$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{C1}}$$

For the output coupling capacitor $C_{C2}$:

$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_{out} + R_L) C_{C2}}$$

For the emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$:

$$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_E \Vert R_E') C_E}$$

The **dominant** low-frequency cutoff is the highest of these three:

$$f_L \approx \max(f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{L3})$$

**Gain at low frequencies** (normalized):

$$\vert A_v(f) \vert_{low} = \frac{A_{mid}}{\sqrt{1 + (f_L / f)^2}}$$

In dB, the gain drops at **+20 dB/decade** slope as frequency decreases.

#### Part (b) — High-Frequency Response & $f_H$ Derivation [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

At high frequencies, the **internal junction capacitances** of the transistor ($C_{be}$ — base-emitter, $C_{bc}$ — base-collector) become significant. The $C_{bc}$ is small (a few pF) but is **multiplied by Miller's Theorem** to a large effective input capacitance.

**Miller's Theorem applied to $C_{bc}$:**

The base-collector voltage gain is $A_v = -g_m R_C$ (large negative value). Therefore:

$$C_{M} = C_{bc}(1 - A_v) \approx C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$

**Total input capacitance:**

$$C_{in(total)} = C_{be} + C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert) = C_{be} + C_{M}$$

**Upper cutoff frequency:**

The effective source resistance $R_{eq} = R_S \Vert R_B \Vert r_{\pi}$ forms a low-pass filter with $C_{in(total)}$:

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{in(total)}}$$

**Gain at high frequencies:**

$$\vert A_v(f) \vert_{high} = \frac{A_{mid}}{\sqrt{1 + (f / f_H)^2}}$$

This rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** as frequency increases above $f_H$.

**Bode Plot:** The complete Bode plot shows a flat mid-band response of $A_{mid}$ (in dB) between $f_L$ and $f_H$, with a low-frequency roll-off of **+20 dB/decade** and a high-frequency roll-off of **−20 dB/decade**. The bandwidth is $BW = f_H - f_L$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Question:** Explain the different **types of distortion** that occur in amplifiers. Derive the **expression for Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** and explain how **negative feedback** reduces distortion.

#### Part (a) — Types of Distortion & THD Derivation [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Types of Distortion:**

1. **Amplitude (Non-linear) Distortion:** Caused by the non-linear $V$-$I$ characteristic of active devices. New frequency components (harmonics) are generated. Measured by THD.

2. **Frequency (Linear) Distortion:** Caused by reactive components (capacitors, inductors) whose impedance varies with frequency. Different frequency components are amplified by different amounts. No new frequencies are generated.

3. **Phase Distortion:** Occurs when different frequency components of a complex signal experience different phase shifts, altering the relative timing of the harmonics and distorting the waveform shape.

**THD Derivation:**

Consider a non-linear transfer characteristic approximated by a power series:

$$v_{out} = a_0 + a_1 v_{in} + a_2 v_{in}^2 + a_3 v_{in}^3 + \cdots$$

For a sinusoidal input $v_{in} = V_m \cos(\omega t)$:

$$v_{out} = a_0 + a_1 V_m \cos(\omega t) + a_2 V_m^2 \cos^2(\omega t) + a_3 V_m^3 \cos^3(\omega t) + \cdots$$

Using the identities $\cos^2\theta = \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2}$ and $\cos^3\theta = \frac{3\cos\theta + \cos 3\theta}{4}$:

$$v_{out} = \underbrace{\left(a_0 + \frac{a_2 V_m^2}{2}\right)}_{DC} + \underbrace{\left(a_1 V_m + \frac{3a_3 V_m^3}{4}\right)}_{\text{Fundamental } \omega} + \underbrace{\frac{a_2 V_m^2}{2}}_{2\omega} \cos(2\omega t) + \underbrace{\frac{a_3 V_m^3}{4}}_{3\omega} \cos(3\omega t) + \cdots$$

Thus $V_1$ (fundamental) and $V_2, V_3, \ldots$ (harmonics) are identified, giving:

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$

#### Part (b) — Negative Feedback & Distortion Reduction [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

In a **negative feedback amplifier**, a portion of the output signal is fed back to the input **out of phase** with the input signal. The closed-loop gain is:

$$A_{CL} = \frac{A_{OL}}{1 + \beta A_{OL}}$$

Where $A_{OL}$ is the open-loop gain and $\beta$ is the feedback factor.

**Effect on Distortion:**

If the open-loop amplifier produces harmonic distortion $D_{OL}$, the distortion in the closed-loop amplifier is:

$$D_{CL} = \frac{D_{OL}}{1 + \beta A_{OL}}$$

The factor $(1 + \beta A_{OL})$ is called the **desensitivity factor** or **loop gain**. For a typical feedback amplifier with $A_{OL} = 10{,}000$ and $\beta = 0.01$, the factor is $1 + 100 = 101$. The distortion is reduced by **101 times**.

**Trade-offs of Negative Feedback:**
- **Reduces distortion** (THD reduced by loop gain factor).
- **Increases bandwidth** ($BW_{CL} = BW_{OL} \times (1 + \beta A_{OL})$).
- **Stabilizes gain** against temperature and component variations.
- **Reduces noise** generated within the amplifier.
- **Cost:** The overall gain is reduced (Gain-Bandwidth product remains constant).
- **Risk of instability** if the phase margin becomes too small at high frequencies.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 100 in THD calculation:** If the open-loop THD is 5% and the loop gain factor is 100, the closed-loop THD is 0.05%, NOT 5%. Students often divide instead of apply the desensitivity formula correctly.
> 2. **Confusing $f_L$ and $f_H$ slopes:** The low-frequency roll-off is **+20 dB/decade** (gain increases as frequency increases toward $f_L$). The high-frequency roll-off is **−20 dB/decade** (gain decreases as frequency increases above $f_H$). Many students invert these signs.
> 3. **Ignoring Miller multiplication:** Forgetting to multiply $C_{bc}$ by $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ leads to **gross overestimation of $f_H$** and an unrealistically wide bandwidth.
> 4. **Bandwidth formula misuse:** $BW = f_H - f_L$ only when $f_H \gg f_L$. When $f_L$ is comparable to $f_H$, the correct formula is $BW = f_H - f_L$ (still valid), but students sometimes write $BW = f_H + f_L$ or $BW = f_H \times f_L$ — both wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Frequency Response and Distortion**

- **Frequency Response** is the variation of magnitude and phase of amplifier gain with frequency.
- **Three regions:** Low-freq, Mid-band (flat), High-freq.
- **Cutoff frequencies ($f_L$, $f_H$):** Points where gain drops to **0.707** of mid-band value, equivalent to **−3 dB**.
- **Bandwidth:** $BW = f_H - f_L$.
- **Low-frequency roll-off slope:** **+20 dB/decade** (caused by coupling & bypass capacitors).
- **High-frequency roll-off slope:** **−20 dB/decade** (caused by Miller-multiplied junction capacitances).
- **Miller's Theorem:** $C_{M} = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ — multiplies small $C_{bc}$ to large effective input capacitance.
- **Decibel formula:** $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$.
- **Three types of distortion:** Amplitude (non-linear), Frequency (linear), Phase.
- **THD formula:** $THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$.
- **Negative feedback reduces distortion by factor $(1 + \beta A_{OL})$** — the loop gain.
- **Phase shift at cutoff frequencies** is $\pm 45^{\circ}$ (for single-pole response).
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** is constant for a given amplifier — reducing gain increases bandwidth.
- **Intermodulation distortion** generates sum and difference frequencies when two or more signals are present.
- **Crossover distortion** is specific to Class-B push-pull amplifiers, eliminated by Class-AB biasing.
- **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio):** $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{signal} / V_{noise})$.
- **Practical audio amplifier bandwidth:** **20 Hz to 20 kHz**.
- **Hi-Fi THD standard:** Less than **0.1%**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Four Basic Feedback Topologies - Voltage Gain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Four Basic Feedback Topologies - Voltage Gain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Feedback Topology?

In analog electronic amplifier design, **feedback topology** refers to the specific architectural arrangement of a feedback network relative to the basic amplifier. The classification is determined by two critical decisions: **(a) how the output signal is sampled** and **(b) how the feedback signal is mixed back with the input signal**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **feedback topology** is the complete structural configuration of an amplifier–feedback network system, uniquely defined by the *sampling method* at the output (voltage or current) and the *mixing method* at the input (series or shunt). There are exactly **four canonical feedback topologies** that exhaustively cover all linear feedback amplifier configurations.

## 1.2 The Four Basic Feedback Topologies — Master Classification

The four topologies arise from a 2 × 2 combinatorial design space:

| # | Sampling at Output | Mixing at Input | Topology Name | Alternative Name |
|---|-------------------|-----------------|---------------|------------------|
| 1 | Voltage | Series | **Voltage-Series Feedback** | Series-Shunt |
| 2 | Voltage | Shunt | **Voltage-Shunt Feedback** | Shunt-Shunt |
| 3 | Current | Series | **Current-Series Feedback** | Series-Series |
| 4 | Current | Shunt | **Current-Shunt Feedback** | Shunt-Series |

> [!NOTE]
> In the alternative naming convention: the **first term** denotes the *output port connection* (series = voltage sampling, shunt = current sampling) and the **second term** denotes the *input port connection* (series = series mixing, shunt = shunt mixing).

## 1.3 Focus of this Module — Voltage Gain Analysis

The **voltage gain** is a meaningful and directly stabilized parameter only in the **Voltage-Series (Series-Shunt)** and **Voltage-Shunt (Shunt-Shunt)** topologies, because voltage gain explicitly requires **voltage sampling at the output**. Current sampling produces a current gain, not a voltage gain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Voltage-Series Feedback is the canonical "voltage amplifier with feedback."**
> This topology is the one most commonly associated with stabilized voltage gain $A_{vf}$. The derivation in Section 3 of these notes is built entirely around this topology.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Cruise Control System in a Car**
> 
> Imagine driving a car where you want to maintain a constant speed of 60 km/h (this is your **desired output**). 
> - The **speedometer** constantly *samples* your actual speed (this is the **output sampling**). 
> - The **error detector** compares your actual speed to the desired speed and produces an error signal. 
> - The **accelerator pedal** acts based on this error to correct the speed.
> 
> In this analogy:
> - The **sampled quantity is speed (analogous to voltage)** → voltage sampling.
> - The **correction is applied additively in the control loop (series mixing)** → series mixing.
> - The result is a **stable speed (= stable voltage gain)** that is insensitive to road conditions (= insensitive to amplifier parameter variations).
> 
> Negative feedback always **sacrifices raw gain** to **gain stability, bandwidth, linearity, and predictability** — a fundamental engineering trade-off.

## 1.5 Standard KTU Reference Metrics

The following physical/symbolic quantities are used throughout this module:

- **A** = Open-loop gain (gain of the basic amplifier alone) → dimensionless ratio
- **β** = Feedback factor (gain of the feedback network) → dimensionless ratio
- **Aβ** = **Loop gain** (the product of forward and reverse path gains) → dimensionless ratio
- **(1 + Aβ)** = **Desensitivity factor** or **Return Difference** → dimensionless ratio
- **$A_f$** = Closed-loop gain (gain of the amplifier with feedback) → dimensionless ratio
- **$R_i$** = Open-loop input resistance → **Ω (ohms)**
- **$R_{if}$** = Closed-loop input resistance → **Ω (ohms)**
- **$R_o$** = Open-loop output resistance → **Ω (ohms)**
- **$R_{of}$** = Closed-loop output resistance → **Ω (ohms)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Independent Design Decisions

Every feedback topology is fully specified by answering two questions about the **two-port feedback network**:

### Decision 1: How is the output sampled?
- **Voltage Sampling (Shunt connection at output):** The feedback network is connected in **parallel (shunt)** with the output port. The sampled quantity is the **output voltage $v_o$**, and ideally the feedback network draws **zero current** from the output (infinite input impedance ideal).
- **Current Sampling (Series connection at output):** The feedback network is connected in **series** with the output port. The sampled quantity is the **output current $i_o$**, and ideally has **zero voltage drop** (zero input impedance ideal).

### Decision 2: How is the feedback signal mixed at the input?
- **Series Mixing (Series connection at input):** The feedback signal is a **voltage $v_f$** inserted in **series** with the input source. The mixing is governed by **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)**.
- **Shunt Mixing (Shunt connection at input):** The feedback signal is a **current $i_f$** injected in **parallel** with the input source. The mixing is governed by **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)**.

## 2.2 Why the Four Topologies Cover All Cases

Mathematically, the sampled quantity can be either $v_o$ or $i_o$ (2 choices) and the mixed quantity can be either voltage or current (2 choices). The 2 × 2 = 4 combinations are **mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive** — no other linear feedback topology exists.

## 2.3 Detailed Structural Description — Voltage-Series Feedback (Series-Shunt)

The **Voltage-Series Feedback** topology, which is the focus of voltage gain analysis, has the following structure:

- **Output Side:** A **voltage-sampling network** (a passive voltage divider, e.g., a resistive divider) is connected in **shunt** with the output terminals. It samples $v_o$ and produces $v_f = \beta v_o$.
- **Input Side:** The feedback voltage $v_f$ is connected in **series** with the source $v_s$ such that KVL gives the **error voltage**:
$$v_i = v_s - v_f$$
- **Forward Path:** The error voltage $v_i$ is amplified by the basic amplifier (gain $A$) to produce $v_o$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **polarity of feedback** is determined by the sign of β in the loop. In all standard amplifier analysis, we assume **negative feedback**, meaning the feedback signal **opposes** the input signal. This is essential for the desensitivity factor $(1 + A\beta)$ to be greater than unity.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Voltage Gain in Feedback Amplifiers**
> 
> Use `\vert` instead of `\vert` for absolute values to keep markdown tables valid.

| # | Quantity | Open-Loop Expression | Closed-Loop Expression (with Negative Feedback) | Topology-Specific Remarks |
|---|----------|---------------------|----------------------------------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Basic Feedback Equation | $A$ | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$ | Universal for all 4 topologies |
| 2 | Loop Gain | $A\beta$ | $\vert A\beta \vert$ (magnitude) | For negative feedback, $A\beta > 0$ |
| 3 | Desensitivity Factor | — | $D = 1 + A\beta$ | $D > 1$ for negative feedback |
| 4 | Stabilized Gain (approx) | — | $A_f \approx \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ when $A\beta \gg 1$ | The "asymptotic" feedback result |
| 5 | Voltage Gain — Voltage-Series | $A_v = \dfrac{v_o}{v_i}$ | $A_{vf} = \dfrac{v_o}{v_s} = \dfrac{A_v}{1 + \beta A_v}$ | Input $v_s$, output $v_o$, both voltages |
| 6 | Input Resistance — Voltage-Series | $R_i$ | $R_{if} = R_i \cdot (1 + A_v \beta)$ | **Increases** by factor $D$ |
| 7 | Output Resistance — Voltage-Series | $R_o$ | $R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1 + A_v \beta}$ | **Decreases** by factor $D$ |
| 8 | Voltage Gain — Voltage-Shunt | $A_v = \dfrac{v_o}{v_i}$ | $A_{vf} = \dfrac{v_o}{v_s} = \dfrac{A_v}{1 + \beta A_v}$ | Input is current, output is voltage (transresistance context) |
| 9 | Input Resistance — Voltage-Shunt | $R_i$ | $R_{if} = \dfrac{R_i}{1 + A_v \beta}$ | **Decreases** by factor $D$ |
| 10 | Output Resistance — Voltage-Shunt | $R_o$ | $R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1 + A_v \beta}$ | **Decreases** by factor $D$ |
| 11 | Sensitivity of $A_f$ to $A$ | $\dfrac{dA}{A}$ | $\dfrac{dA_f}{A_f} = \dfrac{1}{1 + A\beta} \cdot \dfrac{dA}{A}$ | Gain sensitivity reduced by $D$ |
| 12 | Bandwidth Product | $BW \cdot A$ | $BW_f \cdot A_f \approx BW \cdot A$ | **Gain-Bandwidth Product is conserved** |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Engineering:**
> - **Voltage-Series Feedback** is the workhorse of op-amp internal design (e.g., the non-inverting amplifier configuration). It is used in audio pre-amplifiers, instrumentation amplifiers, and active filters.
> - **Voltage-Shunt Feedback** forms the basis of the classic inverting op-amp configuration and is used in current-to-voltage converters (transimpedance amplifiers) for photodiode front-ends.
> - **Current-Series Feedback** is used in transconductance stages and is the topology of the common-emitter BJT amplifier with emitter degeneration.
> - **Current-Shunt Feedback** is the internal topology of feedback current amplifiers and is widely used in regulated power supplies and current-mirror circuits.

The **stabilization of voltage gain** in the voltage-series topology is what allows precision analog circuits to achieve gain accuracy better than 0.01 % using inexpensive, poorly-matched discrete components.
