**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **BCD (8421) code** and **Excess-3 code** with examples. Show that Excess-3 is a **self-complementing code**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Convert the binary number $(10110110)_2$ to **Gray code**, and then convert the resulting Gray code back to binary. Verify the round-trip.

---

#### **Solution to B(a):**

**BCD (8421) Code:** Each decimal digit (0–9) is independently represented by its 4-bit binary equivalent. The weights 8, 4, 2, 1 are assigned to the four bit positions from MSB to LSB.

**Example:** $(529)_{10} = \underbrace{0101}_{5}\;\underbrace{0010}_{2}\;\underbrace{1001}_{9}$ in BCD.

**Excess-3 Code:** Each decimal digit $d$ is represented by its BCD code + 0011 (binary 3). It is also a 4-bit code.

**Example:** For decimal 4, BCD = 0100, XS3 = 0100 + 0011 = **0111**.

**Proof that Excess-3 is self-complementing:**

The 9's complement of a decimal digit $d$ is $(9 - d)$. We need to show that the XS3 code of $(9 - d)$ is the **bitwise complement** of the XS3 code of $d$.

Let $E(d) = \text{BCD}(d) + 0011$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(9 - d) &= \text{BCD}(9 - d) + 0011 \\
&= (1001 - \text{BCD}(d)) + 0011 \quad \text{(since BCD}(9-d) = 9 - \text{BCD}(d)\text{)} \\
&= 1001 + 0011 - \text{BCD}(d) \\
&= 1100 - \text{BCD}(d) \\
&= (1111 - \text{BCD}(d)) - 0011 \\
&= \overline{\text{BCD}(d)} - 0011 \quad \text{(where } \overline{x}\text{ is bitwise NOT)} \\
&= \overline{\text{BCD}(d) + 0011} \\
&= \overline{E(d)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, **complementing a digit and then XS3-encoding gives the same result as XS3-encoding and then bit-flipping.** ∎

---

#### **Solution to B(b):**

**Step 1 — Binary → Gray:**

Given $B = (10110110)_2$, apply the rule $G_{n-1} = B_{n-1}$ and $G_i = B_{i+1} \oplus B_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_7 &= B_7 = 1 \\
G_6 &= B_7 \oplus B_6 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
G_5 &= B_6 \oplus B_5 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
G_4 &= B_5 \oplus B_4 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
G_3 &= B_4 \oplus B_3 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
G_2 &= B_3 \oplus B_2 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
G_1 &= B_2 \oplus B_1 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
G_0 &= B_1 \oplus B_0 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Gray code = $(11101101)_2$**

**Step 2 — Gray → Binary (verification):**

Apply the rule $B_{n-1} = G_{n-1}$ and $B_i = B_{i+1} \oplus G_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_7 &= G_7 = 1 \\
B_6 &= B_7 \oplus G_6 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
B_5 &= B_6 \oplus G_5 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
B_4 &= B_5 \oplus G_4 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
B_3 &= B_4 \oplus G_3 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
B_2 &= B_3 \oplus G_2 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
B_1 &= B_2 \oplus G_1 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
B_0 &= B_1 \oplus G_0 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recovered binary = $(10110110)_2$** ✓ — round-trip verified.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 – Basics):**

- **Radix** = number of unique digit symbols in a number system; determines the positional weight multiplier.
- **Number of distinct values** representable with $n$ bits = $2^n$; max unsigned value = $2^n - 1$.
- **Signed 2's complement range** with $n$ bits: $-2^{n-1}$ to $+2^{n-1} - 1$.
- **Decimal → Binary:** Repeated division by 2; remainders read bottom-up.
- **Binary → Decimal:** Weighted sum $\sum d_i \cdot 2^i$.
- **Decimal → Octal/Hex:** Repeated division by 8 or 16.
- **Decimal fraction → Binary:** Repeated multiplication by 2; integer parts read top-down.
- **Binary ↔ Octal:** group/ungroup in **triplets** from the binary point outward.
- **Binary ↔ Hex:** group/ungroup in **quartets** from the binary point outward.
- **1's complement:** flip every bit. **2's complement:** 1's complement + 1.
- **Subtraction via 2's complement:** $A - B = A + \overline{B}_{\text{2's}}$; carry-out discarded means positive result.
- **BCD (8421):** Each decimal digit is a 4-bit binary; six "forbidden" codes (1010–1111).
- **BCD addition correction:** Add $0110_2$ to any nibble sum exceeding $1001_2$ or producing a carry.
- **Excess-3:** BCD + 0011; it is **self-complementing** (9's complement emerges naturally).
- **Gray code:** Successive codes differ in **exactly one bit**; critical for K-maps and rotary encoders.
- **Binary → Gray:** $G_{n-1} = B_{n-1}$; $G_i = B_{i+1} \oplus B_i$.
- **Gray → Binary:** $B_{n-1} = G_{n-1}$; $B_i = B_{i+1} \oplus G_i$.
- **ASCII** is a 7-bit alphanumeric code; 8-bit variants (extended ASCII) are common.
- **8 bits = 1 byte = 2 hex digits = max value 255 (unsigned) or range -128 to +127 (signed).**
- **Bit width required** to represent a maximum unsigned value $N_{\max}$: $\lceil \log_2(N_{\max}+1) \rceil$ bits.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram of an Op Amp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCPT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Block Diagram of an Op Amp ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition The **Block Diagram of an Operational Amplifier (Op Amp)** is a functional architectural representation that decomposes the monolithic integrated op-amp into a cascade of four distinct internal stages, each performing a specialized signal-processing function. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBCPT304)**, this block-level model is essential because it allows the student to abstract away the complex transistor-level circuitry and focus on the *signal-flow behavior* of the device. The canonical block diagram, universally adopted in KTU textbook references (Boylestad, Gayakwad, Roy Choudhury), consists of: 1. **Input Stage** — Dual Input, Balanced Output Differential Amplifier (DIBO) 2. **Gain Stage** — Dual Input, Unbalanced Output Differential Amplifier (DIUO) 3. **Output / Driver Stage** — Push-Pull Complementary Symmetry Amplifier 4. **Biasing Circuit** — Current Source / Current Mirror (sets the quiescent operating point) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 emphasizes that the op-amp is *not* a single amplifier but a *cascade of specialized sub-amplifiers*. For examination purposes, students must identify each block, its function, and the signal type at its interface (balanced vs. unbalanced). ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **public-address (PA) system in a large auditorium**. The microphone (input) is small and produces a tiny, weak, noisy electrical signal. Before this signal can drive the loudspeaker, it must pass through: - A **pre-amplifier** (boosts the weak signal and rejects hum — *Differential Amp*), - A **power amplifier** (further amplifies the voltage — *Gain Stage*), - A **loudspeaker driver** (delivers high current to the speaker — *Push-Pull Stage*), - And a **DC power supply with regulation** (provides stable operating energy — *Bias Circuit*). The op-amp is exactly this — a *miniature, integrated version* of the PA chain, fabricated on a single silicon chip. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Operating Voltages:** Typical op-amps are powered by dual supplies: **+VCC = +15 V** and **–VEE = –15 V** (symmetric bipolar), though **±5 V to ±18 V** ranges are common in KTU laboratory contexts. ### Why Use a Cascaded Block Structure? - Each stage is *independently optimized* for one job (gain, level shifting, current driving). - The cascade yields **enormous overall gain** (open-loop A_vd ≈ **2 × 10⁵**) without instability. - Balanced-differential signaling at the input rejects common-mode noise — a property exploited in instrumentation. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### The Four Blocks — Operational Logic Steps #### **Block 1: Input Stage — Dual Input Balanced Output Differential Amplifier** - **Function:** Accepts the two input signals V₁ and V₂, amplifies their *difference*, and rejects common-mode noise. - **Configuration:** Usually an emitter-coupled (BJT) or source-coupled (FET) differential pair with an active current-mirror load. - **Why differential?** Real-world sensors produce signals embedded in noise; the differential input extracts V_id = V₁ – V₂ while suppressing any voltage common to both inputs. - **Key feature:** Provides **high input impedance (Z_in ≈ 10⁶ Ω to 10¹² Ω)** and high **CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio ≈ 90 dB)**. #### **Block 2: Gain Stage — Dual Input Unbalanced Output Amplifier** - **Function:** Performs the bulk of the **voltage amplification**. Typically a common-emitter (CE) stage with an active load. - **Why unbalanced output?** It is easier to feed a single-ended signal to the output stage. - **Provides:** Most of the **open-loop gain** (≈ 1000× to 10,000× per stage). #### **Block 3: Output Stage — Push-Pull Complementary Symmetry Amplifier** - **Function:** Delivers high *current* to the load with **low output impedance (Z_out ≈ 10 to 100 Ω)**. - **Topology:** Class-AB push-pull using complementary n-p-n and p-n-p (or NMOS/PMOS) transistors. - **Why push-pull?** It can source current during positive half-cycles and sink current during negative half-cycles, achieving a near rail-to-rail swing. #### **Block 4: Biasing Circuit — Current Mirror / Constant Current Source** - **Function:** Provides stable, temperature-compensated bias currents to all three amplifying stages. - **Why needed?** Sets the Q-point so that transistors operate in the active region, ensuring linear amplification. ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Stage | Unit | |-----------|--------|---------------|-------|------| | Open-loop differential voltage gain | $A_{vd}$ | $\approx 2 \times 10^{5}$ | Overall | V/V | | Input impedance | $Z_{in}$ | $10^{6}$ to $10^{12}$ | Input Stage | $\Omega$ | | Output impedance | $Z_{out}$ | $10$ to $100$ | Output Stage | $\Omega$ | | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | CMRR | $\approx 90$ | Input Stage | dB | | Slew Rate | SR | $\approx 0.5$ | Overall | V/$\mu$s | | Gain-Bandwidth Product | GBW | $\approx 1$ | Overall | MHz | | Differential input voltage | $V_{id}$ | $V_1 - V_2$ | Input Stage | V | | Common-mode input voltage | $V_{ic}$ | $(V_1 + V_2)/2$ | Input Stage | V | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** This four-block cascade architecture is the *universal template* for virtually every modern op-amp IC — from the classic $\mu$A741 to precision instrumentation amplifiers (e.g., AD620, INA128) used in biomedical ECG/EEG front-ends, industrial strain-gauge bridges, and audio mixing consoles. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Derivation: Overall Open-Loop Gain of the Cascaded Op-Amp Since the four stages are connected in **cascade** (output of one feeds the input of the next), and each stage is assumed to have a sufficiently high input impedance that loading effects are negligible, the overall voltage gain is simply the **product** of the individual stage gains. Let the gains of the four stages be denoted as follows: - $A_1$ = Voltage gain of the **Input Stage** (Dual Input, Balanced Output) - $A_2$ = Voltage gain of the **Gain Stage** (Dual Input, Unbalanced Output) - $A_3$ = Voltage gain of the **Output Stage** (Push-Pull Amplifier, typically $\approx 1$ for voltage, but high for current) - $A_4$ = (Optional) Gain contribution of the **Level Shifter / Bias Network** **Step 1 — Output of the Input Stage:** The differential input signal is $V_{id} = V_1 - V_2$. After passing through the input stage, the balanced output voltage is: $$ V_{o1} = A_1 \cdot (V_1 - V_2) = A_1 \cdot V_{id} $$ **Step 2 — Output of the Gain Stage:** The signal $V_{o1}$ is fed to the gain stage. Its output (now unbalanced, single-ended) is: $$ V_{o2} = A_2 \cdot V_{o1} = A_2 \cdot A_1 \cdot V_{id} $$ **Step 3 — Output of the Push-Pull Stage:** The push-pull stage is primarily a *current* amplifier. Its small-signal voltage gain is approximately unity ($A_3 \approx 1$), but it provides the necessary *current-handling* and *low output impedance*. Therefore: $$ V_{o} = A_3 \cdot V_{o2} \approx A_3 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_1 \cdot V_{id} $$ **Step 4 — Overall Open-Loop Differential Gain:** By combining all stages, the **overall open-loop differential voltage gain** of the op-amp is: $$ A_{OL} = \frac{V_{o}}{V_{id}} = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 $$ In dB form: $$ A_{OL}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3) $$ **Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):** Suppose for an op-amp, the input stage has $A_1 = 10$, the gain stage has $A_2 = 1000$, and the output stage has $A_3 = 1$. Then: $$ A_{OL} = 10 \times 1000 \times 1 = 10{,}000 $$ In decibels: $$ A_{OL}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(10{,}000) = 20 \times 4 = 80 \text{ dB} $$ For the typical commercial op-amp 741, $A_{OL} \approx 2 \times 10^{5}$, which gives: $$ A_{OL}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(2 \times 10^{5}) \approx 106 \text{ dB} $$ ### Analytical Matrix — Role of Each Stage | Block | Stage Name | Signal I/O Type | Primary Function | Typical Gain | Output Form | |-------|------------|------------------|------------------|--------------|--------------| | 1 | Input Stage | Dual-In, Balanced-Out | Differential amplification + CMRR | $\approx 10$ | Two opposite-phase signals | | 2 | Gain Stage | Dual-In, Unbalanced-Out | Bulk voltage amplification | $\approx 1000$ | Single-ended signal | | 3 | Output Stage | Single-In, Single-Out | Current drive, low Z_out | $\approx 1$ (V), high (I) | Rail-to-rail swing | | 4 | Bias Circuit | DC only | Set Q-points | N/A | Stable bias currents | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid) The following Mermaid diagram renders the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a generic integrated op-amp, showing signal flow from differential input to single-ended output, including the biasing subsystem. ```mermaid graph TD A[INPUT STAGE
Dual Input Balanced Output
Differential Amplifier] --> B[GAIN STAGE
Dual Input Unbalanced Output
CE Amplifier with Active Load] B --> C[LEVEL SHIFTER
DC Restoration
Optional in Some Designs] C --> D[OUTPUT STAGE
Push Pull Complementary
Class AB Amplifier] D --> E[OUTPUT TERMINAL Vo
Single Ended Low Impedance] F[BIASING CIRCUIT
Current Mirror Reference] --> A F --> B F --> D G[INPUT TERMINAL V1
Non Inverting] --> A H[INPUT TERMINAL V2
Inverting] --> A ``` ### Annotated Block Diagram (Textual Schematic) ``` +VCC | | +----------+----------+ | | | BIASING | | CIRCUIT | | (Current | | Mirror) | | | +-----+----------+----+ | | | | (Bias currents to all stages) v v V1 ----> [INPUT STAGE]----[GAIN STAGE]----[OUTPUT STAGE]----> Vo V2 ----> (DIBO) (DIUO) (Push-Pull) | | | +----+ +---------+ | | | | v v v -VEE -VEE ``` ### Functional Description of Each Block (For KTU Diagram Labeling) | Block Label | KTU-Accepted Name | Function | |-------------|--------------------|----------| | B1 | Dual-Input Balanced-Output Differential Amplifier | Provides differential gain, high Z_in, CMRR | | B2 | Dual-Input Unbalanced-Output Amplifier | Provides large voltage gain | | B3 | Push-Pull Complementary Output Amplifier | Provides current gain, low Z_out | | B4 | Biasing Circuit (Current Source) | Sets stable Q-point currents | --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** List the four main building blocks of an op-amp and state the function of each in one sentence. **Model Answer:** | Block | Name | Function | |-------|------|----------| | 1 | Input Stage (DIBO Differential Amp) | Provides differential gain and high input impedance; rejects common-mode signals | | 2 | Gain Stage (DIUO Amplifier) | Provides the bulk of the voltage amplification | | 3 | Output Stage (Push-Pull Amplifier) | Delivers high current to load with low output impedance | | 4 | Biasing Circuit (Current Mirror) | Provides stable DC bias current to all stages | --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Why is the input stage of an op-amp made differential rather than single-ended? **Model Answer:** A differential input stage provides two critical advantages: (i) It amplifies the *difference* $V_{id} = V_1 - V_2$ between the two input signals while **rejecting common-mode noise** (quantified by CMRR ≈ 90 dB). (ii) It provides a very **high input impedance** (typically $10^{6}$ to $10^{12} \, \Omega$), ensuring the op-amp does not load the source. These properties make differential input essential for **instrumentation, sensor signal conditioning, and noise-immune amplification**. --- ### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **(a)** Draw the block diagram of an op-amp and explain the function of each block. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for the overall open-loop voltage gain of the cascaded op-amp. If $A_1 = 20$, $A_2 = 1500$, and $A_3 = 1$, calculate the overall gain in dB. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** Refer to the block diagram shown in **Section 4** of these notes. The four blocks are: Input Stage (DIBO), Gain Stage (DIUO), Output Stage (Push-Pull), and Biasing Circuit. Each block's function is summarized in the table provided. **(b)** Since the three amplifying stages are in cascade with negligible loading: $$ A_{OL} = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 $$ Substituting $A_1 = 20$, $A_2 = 1500$, $A_3 = 1$: $$ A_{OL} = 20 \times 1500 \times 1 = 30{,}000 $$ Converting to dB: $$ A_{OL}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(30{,}000) = 20 \times (\log_{10} 3 + 4) = 20 \times 4.477 = 89.54 \text{ dB} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** *(Internal Choice Alternative)* **(a)** Differentiate between balanced-output and unbalanced-output stages. Which stage in the op-amp uses each configuration and why? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain why a push-pull complementary output stage is preferred over a single-transistor emitter-follower. Calculate the overall gain if three identical stages each of gain 50 are cascaded. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** - **Balanced Output:** Two signals of equal amplitude but opposite phase appear at the output terminals. Used in the **Input Stage** to feed the next stage differentially, preserving noise immunity. - **Unbalanced Output:** A single signal appears between one output terminal and ground. Used in the **Gain Stage** and **Output Stage** because single-ended signals are easier to couple to the load and to subsequent circuitry. **(b)** A push-pull complementary stage uses both n-p-n (sources current) and p-n-p (sinks current) transistors in a Class-AB configuration. This provides: - **Bidirectional current drive** — can source or sink load current. - **Lower crossover distortion** than Class-B. - **Higher efficiency** than Class-A single-transistor emitter follower. **Gain Calculation (three identical stages, $A_1 = A_2 = A_3 = 50$):** $$ A_{OL} = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 = 50 \times 50 \times 50 = 125{,}000 $$ In dB: $$ A_{OL}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(125{,}000) = 20 \times 5.0969 \approx 101.94 \text{ dB} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing "balanced" with "symmetric"** — Balanced means *two anti-phase signals*, not symmetric geometry. > 2. **Forgetting the biasing block** — Many students draw only three blocks. The bias circuit carries **2 marks** by itself in long-answer questions. > 3. **Not stating CMRR / Z_in values** — Examiners reward *quantitative* answers. Always cite typical values (e.g., CMRR ≈ 90 dB, $Z_{in} \approx 2 \times 10^{6} \, \Omega$ for the 741). > 4. **Gain calculation errors** — Remember the cascade rule: gains *multiply* in linear scale but *add in dB only if they were first converted to dB individually* — otherwise the formula $20\log_{10}(A_1 A_2 A_3)$ must be used directly. > 5. **Skipping the level-shifter mention** — Some op-amps (e.g., 741) include an internal level-shifter between the gain and output stages to restore the DC baseline. Mentioning this can earn bonus marks. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** - The op-amp is a **cascade of four functional blocks**: Input Stage, Gain Stage, Output Stage, and Biasing Circuit. - The **Input Stage** is a **Dual-Input Balanced-Output (DIBO)** differential amplifier — provides high $Z_{in}$, high CMRR, and moderate gain. - The **Gain Stage** is a **Dual-Input Unbalanced-Output (DIUO)** amplifier — provides the bulk of the voltage gain. - The **Output Stage** is a **Push-Pull Complementary** amplifier (Class-AB) — provides current drive and low $Z_{out}$. - The **Biasing Circuit** (current mirror) sets stable Q-point currents for all stages. - The **overall open-loop gain** is the product: $A_{OL} = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3$. - For a typical 741-class op-amp: $A_{OL} \approx 2 \times 10^{5}$ (≈ 106 dB), $Z_{in} \approx 2 \, M\Omega$, $Z_{out} \approx 75 \, \Omega$, CMRR ≈ 90 dB. - **Balanced vs Unbalanced:** Balanced = two anti-phase outputs; Unbalanced = single-ended output w.r.t. ground. - The push-pull output stage uses complementary n-p-n and p-n-p transistors to source/sink current efficiently with low crossover distortion. - Always convert gain to **dB** using $20\log_{10}(A)$ for voltage gains in KTU exam answers. ---

topics.title: Op Amp Parameters - Slew Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Op Amp Parameters - Slew Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slew Rate (SR)** of an operational amplifier is formally defined as the **maximum rate of change of the output voltage with respect to time**, when the input is driven by a large step or high-frequency sinusoidal signal such that the internal current sourcing/sinking capability becomes the limiting factor.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$SR = \left.\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{max}$$

The standard engineering unit of slew rate is **Volts per microsecond (V/μs)**. For the popular general-purpose op-amp **μA741**, the typical slew rate is approximately **0.5 V/μs**, while high-speed op-amps like the **LM318** offer slew rates in the range of **50 to 70 V/μs**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Slew rate is a *dynamic (large-signal) parameter* of the op-amp. It differs from the *small-signal parameters* (like bandwidth or gain-bandwidth product) because it depends on the **non-linear charging behavior** of the internal frequency-compensation capacitor rather than the small-signal linear gain.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. The car has a **top speed** of, say, **150 km/h**. Even if the road is perfectly straight and flat, the car cannot accelerate beyond this top speed — no matter how hard you press the accelerator pedal. The engine has a physical limit.

In this analogy:
- **The car's velocity** corresponds to **dVo/dt** (rate of change of output voltage).
- **The car's top speed (150 km/h)** corresponds to the **slew rate** of the op-amp.
- **The road (straight, flat, no obstacles)** represents an ideal input that *requests* faster change.
- **The engine's mechanical limit** corresponds to the **internal current limit** charging the compensation capacitor.

So, **slew rate is the op-amp's "speed limit" for its output voltage**. If the input signal demands that the output change *faster* than the slew rate allows, the op-amp simply *cannot keep up*, and the output waveform becomes **distorted** (triangular instead of sinusoidal). This specific distortion is called **Slew-Rate Induced Distortion** or **Slewing Distortion**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why it matters in real engineering:** Slew rate is critical in **audio amplifiers** (to avoid distortion in high-frequency music), **video amplifiers**, **high-speed data acquisition systems**, and **pulse amplifiers** (to preserve sharp edges of square waves).

---

## 1.3 Physical Origin of Slew Rate (Where does it come from?)

The op-amp contains an internal **frequency-compensation capacitor** (typically around **30 pF** in the μA741), placed intentionally to ensure stability (prevent oscillations). This capacitor is charged and discharged by a **limited internal current source** (the bias current of the input differential stage, typically a few microamperes).

Since the voltage across a capacitor cannot change instantaneously (it is governed by $i = C \cdot dV/dt$), the **maximum current available** to charge this capacitor dictates the **maximum rate of voltage change** at the output.

This is the **physical root cause** of the slew rate limit.

---

## 1.4 The "Why" — Why is Slew Rate Important?

| Scenario | Consequence of Low Slew Rate |
|---|---|
| High-frequency sine wave at the input | Output becomes a **triangle wave** (distortion) |
| Large-amplitude high-frequency signal | Output is **clipped** at the slew-limited slopes |
| Square wave input | Output edges become **ramped/triangular** |
| Audio amplifier | **High-frequency audio distortion** (loss of treble fidelity) |
| Video signal | Loss of **sharp transitions**, blurred edges |

> [!WARNING]
> Slew-rate-induced distortion is a **non-linear distortion**, meaning it cannot be corrected by simple filtering. It is a hard physical limitation of the device.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Mechanism (Step-by-Step Internal View)

Consider a typical two-stage op-amp (like the μA741). The architecture is:

**Stage 1:** High-gain differential amplifier (with active current-mirror load).
**Stage 2:** Class-AB (or Class-A) output gain stage.
**Compensation Capacitor (Cc):** Connected between the output of Stage 1 and the output of Stage 2, or in a Miller configuration.

### The Internal Current Limit

The differential input stage has a **tail current** $I_{tail}$ (set by a current mirror). When the input differential voltage is large, this entire tail current is steered to one side of the differential pair.

The maximum current that can be delivered to charge/discharge $C_c$ is therefore limited to $\pm I_{tail}$ (or some multiple, depending on the gain stage). The maximum rate of change of voltage across $C_c$ is:

$$\left.\frac{dV_c}{dt}\right|_{max} = \frac{I_{tail}}{C_c}$$

Since the output stage is a unity-gain buffer (or high-gain amplifier) that follows this internal node voltage, the slew rate at the output is:

$$SR = \frac{I_{max}}{C_c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For the **μA741**: $I_{tail} \approx 20 \mu A$ and $C_c \approx 30 pF$, giving $SR \approx 0.67 V/\mu s$ (matching the datasheet's typical value of 0.5 V/μs).

## 2.2 Slew Rate for Sinusoidal Input — The Critical Derivation

This is the **most important KTU derivation** for slew rate. Suppose the op-amp output is a pure sine wave:

$$V_o(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

The rate of change of output voltage is:

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = V_m \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

The **maximum value** of $dV_o/dt$ occurs when $\cos(\omega t) = 1$, i.e., at the **zero crossings** of the sine wave:

$$\left.\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{max} = \omega V_m = 2\pi f V_m$$

For **no slew-induced distortion**, the op-amp's slew rate must be **greater than or equal to** this maximum demanded rate:

$$SR \geq 2\pi f V_m$$

The **maximum usable frequency** for a given output amplitude (and a given op-amp slew rate) is therefore:

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_m}$$

## 2.3 Full-Power Bandwidth (FPB)

The **Full-Power Bandwidth (FPB)** is defined as the maximum frequency at which the op-amp can deliver a sinusoidal output of **maximum rated amplitude** (i.e., the maximum unclipped output swing, $V_{max}$) without slew-rate distortion.

$$FPB = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_{max}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Full-Power Bandwidth is **always less than or equal to** the small-signal bandwidth (closed-loop bandwidth, $f_{-3dB}$). At frequencies above FPB, the output is slew-limited even for small signals, because the op-amp simply cannot change its output fast enough.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Notes** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Slew Rate Definition | $SR = (dV_o/dt)_{max}$ | V/μs or V/s | Maximum rate of output voltage change |
| 2 | Internal Origin | $SR = I_{max} / C_c$ | V/s | $I_{max}$ = max internal current; $C_c$ = compensation capacitor |
| 3 | Sine Wave Requirement | $SR \geq 2\pi f V_m$ | V/s | Condition to avoid slew distortion |
| 4 | Max Frequency (Sine) | $f_{max} = SR / (2\pi V_m)$ | Hz | Highest undistorted sine frequency for amplitude $V_m$ |
| 5 | Full-Power Bandwidth | $FPB = SR / (2\pi V_{max})$ | Hz | Maximum frequency at maximum amplitude $V_{max}$ |
| 6 | Slew Rate in V/μs | $SR (\text{V/}\mu s) = (2\pi f V_m) / 10^6$ | V/μs | When f in Hz and $V_m$ in Volts |
| 7 | Rise Time (Slew Limited) | $t_r = \Delta V / SR$ | seconds | Time to swing $\Delta V$ at slew rate limit |
| 8 | Condition for Linear Operation | $2\pi f V_m \leq SR$ | — | Must hold for sinusoidal signals |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** These formulas are used in designing **active filters**, **oscillators**, **data converters (ADC/DAC drivers)**, and **sample-and-hold circuits**, where signal integrity at high frequencies is paramount.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Slew Rate Matters |
|:---|:---|
| **Hi-Fi Audio Amplifiers** | SR ≥ 13 V/μs for 20 kHz @ 100 V peak (high-voltage audio) |
| **Medical ECG/EEG Amplifiers** | Fast QRS complexes need sufficient SR to avoid distortion |
| **Oscilloscope Front-Ends** | Wide-bandwidth vertical amplifiers need SR > 1000 V/μs |
| **Function Generators** | Output op-amps must slew fast enough for triangular/square waves |
| **Switch-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS)** | Op-amp error amplifiers need fast SR for loop response |
| **Active Filters (high Q)** | Q-peaked signals can exceed op-amp's internal rate limits |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Slew Rate for Sinusoidal Output

### Given
- Op-amp output: $V_o(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$
- Op-amp slew rate: $SR$ (a fixed device parameter)
- Input signal frequency: $f$
- Input signal angular frequency: $\omega = 2\pi f$

### Goal
Find the condition that the op-amp must satisfy to reproduce this sine wave **without slew-rate distortion**.

---

### Step 1: Write the output voltage expression
$$V_o(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 2: Differentiate the output with respect to time
We apply the chain rule. The derivative of $\sin(\omega t)$ is $\omega \cos(\omega t)$:

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left[V_m \sin(\omega t)\right] = V_m \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left[\sin(\omega t)\right]$$

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = V_m \cdot \omega \cos(\omega t) = \omega V_m \cos(\omega t)$$

### Step 3: Identify the maximum value
The function $\cos(\omega t)$ oscillates between **+1 and −1**. Therefore, the **maximum magnitude** of $dV_o/dt$ occurs when $\cos(\omega t) = \pm 1$:

$$\left|\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{max} = \omega V_m \cdot 1 = \omega V_m$$

### Step 4: Substitute $\omega = 2\pi f$
$$\left|\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi f V_m$$

### Step 5: Apply the slew rate constraint
The op-amp can change its output at a rate **no faster than** $SR$. Therefore, for the output to faithfully follow the input sine wave (no slew distortion):

$$SR \geq \left|\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{max}$$

$$\boxed{SR \geq 2\pi f V_m}$$

### Step 6: Solve for the maximum allowable frequency
Rearranging the inequality (taking the equality case for the *critical* limit):

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_m}$$

### Step 7: Express in convenient V/μs units
Since $SR$ is typically given in V/μs and $f$ in Hz, we can rewrite:

$$SR \, (\text{in V/}\mu s) = \frac{2\pi f V_m}{10^6}$$

> **Logic note for Step 7:** The factor of $10^6$ converts V/s to V/μs (since 1 μs = $10^{-6}$ s, dividing by $10^6$ is the same as multiplying the rate by the duration of 1 microsecond). Equivalently, $f \cdot V_m$ in Hz·V, multiplied by $2\pi \approx 6.283$, and the result divided by $10^6$ yields V/μs.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example #1 — Maximum Frequency for a Sine Wave

**Problem:** An op-amp has a slew rate of **0.5 V/μs**. Find the maximum frequency at which it can amplify a sine wave of peak amplitude **10 V** without slew distortion.

**Solution:**

### Step 1: Identify the given values
- $SR = 0.5 \, \text{V/}\mu s = 0.5 \times 10^6 \, \text{V/s} = 5 \times 10^5 \, \text{V/s}$
- $V_m = 10 \, \text{V}$

### Step 2: Use the formula for $f_{max}$
$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_m}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the values
$$f_{max} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{2\pi \times 10} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{62.832}$$

### Step 4: Evaluate
$$f_{max} = \frac{500000}{62.832} \approx 7957.7 \, \text{Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_{max} \approx 7.96 \, \text{kHz}}$$

> **Interpretation:** Above ~7.96 kHz, the op-amp's output will become slew-rate limited, and the 10 V sine wave will appear as a **triangular-like waveform** (the peaks will be flattened and the slopes will be linear at $\pm SR$).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example #2 — Finding the Required Slew Rate

**Problem:** An audio power amplifier must reproduce a sine wave of peak amplitude **15 V** at a maximum frequency of **20 kHz** faithfully. Find the **minimum required slew rate** of the op-amp.

**Solution:**

### Step 1: Identify the given values
- $V_m = 15 \, \text{V}$
- $f = 20 \, \text{kHz} = 20{,}000 \, \text{Hz}$

### Step 2: Use the critical slew rate equation
$$SR = 2\pi f V_m$$

### Step 3: Substitute
$$SR = 2 \times \pi \times 20000 \times 15$$

### Step 4: Evaluate
$$SR = 2 \times 3.14159 \times 20000 \times 15 = 1884955.6 \, \text{V/s}$$

### Step 5: Convert to V/μs
$$SR = \frac{1884955.6}{10^6} \approx 1.885 \, \text{V/}\mu s$$

$$\boxed{SR_{min} \approx 1.89 \, \text{V/}\mu s}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** A standard μA741 (SR = 0.5 V/μs) is **inadequate** for this application. A faster op-amp like the **LF353 (SR = 13 V/μs)** or **TL081 (SR = 16 V/μs)** would be required.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example #3 — Rise Time for a Step Input

**Problem:** The op-amp output must swing from **−5 V to +5 V** (a total change of 10 V) in response to a step input. The op-amp's slew rate is **2 V/μs**. Find the **minimum rise time**.

**Solution:**

### Step 1: Identify the values
- $\Delta V = V_{final} - V_{initial} = 5 - (-5) = 10 \, \text{V}$
- $SR = 2 \, \text{V/}\mu s$

### Step 2: Use the rise-time formula
$$t_r = \frac{\Delta V}{SR}$$

### Step 3: Substitute
$$t_r = \frac{10 \, \text{V}}{2 \, \text{V/}\mu s} = 5 \, \mu s$$

$$\boxed{t_r = 5 \, \mu s}$$

> **Physical meaning:** Even with an ideal instantaneous step input, the op-amp output cannot rise faster than its slew rate allows. The output will ramp linearly at 2 V/μs, taking 5 μs to complete the 10 V transition.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example #4 — Full-Power Bandwidth

**Problem:** The op-amp μA741 has $SR = 0.5 \, V/\mu s$ and a maximum output swing of $\pm 13 \, V$. Find its Full-Power Bandwidth.

**Solution:**

### Step 1: Identify the values
- $SR = 0.5 \times 10^6 \, \text{V/s}$
- $V_{max} = 13 \, \text{V}$ (peak)

### Step 2: Apply the FPB formula
$$FPB = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_{max}}$$

### Step 3: Substitute
$$FPB = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 13} = \frac{500000}{81.68}$$

### Step 4: Evaluate
$$FPB \approx 6120.6 \, \text{Hz} \approx 6.12 \, \text{kHz}$$

$$\boxed{FPB \approx 6.12 \, \text{kHz}}$$

> **Critical Insight:** The μA741's small-signal bandwidth is about **1 MHz** at unity gain, but its **full-power bandwidth is only ~6 kHz**. This means that while the op-amp *can* amplify small signals up to 1 MHz, it can only deliver a $\pm 13 \, V$ sine wave faithfully up to 6 kHz. This is a classic KTU viva question.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Worked Examples

| **Example** | **Given** | **Find** | **Formula Used** | **Result** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | SR = 0.5 V/μs, $V_m$ = 10 V | $f_{max}$ | $f_{max} = SR/(2\pi V_m)$ | ~7.96 kHz |
| 2 | $V_m$ = 15 V, f = 20 kHz | $SR_{min}$ | $SR = 2\pi f V_m$ | ~1.89 V/μs |
| 3 | $\Delta V$ = 10 V, SR = 2 V/μs | $t_r$ | $t_r = \Delta V / SR$ | 5 μs |
| 4 | SR = 0.5 V/μs, $V_{max}$ = 13 V | FPB | $FPB = SR/(2\pi V_{max})$ | ~6.12 kHz |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Internal Origin of Slew Rate in a Two-Stage Op-Amp

This Mermaid diagram illustrates the internal architecture of a typical op-amp (like the μA741) and shows how the limited internal current charging the compensation capacitor produces the slew rate.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INP[Input Vin+] --> DA1[Differential Amplifier Stage 1]
    INN[Input Vin-] --> DA1
    DA1 --> CP[Compensation Capacitor Cc]
    CS[Current Source Itail Limits Charging Current] -.-> CP
    CP --> GS2[Gain Stage 2]
    GS2 --> OUT[Output Vout]
    SRNODE{Slew Rate Limit SR Imax per Cc} -.-> CP
    SLOPE[Output dV/dt max equals SR] -.-> OUT
    DA1 --> CS
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The **Differential Amplifier Stage 1** receives the two inputs (Vin+ and Vin−).
> - The **Current Source ($I_{tail}$)** limits the current available to charge the **Compensation Capacitor $C_c$**.
> - The **Gain Stage 2** buffers the voltage across $C_c$ to the output.
> - The dashed arrows indicate the *constraint* relationship: the slew rate is determined by the ratio of maximum internal current to $C_c$.

---

## 4.2 Functional Flow: From Input Sine to Slew-Limited Output

This diagram shows the signal flow and how distortion arises when the demanded rate exceeds the slew rate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SINE[Input Sine Wave Frequency f Amplitude Vm] --> DEMAND[Compute Max dV per dt Required Rate equals 2 pi f Vm]
    DEMAND --> COMPARE{Is Required Rate less than or equal to Op Amp SR}
    COMPARE -- YES --> FAITH[Faithful Sine Output at Vout]
    COMPARE -- NO --> SLEW[Slew Limited Output Triangular Shape Peaks Flattened]
    FAITH --> OK[No Distortion]
    SLEW --> WARN[Non Linear Distortion]
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> - At **low frequencies** or **small amplitudes**, the demanded rate ($2\pi f V_m$) is well within the op-amp's slew rate, producing a **clean sine wave**.
> - At **high frequencies** or **large amplitudes**, the demanded rate exceeds SR, and the output is **slew-limited** (a triangular-ish wave with flattened peaks).

---

## 4.3 Input vs Output Waveform Comparison (Descriptive Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[At Low Frequency or Small Amplitude]
        I1[Vin: Pure Sine Wave] --> O1[Vout: Faithful Sine Wave]
    end
    subgraph B[At High Frequency or Large Amplitude SR Violation]
        I2[Vin: Pure Sine Wave] --> O2[Vout: Distorted Triangular Wave]
    end
```

> **Interpretation:** When the op-amp is operating **within** its slew rate limit, the output faithfully reproduces the input. When the slew rate is **violated**, the output is distorted — the rising and falling portions become linear ramps (at slope = ±SR) instead of the smooth sinusoid, and the peaks are clipped.

---

## 4.4 Slew Rate Constraint Trade-Off Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pick an Op Amp] --> Q1{High Frequency Required}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Large Amplitude Required}
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3{Need High Slew Rate}
    Q3 -- YES --> FAST[Choose High SR Op Amp e.g. LM318 or LF353]
    Q2 -- NO --> LOWF[Standard Op Amp e.g. muA741 May Suffice]
    Q1 -- NO --> STD[Standard Op Amp Acceptable]
    Q3 -- NO --> CALC[Calculate SR equals 2 pi f Vm then Choose Op Amp]
```

> **Design insight:** This flow chart guides a designer's decision-making: high-frequency *and* large-amplitude requirements demand a high-slew-rate op-amp. If the signal is low-frequency or low-amplitude, a general-purpose op-amp suffices.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define slew rate of an operational amplifier. State its unit and the typical value for the μA741.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — Distribution: 1+1+1):

**Definition (1 Mark):**
> The slew rate of an op-amp is defined as the **maximum rate of change of output voltage with respect to time** under large-signal conditions.
> $$SR = \left(\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right)_{max}$$

**Unit (1 Mark):**
> The unit of slew rate is **Volts per microsecond (V/μs)**. It can also be expressed in V/s.

**Typical Value (1 Mark):**
> For the popular general-purpose op-amp **μA741**, the typical slew rate is **0.5 V/μs**.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**What is the physical cause of slew rate in an op-amp?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

The physical cause of slew rate is the **limited internal current available to charge and discharge the internal frequency-compensation capacitor** ($C_c$, typically 30 pF) in the op-amp.

Since the voltage across a capacitor cannot change instantaneously and is governed by the equation:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{I}{C}$$

the **maximum rate of voltage change** at the output is limited by the **maximum internal bias current** ($I_{tail}$) divided by the compensation capacitor value. This gives the practical slew rate expression:

$$SR = \frac{I_{max}}{C_c}$$

When the input signal demands a faster rate of change, the capacitor cannot charge/discharge any faster, and the output is slew-limited.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 2]` — **14 Marks**

**a)** Derive the expression for the slew rate requirement when an op-amp is used to amplify a sinusoidal signal. **[7 Marks]**

**b)** An op-amp has a slew rate of **0.5 V/μs**. Determine the **maximum frequency** at which it can amplify a sinusoidal signal of peak amplitude **8 V** without slew-rate distortion. Also calculate the **Full-Power Bandwidth** if the maximum output swing is **±12 V**. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for B-A(a): [7 Marks]

**Given:** Output is a sine wave $V_o(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Write the output voltage expression.
$$V_o(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Differentiate with respect to time.
$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = \omega V_m \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Identify the maximum value of the rate of change.
Since $|\cos(\omega t)| \leq 1$, the maximum rate is:
$$\left|\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{max} = \omega V_m$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Substitute $\omega = 2\pi f$.
$$\left|\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi f V_m$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Apply the slew rate constraint.
For no slew distortion, the op-amp's slew rate must be greater than or equal to the maximum demanded rate:
$$SR \geq 2\pi f V_m$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Express in practical units.
$$SR \, (\text{V/}\mu s) = \frac{2\pi f V_m}{10^6}$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** State the physical interpretation.
This inequality states that the op-amp can faithfully reproduce a sine wave of peak amplitude $V_m$ at frequency $f$ only if its slew rate is at least $2\pi f V_m$. Otherwise, the output waveform will be distorted.

---

#### Model Solution for B-A(b): [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Convert slew rate to SI units.
$$SR = 0.5 \, \text{V/}\mu s = 0.5 \times 10^6 \, \text{V/s} = 5 \times 10^5 \, \text{V/s}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Identify the maximum frequency formula.
$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_m}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Substitute values ($V_m$ = 8 V).
$$f_{max} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{2\pi \times 8} = \frac{500000}{50.265}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Evaluate.
$$f_{max} \approx 9947.2 \, \text{Hz} \approx 9.95 \, \text{kHz}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Identify the FPB formula.
$$FPB = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_{max}}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Substitute ($V_{max}$ = 12 V).
$$FPB = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{2\pi \times 12} = \frac{500000}{75.398}$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Evaluate and present.
$$FPB \approx 6631.5 \, \text{Hz} \approx 6.63 \, \text{kHz}$$

**Final Answers:**
$$\boxed{f_{max} \approx 9.95 \, \text{kHz} \quad \text{and} \quad FPB \approx 6.63 \, \text{kHz}}$$

---

### **Question B-B** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 2]` — **14 Marks** (Alternative Choice)

**a)** Explain the **physical origin of slew rate** in a two-stage op-amp with the help of a neat sketch of the internal architecture. State the relationship between slew rate, internal current, and compensation capacitor. **[7 Marks]**

**b)** A **square wave of peak-to-peak amplitude 10 V** is applied to the input of an op-amp circuit. The slew rate of the op-amp is **1.2 V/μs**. Calculate:
   (i) The **rise time** of the output waveform. **[3.5 Marks]**
   (ii) The **maximum frequency** of an equivalent sine wave of **5 V peak** that the same op-amp can handle without slew distortion. **[3.5 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for B-B(a): [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Draw/sketch the two-stage op-amp architecture.
- Show the **Differential Input Stage** with two transistors (or input pair) and a **tail current source** $I_{tail}$.
- Show the **Gain Stage 2** (a common-emitter/inverter stage with active load).
- Show the **Compensation Capacitor $C_c$** connected in a Miller configuration between the output of Stage 1 and the output node.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Explain the role of the current source.
- The tail current source $I_{tail}$ sets a hard limit on the maximum current that can be steered to one side of the differential pair.
- This maximum current $I_{max}$ (proportional to $I_{tail}$) is what charges/discharges $C_c$.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Derive the slew rate expression.
The voltage across a capacitor changes at the rate:
$$\frac{dV_c}{dt} = \frac{I}{C_c}$$
At maximum current $I_{max}$:
$$SR = \frac{I_{max}}{C_c}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Conclude.
Since $I_{max}$ and $C_c$ are fixed by the op-amp's design, the slew rate is an **inherent, fixed property** of the device, independent of external circuit components.

---

#### Model Solution for B-B(b): [7 Marks]

**Part (i): Rise Time Calculation [3.5 Marks]**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Identify the given values.
- $V_{p-p} = 10 \, \text{V}$ (peak-to-peak)
- $SR = 1.2 \, \text{V/}\mu s$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** State the rise time formula.
$$t_r = \frac{\Delta V}{SR}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Substitute and evaluate.
$$t_r = \frac{10 \, \text{V}}{1.2 \, \text{V/}\mu s} = 8.33 \, \mu s$$

**Step 4 (0.5 Mark):** Present the final answer.
$$\boxed{t_r \approx 8.33 \, \mu s}$$

---

**Part (ii): Maximum Frequency for 5 V Peak Sine [3.5 Marks]**

**Step 1 (0.5 Mark):** Identify the given values.
- $SR = 1.2 \times 10^6 \, \text{V/s}$
- $V_m = 5 \, \text{V}$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Use the maximum frequency formula.
$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_m}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Substitute.
$$f_{max} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 5} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{31.416}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Evaluate and present.
$$f_{max} \approx 38197.2 \, \text{Hz} \approx 38.2 \, \text{kHz}$$

$$\boxed{f_{max} \approx 38.2 \, \text{kHz}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (Most Common!):** Forgetting to convert V/μs to V/s (multiply by $10^6$) or Hz to kHz. A wrong unit gives a wrong numerical answer, even if the formula is right. **Always check units first.**
>
> 2. **Confusing SR with Bandwidth:** Students often confuse *slew-rate-limited maximum frequency* ($f_{max} = SR/(2\pi V_m)$) with the *small-signal bandwidth* (set by the gain-bandwidth product). These are **two different limits**. Slew rate is a **large-signal** limit; bandwidth is a **small-signal** limit.
>
> 3. **Using Peak-to-Peak instead of Peak:** In the slew rate formula $SR = 2\pi f V_m$, the $V_m$ refers to the **peak amplitude**, NOT the peak-to-peak amplitude. Mixing these up gives an answer that is off by a factor of 2.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Physical Origin:** Examiners often award marks for explaining *why* slew rate exists (internal current limit, compensation capacitor). Students who only state the formula without the physical reasoning lose 2-3 marks.
>
> 5. **Confusing Full-Power Bandwidth with Unity-Gain Bandwidth:** For μA741, the unity-gain bandwidth is ~1 MHz, but FPB is only ~6 kHz. Writing "1 MHz" as the FPB is a common error.
>
> 6. **Mathematical Slip in Derivation:** When differentiating $\sin(\omega t)$, students sometimes forget the chain rule and write $V_m \cos(\omega t)$ instead of $V_m \omega \cos(\omega t)$. The $\omega$ factor is critical.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Slew Rate (SR)** = maximum rate of change of output voltage: $SR = (dV_o/dt)_{max}$. **Unit: V/μs.**
> - **Physical Cause:** Limited internal bias current charging the **frequency-compensation capacitor** $C_c$. Expressed as $SR = I_{max}/C_c$.
> - **For sine wave $V_o = V_m \sin(\omega t)$:** Required $SR = 2\pi f V_m$ to avoid distortion.
> - **Maximum Frequency for a given amplitude:** $f_{max} = SR / (2\pi V_m)$.
> - **Full-Power Bandwidth:** $FPB = SR / (2\pi V_{max})$ — the max frequency at maximum rated output amplitude.
> - **Rise Time for Step Input:** $t_r = \Delta V / SR$.
> - **μA741 Typical SR:** 0.5 V/μs (FPB ≈ 6 kHz for $\pm 13 \, V$ swing).
> - **LM318 (high-speed):** SR ≈ 50-70 V/μs.
> - **SR is a LARGE-signal parameter**, while bandwidth is a SMALL-signal parameter.
> - **Slew-induced distortion is non-linear** — it appears as triangular waveforms with flattened peaks.
> - **Always check units:** SR in V/μs requires multiplication by $10^6$ when working in SI (V/s).
> - **$V_m$ in the formula is the peak (not peak-to-peak) amplitude.**
> - Slew rate is **independent of the closed-loop gain configuration** — it is a property of the op-amp's internal design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bandwidth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bandwidth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the discipline of **Analog and Digital Electronics**, **Bandwidth (BW)** is rigorously defined as the range of frequencies over which an electronic system—such as an amplifier, filter, or communication channel—transmits signals with acceptable amplitude and phase fidelity. Quantitatively, for an amplifier's frequency response, the bandwidth is the difference between the **upper cutoff frequency ($f_H$)** and the **lower cutoff frequency ($f_L$)** at which the mid-band power gain drops to **half its maximum value**, corresponding to the **–3 dB points**.

Mathematically, this is expressed as:

$$BW = f_H - f_L$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, bandwidth is studied as the decisive figure-of-merit that quantifies the **frequency-selective behavior** of linear electronic systems, intrinsically tied to the **time constant ($\tau$)** of energy-storage elements (capacitors and inductors) within the circuit.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pipe" Intuition

Imagine you are watering a garden using a flexible pipe connected to a tap. If the pipe is too narrow at one end, it will **only let slow trickles of water through easily**, while rapid bursts of water will be restricted. Similarly, an electronic circuit with limited bandwidth behaves like that narrow pipe: it cannot efficiently pass **rapidly changing signals (high frequencies)** or **very slow signals (low frequencies)**, but it allows a middle range of frequencies to flow through with full strength.

> The "width" of the pipe's acceptable flow range is the **bandwidth**.

- **Wide bandwidth** → The system faithfully amplifies/transmits everything from deep bass notes to high-pitched whistles (like a high-fidelity audio amplifier).
- **Narrow bandwidth** → The system is highly selective; it amplifies only a small slice of frequencies (like a radio tuner locked onto a single FM station).

## 1.3 The 3 dB Power Rule and Voltage/Current Conventions

The **–3 dB point** (also called the **half-power point**) arises because power is proportional to the square of voltage or current. When voltage gain falls to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (approximately **0.7071**) of its mid-band value, the power gain falls to $\frac{1}{2}$.

In decibels:

$$A_{V(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$$

So if the voltage gain is 0.7071 times the mid-band gain:

$$20 \log_{10}(0.7071) = -3 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standards Highlighted in Bold:**
> - The standard cutoff threshold is **–3 dB** (one-half power).
> - 0 dB reference is the **maximum mid-band gain** ($A_{mid}$).
> - $f_L$ and $f_H$ are universally measured in **Hertz (Hz)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition — How Bandwidth Emerges

To fully understand bandwidth, we must dissect the **frequency response** of a typical cascaded electronic amplifier into three distinct regions:

### **Region 1: Low-Frequency Region ($f < f_L$)**
- **Dominant Physics:** Coupling capacitors ($C_C$) and bypass capacitors ($C_E$) exhibit high reactance $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ at low frequencies.
- **Effect on Signal:** The capacitors block the AC signal, attenuating the output.
- **Mathematical Signature:** Gain rises at **+20 dB/decade** as frequency increases toward $f_L$.

### **Region 2: Mid-Band Region ($f_L \leq f \leq f_H$)**
- **Dominant Physics:** All capacitive reactances become negligible. Coupling and bypass capacitors act as short circuits; internal (parasitic) capacitances act as open circuits.
- **Effect on Signal:** The amplifier provides its **maximum, constant gain** ($A_{mid}$), with **zero phase shift** contributed by coupling networks.
- **Mathematical Signature:** Flat response on a Bode magnitude plot.

### **Region 3: High-Frequency Region ($f > f_H$)**
- **Dominant Physics:** Internal parasitic capacitances (Miller capacitance, junction capacitances of BJTs/FETs, wiring capacitances) become significant.
- **Effect on Signal:** These capacitances shunt the signal to ground, attenuating the output.
- **Mathematical Signature:** Gain falls at **–20 dB/decade** as frequency increases.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Cutoff Frequencies

A cutoff frequency is fundamentally set by the **RC time constant** of the signal path. The general relationship is:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{eq}}$$

where $R_{eq}$ and $C_{eq}$ are the **Thevenin equivalent resistance and capacitance** seen by the energy-storage element at the node of interest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of KTU Frequency Analysis:** For any single time-constant (STC) network, the **–3 dB frequency is the inverse of $2\pi$ times the time constant**. Memorize this—it is the single most useful relation in amplifier frequency response problems.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L$ | **Hz** | Defines useful operating frequency range |
| 2 | Lower Cutoff Frequency | $f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_{out} + R_{in}) C_C}$ | **Hz** | Set by coupling/bypass capacitors |
| 3 | Upper Cutoff Frequency | $f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{eq}}$ | **Hz** | Set by parasitic/Miller capacitances |
| 4 | Low-Freq Voltage Gain | $A_L = \frac{A_{mid}}{1 - j(f_L / f)}$ | **dimensionless** | Bode plot construction for low region |
| 5 | High-Freq Voltage Gain | $A_H = \frac{A_{mid}}{1 + j(f / f_H)}$ | **dimensionless** | Bode plot construction for high region |
| 6 | Overall Gain | $A = \frac{A_{mid}}{(1 - j f_L / f)(1 + j f / f_H)}$ | **dimensionless** | Complete amplifier transfer function |
| 7 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $GBP = A_{mid} \times BW$ | **Hz** | Figure of merit for op-amps |
| 8 | Miller Capacitance | $C_M = C_{gd}(1 + A_v)$ | **Farads** | Dominant high-frequency limitation |
| 9 | dB Conversion | $dB = 20 \log_{10}(A_v)$ | **dB** | Logarithmic gain scale |
| 10 | Half-Power Condition | $P_{out} = \frac{1}{2} P_{max} \Leftrightarrow A_v = 0.7071 A_{mid}$ | **W** | Definition of cutoff points |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Bandwidth is the cornerstone of every modern technology. It dictates the **channel capacity** in 5G/6G wireless systems (Shannon-Hartley theorem), the **fidelity** of audio amplifiers, the **resolution** of oscilloscopes, and the **speed** of operational amplifiers. KTU examiners frequently test bandwidth in the context of op-amp selection and filter design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Lower Cutoff Frequency for a Capacitively-Coupled Amplifier

Consider a single-stage CE amplifier with an **input coupling capacitor $C_C$** between the source and the transistor base. The Thevenin equivalent looking from $C_C$ is a series combination of the source resistance $R_S$ and the amplifier's input resistance $R_{in}$.

### Step 1 — Identify the Equivalent Circuit at Low Frequency
At low frequencies, $C_C$ has appreciable impedance. The relevant loop is the voltage divider formed by:
- $R_S$ (source resistance)
- $C_C$ (coupling capacitor)
- $R_{in}$ (input resistance of amplifier)

The total series resistance is:

$$R_{tot} = R_S + R_{in}$$

### Step 2 — Express the Transfer Function Across $C_C$
The voltage reaching the amplifier input is attenuated by the voltage divider:

$$\frac{V_{in}}{V_{source}} = \frac{R_{in}}{R_S + R_{in} + \frac{1}{j\omega C_C}}$$

### Step 3 — Simplify by Factoring Out the Denominator Constant
Divide numerator and denominator by $(R_S + R_{in})$:

$$\frac{V_{in}}{V_{source}} = \frac{R_{in} / (R_S + R_{in})}{1 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_C (R_S + R_{in})}}$$

Let us define the **mid-band ratio** as $\alpha = \frac{R_{in}}{R_S + R_{in}}$ and the **time constant** as $\tau = C_C (R_S + R_{in})$.

Then:

$$\frac{V_{in}}{V_{source}} = \frac{\alpha}{1 + \frac{1}{j\omega \tau}}$$

### Step 4 — Identify the Cutoff Frequency
The transfer function has a pole where $j\omega \tau = -1$, i.e., where $\omega = \frac{1}{\tau}$. This is precisely the **–3 dB cutoff frequency**:

$$\omega_L = \frac{1}{\tau} = \frac{1}{C_C (R_S + R_{in})}$$

Converting to Hz:

$$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi C_C (R_S + R_{in})}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** Larger coupling capacitors or smaller source/input resistances shift $f_L$ to lower values, extending the low-frequency response. This is why audio amplifiers use large electrolytic capacitors (typically 1–10 $\mu$F).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Upper Cutoff Frequency Using the Miller Effect

The high-frequency limitation is dominated by the **Miller capacitance** $C_M$ between the collector and base of a BJT. The **voltage gain** from base to collector is $A_v$ (which is negative for a CE amplifier). The effective input capacitance becomes:

$$C_{M(in)} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v) \approx C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$

> **Why the sign flip?** A negative $A_v$ means a positive swing at the input causes a negative swing at the output. The voltage **across** $C_{gd}$ is therefore $(1 - A_v)$ times the input voltage, and since $A_v$ is large and negative, the term $(1 - A_v)$ becomes very large and positive. The current through $C_{gd}$ is amplified by this factor, making its effective input contribution enormous.

### Step 1 — Define the Equivalent High-Frequency Input Network
Thevenin equivalent at the input node:
- Source: $V_S$ with resistance $R_S$
- Parallel combination: $R_{in} \parallel R_{M(in)}$
- Miller capacitance $C_{M(in)}$ in parallel

### Step 2 — Compute the Equivalent Resistance
$$R_{eq} = R_S \parallel R_{in} \parallel R_{M(in)}$$

(In practice, $R_{M(in)}$ is usually much larger than $R_S \parallel R_{in}$, so it can often be ignored.)

### Step 3 — Express the Time Constant
$$\tau_H = R_{eq} \cdot C_{eq}$$

where the dominant capacitance is $C_{M(in)}$.

### Step 4 — Compute the Upper Cutoff Frequency
$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{M(in)}}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $C_{M(in)}$ Explicitly
$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Insight:** A common trap is forgetting the $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ factor. The Miller effect can multiply $C_{gd}$ (a few pF) into an effective capacitance of several hundred pF, drastically reducing $f_H$. This is why high-frequency transistors are designed with **very small $C_{gd}$**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Complete Gain Expression (Combining Both Regions)

Starting from the low-frequency transfer function and the high-frequency transfer function derived above, we **cascade (multiply)** them to obtain the complete frequency-dependent gain:

$$A_v(f) = A_{mid} \cdot \underbrace{\left(\frac{1}{1 - j(f_L / f)}\right)}_{\text{Low-frequency term}} \cdot \underbrace{\left(\frac{1}{1 + j(f / f_H)}\right)}_{\text{High-frequency term}}$$

This compact form is the **canonical amplifier transfer function** recognized globally and is the foundation for every Bode plot you will be asked to draw in KTU examinations.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem:** A CE amplifier has the following parameters:
- $A_{mid} = 200$
- $f_L = 50$ Hz
- $f_H = 100$ kHz

**Find:**
1. The bandwidth.
2. The gain (in dB) at $f = 200$ kHz.
3. The gain (in dB) at $f = 20$ Hz.

### Solution

**Part 1 — Bandwidth:**

$$BW = f_H - f_L = 100{,}000 - 50 \approx 99{,}950 \text{ Hz} \approx 100 \text{ kHz}$$

(Since $f_L \ll f_H$, we often approximate $BW \approx f_H$.)

**Part 2 — Gain at 200 kHz (in the high-frequency roll-off region):**

$$\vert A_v \vert = \frac{A_{mid}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_H)^2}} = \frac{200}{\sqrt{1 + (200{,}000/100{,}000)^2}}$$

$$= \frac{200}{\sqrt{1 + 4}} = \frac{200}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{200}{2.236} = 89.44$$

In dB:

$$A_{v(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(89.44) = 20 \times 1.9514 = 39.03 \text{ dB}$$

The mid-band gain in dB is $20 \log_{10}(200) = 46.02$ dB, so the gain has dropped by **7 dB** (more than one octave of roll-off, as expected for $f = 2 f_H$).

**Part 3 — Gain at 20 Hz (in the low-frequency roll-off region):**

$$\vert A_v \vert = \frac{A_{mid}}{\sqrt{1 + (f_L/f)^2}} = \frac{200}{\sqrt{1 + (50/20)^2}} = \frac{200}{\sqrt{1 + 6.25}}$$

$$= \frac{200}{\sqrt{7.25}} = \frac{200}{2.693} = 74.27$$

In dB:

$$A_{v(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(74.27) = 37.42 \text{ dB}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Amplifier's Frequency Response

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **decision logic** that determines which region of the frequency response is dominant at any given input frequency, mapping the signal flow from source to load.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT SIGNAL SOURCE V_S"] --> nodeB{"IS f LESS THAN f_L?"}
    nodeB -- YES --> nodeC["LOW-FREQUENCY REGION"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["COUPLING CAPACITORS C_C DOMINATE"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["GAIN RISES AT 20 DB PER DECADE"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["ATTENUATED OUTPUT TO LOAD"]
    nodeB -- NO --> nodeG{"IS f GREATER THAN f_H?"}
    nodeG -- NO --> nodeH["MID-BAND REGION"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["ALL CAPACITORS NEGLIGIBLE"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["CONSTANT MAXIMUM GAIN A_MID"]
    nodeJ --> nodeF
    nodeG -- YES --> nodeK["HIGH-FREQUENCY REGION"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["MILLER AND PARASITIC CAPACITANCES DOMINATE"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["GAIN FALLS AT 20 DB PER DECADE"]
    nodeM --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeN["FINAL OUTPUT V_OUT TO LOAD"]
```

## 4.2 Bode Plot Schematic — Conceptual Magnitude Response

The following Mermaid block represents the **Bode magnitude plot** of a band-pass amplifier response (logarithmic frequency axis). The three regions, cutoff points, and roll-off slopes are labeled as nodes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1["F = 10 HZ"] --> p2["DEEP LOW-FREQUENCY ROLL-OFF"]
    p2 --> p3["F = f_L AT 3 DB BELOW MID-BAND"]
    p3 --> p4["MID-BAND PLATEAU AT A_MID DB"]
    p4 --> p5["F = f_H AT 3 DB BELOW MID-BAND"]
    p5 --> p6["HIGH-FREQUENCY ROLL-OFF AT 20 DB PER DECADE"]
    p6 --> p7["F = 1 MHZ AND BEYOND"]
```

> **Reading Guide for Students:** The plot is symmetric in **dB** (not in linear frequency). The plateau between $f_L$ and $f_H$ defines the **passband**, while the slopes outside it (–20 dB/decade in this single-pole example) define the **stopband transition**.

## 4.3 Cascaded Amplifier Bandwidth Diagram

When multiple amplifier stages are cascaded, the **overall bandwidth shrinks** because the cumulative gain roll-off is steeper. The Mermaid diagram below shows the architecture and the bandwidth relationship.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stageA["STAGE 1: f_L1, f_H1, A_MID1"] --> stageB["STAGE 2: f_L2, f_H2, A_MID2"]
    stageB --> stageC["STAGE N: f_LN, f_HN, A_MIDN"]
    stageC --> result["OVERALL SYSTEM BANDWIDTH BW_TOTAL IS NARROWER THAN INDIVIDUAL BW"]
    result --> formula["APPROXIMATION: 1 OVER BW_TOTAL ≈ 1 OVER BW1 PLUS 1 OVER BW2 PLUS ..."]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** In multi-stage amplifiers, the **dominant pole** (the one with the lowest $f_H$) sets the overall upper cutoff. This is why KTU problems often ask you to identify "which stage limits the bandwidth."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define the term "bandwidth" of an amplifier. What are the upper and lower cutoff frequencies?**

**Model Answer:**

The bandwidth of an amplifier is the range of frequencies over which the amplifier provides useful amplification. It is mathematically defined as the difference between the upper and lower cutoff frequencies:

$$BW = f_H - f_L$$

The **lower cutoff frequency ($f_L$)** is the frequency at which the voltage gain falls to **0.7071 times** the mid-band value (or the power gain falls to half, i.e., **–3 dB**) in the low-frequency region. It is determined by the coupling and bypass capacitors.

The **upper cutoff frequency ($f_H$)** is the corresponding frequency in the high-frequency region where the gain again drops by **3 dB** from the mid-band value. It is determined by the internal (parasitic and Miller) capacitances of the active device.

> **Total Marks Allocation (3):** Definition = 1, Formula = 1, Description of $f_L$ and $f_H$ = 1.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**State the significance of the –3 dB point in frequency response. Why is it chosen as the cutoff criterion?**

**Model Answer:**

The –3 dB point is the frequency at which the **output power** of the amplifier has dropped to **half** of its mid-band power, which corresponds to the **voltage gain** falling to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (approximately **70.71%**) of its mid-band value.

**Reasons for choosing –3 dB as the cutoff criterion:**

1. **Power Interpretation:** It represents the **half-power point**, a clear and unambiguous physical benchmark.
2. **Logarithmic Convenience:** On a decibel (logarithmic) scale, it corresponds exactly to a **3 dB drop**, which is easy to read and mark on a Bode plot.
3. **Auditory Perception:** For audio systems, a 3 dB drop is the smallest change in power that the average human ear can reliably detect.
4. **Mathematical Simplicity:** It is the natural breakpoint of an RC time-constant network, where the real and imaginary parts of the transfer function are equal in magnitude.

> **Total Marks Allocation (3):** Definition of –3 dB = 1, Power interpretation = 1, Two valid reasons = 1.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **OPTION A: Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the lower cutoff frequency of a single-stage RC-coupled CE amplifier. Discuss the role of the coupling and bypass capacitors.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description:**
A single-stage RC-coupled CE amplifier has a coupling capacitor $C_C$ between the source and base, a coupling capacitor $C_{C2}$ between the collector and the load, and a bypass capacitor $C_E$ across the emitter resistor. At low frequencies, these capacitors have non-negligible reactance.

**Step 2 — Lower Cutoff Due to Input Coupling Capacitor $C_C$:**
The Thevenin resistance seen by $C_C$ is $R_{th1} = R_S + (R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi)$.

$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_C}$$

**Step 3 — Lower Cutoff Due to Output Coupling Capacitor $C_{C2}$:**
The Thevenin resistance seen by $C_{C2}$ is $R_{th2} = R_C + R_L$.

$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{C2}}$$

**Step 4 — Lower Cutoff Due to Bypass Capacitor $C_E$:**
The resistance seen by $C_E$ (reflected through the emitter) is $R_E \parallel \left(\frac{R_S'}{1 + \beta} + \frac{r_e}{1 + \beta}\right) \approx R_E \parallel \frac{R_S' + r_e}{1 + \beta}$.

$$f_{L3} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{E(eq)} C_E}$$

**Step 5 — Dominant Pole Approximation:**
The overall $f_L$ is determined by the **largest** of $f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{L3}$ (the dominant pole):

$$f_L \approx \max(f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{L3})$$

**Role of the capacitors:**
- **Coupling capacitors ($C_C$, $C_{C2}$):** Block DC between stages while allowing AC to pass, preventing bias-point shifts. Their large capacitance ensures low reactance at the lowest signal frequency.
- **Bypass capacitor ($C_E$):** Provides a low-impedance path for the AC emitter current to ground, preventing AC negative feedback that would reduce the gain. Without it, the gain would be drastically reduced.

> **Marks Allocation:** Step 1 = 1, Steps 2–4 = 4, Step 5 = 1, Role discussion = 1.

---

**(b) Explain the Miller effect. How does it limit the high-frequency response of a CE amplifier? Derive the expression for the upper cutoff frequency. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of Miller's Theorem:**
Miller's theorem states that an impedance $Z$ connected between the input and output of an inverting amplifier with gain $A_v$ can be replaced by two separate impedances: $Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - A_v}$ at the input and $Z_2 = \frac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}$ at the output.

**Step 2 — Application to CE Amplifier:**
The base-collector capacitance $C_{gd}$ (or $C_\mu$) is the dominant parasitic capacitance. Substituting $Z = \frac{1}{j\omega C_{gd}}$ into Miller's theorem:

$$C_{M(in)} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v) = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$

Since $A_v$ is large and negative, $(1 - A_v)$ is a large positive number, so $C_{M(in)} \gg C_{gd}$.

**Step 3 — Equivalent Input Network:**
The high-frequency input network consists of $R_S \parallel R_{in}$ driving the Miller capacitance $C_{M(in)}$.

**Step 4 — Derivation of $f_H$:**
The transfer function of this STC low-pass network gives:

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{M(in)}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S \parallel R_{in}) C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)}$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation:**
Because $\vert A_v \vert$ is typically 100–200, the effective input capacitance is **100–200 times** the actual $C_{gd}$. A 5 pF $C_{gd}$ effectively becomes a 500–1000 pF capacitor at the input, drastically lowering $f_H$ (often into the kHz range). This is the **fundamental reason** CE amplifiers have limited high-frequency response compared to CB or cascoded configurations.

> **Marks Allocation:** Miller definition = 1, Application = 2, Derivation = 2, Physical interpretation = 2.

---

### **OPTION B: Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) With the help of a neat sketch, explain the frequency response of an RC-coupled amplifier. Mark the three regions and state the gain equation in each region.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sketch Description:**
The frequency response is a **band-pass** curve on a semi-log plot:
- X-axis: Frequency in Hz (logarithmic scale)
- Y-axis: Gain in dB
- The curve is flat in the middle and rolls off at both ends.

**Step 2 — Three Regions:**

**Region I — Low-Frequency Region ($f < f_L$):**
Gain rolls off at **+20 dB/decade** as frequency increases toward $f_L$. The gain equation is:

$$A_L = \frac{A_{mid}}{1 - j(f_L / f)}$$

The phase shift is **+90°** (leading).

**Region II — Mid-Band Region ($f_L \leq f \leq f_H$):**
Gain is **constant and maximum**:

$$A_{mid} = \text{constant}$$

The phase shift is **0°** (no reactive contribution).

**Region III — High-Frequency Region ($f > f_H$):**
Gain rolls off at **–20 dB/decade** as frequency increases beyond $f_H$. The gain equation is:

$$A_H = \frac{A_{mid}}{1 + j(f / f_H)}$$

The phase shift is **–90°** (lagging).

**Step 3 — The –3 dB Points:**
At $f = f_L$ and $f = f_H$, the gain is $A_{mid} - 3$ dB. The bandwidth is:

$$BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H \quad (\text{since } f_L \ll f_H)$$

> **Marks Allocation:** Sketch = 2, Region I = 1.5, Region II = 1, Region III = 1.5, Gain equations = 1.

---

**(b) A single-stage CE amplifier has a mid-band gain of 200, lower cutoff frequency of 50 Hz, and upper cutoff frequency of 200 kHz. Calculate: (i) the bandwidth, (ii) the gain in dB at the cutoff frequencies, (iii) the gain in dB at 50 kHz, and (iv) the gain in dB at 25 Hz.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $A_{mid} = 200$, $f_L = 50$ Hz, $f_H = 200$ kHz.

**(i) Bandwidth:**

$$BW = f_H - f_L = 200{,}000 - 50 = 199{,}950 \text{ Hz} \approx 200 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Gain in dB at the Cutoff Frequencies:**
By definition, gain at both $f_L$ and $f_H$ is **3 dB below** the mid-band gain.

Mid-band gain in dB:

$$A_{mid(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(200) = 20 \times 2.301 = 46.02 \text{ dB}$$

At the cutoff frequencies:

$$A_{c(dB)} = 46.02 - 3 = 43.02 \text{ dB}$$

**(iii) Gain in dB at 50 kHz (within the high-frequency roll-off region):**

$$f / f_H = 50{,}000 / 200{,}000 = 0.25$$

$$\vert A_v \vert = \frac{200}{\sqrt{1 + (0.25)^2}} = \frac{200}{\sqrt{1.0625}} = \frac{200}{1.0308} = 194.03$$

In dB:

$$A_{v(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(194.03) = 20 \times 2.288 = 45.76 \text{ dB}$$

**(iv) Gain in dB at 25 Hz (within the low-frequency roll-off region):**

$$f_L / f = 50 / 25 = 2$$

$$\vert A_v \vert = \frac{200}{\sqrt{1 + (2)^2}} = \frac{200}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{200}{2.236} = 89.44$$

In dB:

$$A_{v(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(89.44) = 20 \times 1.951 = 39.03 \text{ dB}$$

> **Marks Allocation:** (i) = 1, (ii) = 2, (iii) = 2, (iv) = 2.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Miller multiplication factor $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$:** This is the #1 error. A student writing $C_{M(in)} = C_{gd}$ instead of $C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ will lose **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Mixing up $f_L / f$ vs $f / f_L$:** In the low-frequency gain equation, the correct form is $\frac{1}{1 - j(f_L/f)}$, NOT $\frac{1}{1 - j(f/f_L)}$. Drawing the Bode plot with the wrong slope direction will cost full marks.
> 3. **Not approximating $BW \approx f_H$:** When $f_L \ll f_H$ (which is the typical case), failing to recognize this and writing $BW = f_H - f_L$ in symbolic form is acceptable, but using the approximation in numerical answers shows understanding.
> 4. **Ignoring the bypass capacitor:** Many students compute only $f_{L1}$ and $f_{L2}$ from coupling capacitors, missing that $f_{L3}$ from $C_E$ is often the **dominant** low-frequency pole.
> 5. **Confusing dB conventions:** Power uses $10 \log_{10}$, voltage/current use $20 \log_{10}$. Writing $10 \log_{10}(200)$ for voltage gain is a fatal error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bandwidth ($BW$)** is defined as $f_H - f_L$, where $f_L$ and $f_H$ are the **–3 dB** (half-power) frequencies.
- **Low-frequency response** is limited by **coupling and bypass capacitors**, with cutoff frequency $f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{eq}}$.
- **High-frequency response** is limited by **parasitic and Miller-multiplied capacitances**, with $f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{M(in)}}$.
- **Miller's theorem** multiplies $C_{gd}$ by $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$, making it the dominant high-frequency limitation in CE amplifiers.
- The **complete amplifier transfer function** is the product of low-frequency and high-frequency responses: $A_v(f) = \frac{A_{mid}}{(1 - j f_L/f)(1 + j f/f_H)}$.
- The **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)** is a constant for a given op-amp; increasing the closed-loop gain reduces the bandwidth proportionally.
- **–3 dB corresponds to 0.7071 of the voltage gain** and 0.5 of the power gain — memorize this constant.
- The **roll-off slope** in a single-pole system is **±20 dB/decade** (or **±6 dB/octave**).
- For **cascaded stages**, the overall bandwidth is **narrower** than any individual stage's bandwidth.
- In **audio amplifiers**, $f_L$ is typically 20 Hz and $f_H$ is typically 20 kHz (the human hearing range).
- For **video amplifiers**, $f_H$ must extend to several MHz to preserve fine detail.
- For **RF and communication systems**, bandwidth determines channel capacity per the **Shannon-Hartley theorem**.
- The **dominant pole** in a multi-pole system is the one with the **lowest $f_H$** (or highest $f_L$); it sets the system bandwidth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CMRR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CMRR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** is a definitive figure-of-merit for any differential amplifier (including the operational amplifier) that quantifies its proficiency in amplifying the *difference* between two input signals while simultaneously *rejecting* any signal component that is common (identical in magnitude and phase) to both inputs.

Mathematically, CMRR is defined as the ratio of the **differential-mode gain ($A_d$)** to the **common-mode gain ($A_{cm}$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The CMRR is universally expressed in **decibels (dB)** in all university-level derivations and numerical problems. A higher CMRR value denotes a superior, more noise-immune differential amplifier.

### 1.2 Differential vs. Common-Mode Signals — The Foundation

To fully grasp CMRR, the student must first decouple the two input voltages $V_1$ and $V_2$ into their orthogonal components:

| Signal Type | Definition | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Differential-Mode Signal ($V_d$)** | $V_d = V_1 - V_2$ | The *useful* signal we want to amplify. |
| **Common-Mode Signal ($V_{cm}$)** | $V_{cm} = \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ | The *noise/interference* riding on both lines equally (e.g., 50 Hz mains hum, thermal drift). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Stethoscope" Effect:** Imagine you are a doctor using a stethoscope in a noisy hospital ward. The heartbeat is the *differential signal* (it exists only between the two ears of the stethoscope), while the general ward chatter is the *common-mode signal* (it enters both earpieces equally). A good stethoscope has a high "CMRR" — it amplifies the heartbeat and crushes the background chatter. An op-amp with high CMRR does the exact same thing for electrical signals — it amplifies the *intended* differential input and brutally rejects unwanted common-mode noise.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants
