- **Ideal CMRR:** **$\infty$ (infinity)** for an ideal op-amp.
- **Typical Real-World CMRR:** **60 dB to 120 dB** for general-purpose op-amps (e.g., $\mu$A741 ≈ 90 dB).
- **Unit:** Dimensionless ratio, conventionally converted to **dB** for practical use.
- **Inverting/Non-inverting Gain Denoted as $A_d$:** $A_d$ is the gain applied *only* to the differential input component.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Output Voltage Equation (The Master Equation)

For a real (non-ideal) differential amplifier with finite CMRR, the output voltage $V_o$ is governed by the **superposition of two gain paths**:

$$
V_o = A_d \cdot V_d + A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm}
$$

Where:
- $A_d$ = Differential-mode gain (the *desired* amplification).
- $A_{cm}$ = Common-mode gain (the *unwanted* leakage of common signals to the output).

Expanding this using $V_d = V_1 - V_2$ and $V_{cm} = \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$:

$$
V_o = A_d(V_1 - V_2) + A_{cm}\left(\frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}\right)
$$

Rearranging in terms of the two individual inputs:

$$
V_o = \left(A_d + \frac{A_{cm}}{2}\right)V_1 + \left(-A_d + \frac{A_{cm}}{2}\right)V_2
$$

### 2.2 Why CMRR Exists — The "Why" Behind the Metric

In a perfectly symmetric differential amplifier (e.g., a balanced emitter-coupled pair, an ideal instrumentation amplifier), any signal applied *identically* to both inputs should produce *zero* output because of perfect cancellation. However, real transistors and resistors have mismatches, leading to a small, non-zero $A_{cm}$. The CMRR simply *normalizes* how small this leakage is relative to the desired differential amplification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In industrial environments, long cables carrying low-level sensor signals (e.g., from a thermocouple or strain gauge) pick up identical 50 Hz/60 Hz interference on both lines. An op-amp with high CMRR (such as an **Instrumentation Amplifier**, typically > 100 dB) can cancel this interference while amplifying the tiny sensor signal — making CMRR the single most critical specification for **sensor signal conditioning, ECG/biomedical amplifiers, and audio balanced line receivers**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** To avoid breaking the markdown table, all absolute value bars and set-builder braces are rendered using `\vert` and `\mid` LaTeX commands instead of the literal `|` pipe character.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit / Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $CMRR = \left\vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert$ | CMRR in absolute (linear) ratio. | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $CMRR_{(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left\vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert$ | CMRR expressed in decibels. | **dB** |
| 3 | $A_{cm} = \dfrac{\vert A_d \vert}{10^{CMRR_{(dB)}/20}}$ | Common-mode gain in terms of $A_d$ and CMRR. | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $V_o = A_d V_d + A_{cm} V_{cm}$ | Total output voltage equation. | Volts (V) |
| 5 | $V_d = V_1 - V_2$ | Differential input voltage. | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $V_{cm} = \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ | Common-mode input voltage. | Volts (V) |
| 7 | $A_d = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ (standard diff. amp) | Differential gain of classic 4-resistor diff. amp. | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $A_{cm} = \dfrac{R_f / R_1}{1 + \left(1 + R_f/R_1\right)(2R_1/R_{tail})}$ | Common-mode gain with tail resistor $R_{tail}$ (BJT). | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Ideal Op-Amp Condition | $A_d \to \infty$, $A_{cm} \to 0$, $CMRR \to \infty$ | — |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Output Equation to the CMRR Definition

**Starting Point:** The output of any linear differential amplifier is the sum of its two independent gain paths acting on $V_d$ and $V_{cm}$.

$$
V_o = A_d \cdot V_d + A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm}
$$

**Step 1 — Factor out the differential gain $A_d$:**

$$
V_o = A_d \left[ V_d + \left(\frac{A_{cm}}{A_d}\right) V_{cm} \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the inverse of the ratio inside the bracket as $1/CMRR$:**

Since $CMRR = \left\vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert$, we have $\dfrac{A_{cm}}{A_d} = \dfrac{1}{CMRR}$. Substituting:

$$
V_o = A_d \left[ V_d + \frac{V_{cm}}{CMRR} \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result:**
The output is the desired amplified differential signal $A_d \cdot V_d$ **plus** an *error term* $\dfrac{A_d \cdot V_{cm}}{CMRR}$ caused by the imperfect rejection of the common-mode noise.

$$
\boxed{V_o = A_d \cdot V_d + \frac{A_d \cdot V_{cm}}{CMRR}}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** The term $\dfrac{V_{cm}}{CMRR}$ represents the **equivalent differential input error voltage** — the false differential signal that mimics a real one due to finite CMRR.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — The "KTU Classic" Problem Pattern

> **Problem:** A differential amplifier has a differential gain $A_d = 2000$ and a common-mode gain $A_{cm} = 0.2$. The two inputs are $V_1 = 105 \,\mu V$ and $V_2 = 95 \,\mu V$. Calculate:
> (i) CMRR in dB
> (ii) The actual output voltage $V_o$.

#### (i) Calculation of CMRR

**Step 1:** Compute the linear CMRR ratio.

$$
CMRR = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert = \frac{2000}{0.2} = 10000
$$

**Step 2:** Convert to decibels.

$$
CMRR_{(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(10000) = 20 \times 4 = 80 \text{ dB}
$$

#### (ii) Calculation of Output Voltage

**Step 3:** Find the differential input voltage.

$$
V_d = V_1 - V_2 = 105\,\mu V - 95\,\mu V = 10\,\mu V
$$

**Step 4:** Find the common-mode input voltage.

$$
V_{cm} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} = \frac{105\,\mu V + 95\,\mu V}{2} = 100\,\mu V
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the master output equation.

$$
V_o = A_d \cdot V_d + A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm}
$$

$$
V_o = (2000)(10\,\mu V) + (0.2)(100\,\mu V)
$$

$$
V_o = 20{,}000\,\mu V + 20\,\mu V = 20{,}020\,\mu V = 20.02 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 6 — Ideal vs. Real Output Comparison:**

If the amplifier were ideal ($A_{cm} = 0$), the output would be exactly $20 \text{ mV}$. The extra **$20 \,\mu V$** is the **CMRR-induced error** — a small but critical deviation in precision instrumentation.

---

### 3.3 Reverse Derivation: Finding Common-Mode Gain from dB Spec

**Given:** $CMRR = 90$ dB, $A_d = 5000$. Find $A_{cm}$.

**Step 1:** Convert CMRR from dB to linear ratio.

$$
CMRR_{linear} = 10^{90/20} = 10^{4.5} \approx 31622.77
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $A_{cm}$.

$$
A_{cm} = \frac{A_d}{CMRR_{linear}} = \frac{5000}{31622.77} \approx 0.1581
$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation — for every volt of differential input, only ~158 mV of common-mode signal leaks through. This is exceptionally good rejection.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — CMRR Signal Flow Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the two input signals are decomposed, scaled by their respective gains, and summed at the output. **All node IDs are purely alphanumeric to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rendering safeguards.**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["Input V1"] --> M1["Differential Vd = V1 minus V2"]
    A2["Input V2"] --> M1
    A1 --> M2["Common Mode Vcm = V1 plus V2 over 2"]
    A2 --> M2
    M1 --> G1["Gain Ad Differential"]
    M2 --> G2["Gain Acm Common Mode"]
    G1 --> SUM["Summing Junction"]
    G2 --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT["Output Vo = Ad Vd plus Acm Vcm"]
    G1 --> CMRR_CALC["CMRR Formula Ad divided by Acm in dB"]
    G2 --> CMRR_CALC
    CMRR_CALC --> METRIC["Quality Metric in decibels"]
```

### 4.2 Signal Decomposition Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    SUB["Two Input Voltages V1 and V2"] --> DECOMP["Signal Decomposer"]
    DECOMP --> VDIFF["Differential Path Vd = V1 minus V2"]
    DECOMP --> VCOM["Common Mode Path Vcm equals V1 plus V2 divided by 2"]
    VDIFF --> GDIFF["Amplified by Ad Desired Signal"]
    VCOM --> GCOM["Leaked by Acm Undesired Noise"]
    GDIFF --> ADD["Adder Output Node"]
    GCOM --> ADD
    ADD --> VO["Final Output Vo"]
    GDIFF -.->|Drives| METRIC1["CMRR is HIGH if Ad is large and Acm is small"]
    GCOM -.->|Drives| METRIC2["CMRR is LOW if Acm becomes significant"]
```

### 4.3 CMRR vs. Frequency Behavior (Conceptual Plot via Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    LOWF["Low Frequency Region"] -->|Steep Plateau| MIDF["Midband Flat High CMRR Region"]
    MIDF -->|Roll off at 20 dB per decade| HIGHF["High Frequency Roll off Region"]
    HIGHF -->|CMRR degrades| ZONE["Critical Performance Drop"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Architecture Note:** In physical textbooks, the CMRR is often depicted as a Bode plot where CMRR (in dB) remains flat across the low/mid frequencies and then rolls off (decreases) at high frequencies due to *mismatched pole frequencies* in the two halves of the differential pair. The above block diagram captures this conceptual drop in a renderable form, replacing the analog plot with a directional flow of the degradation zones.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of a differential amplifier. State its ideal value.

**Model Answer:**
CMRR is defined as the ratio of the differential-mode gain ($A_d$) to the common-mode gain ($A_{cm}$) of a differential amplifier. It is expressed as:

$$
CMRR = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert = 20 \log_{10}\left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert \text{ dB}
$$

It quantifies the amplifier's ability to reject signals common to both inputs while amplifying the difference. **The ideal value of CMRR is infinity ($\infty$)**, meaning an ideal differential amplifier has zero common-mode gain and amplifies only the differential component.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Mention any two factors that affect the CMRR of a differential amplifier.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Mismatch in collector (or drain) load resistors** of the two halves of the differential pair — even a 1% mismatch drastically reduces CMRR.
2. **Finite output resistance of the tail current source (or emitter degeneration resistor)** — a higher tail resistance provides better common-mode rejection.
3. *(Additional valid answer)* Variation of transistor parameters ($\beta$, $V_{BE}$, $g_m$) between the two sides of the differential pair due to manufacturing tolerances.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> ### 📌 Question A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Style]**

**For a differential amplifier, the differential mode gain is $A_d = 4000$ and the common mode gain is $A_{cm} = 0.5$. Two input signals $V_1 = 1.205$ V and $V_2 = 1.195$ V are applied.**

**(a)** Calculate the CMRR in dB. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Determine the output voltage $V_o$ and the percentage error introduced due to finite CMRR. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Linear CMRR Calculation:**

$$
CMRR = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert = \frac{4000}{0.5} = 8000
$$

**Step 2 — Conversion to Decibels:**

$$
CMRR_{(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(8000) = 20 \times \log_{10}(8 \times 10^3)
$$

$$
= 20 \times [ \log_{10}(8) + 3 ] = 20 \times [0.9031 + 3] = 20 \times 3.9031
$$

$$
\boxed{CMRR_{(dB)} = 78.06 \text{ dB}}
$$

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Differential Mode Voltage:**

$$
V_d = V_1 - V_2 = 1.205 - 1.195 = 0.010 \text{ V} = 10 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 2 — Common Mode Voltage:**

$$
V_{cm} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} = \frac{1.205 + 1.195}{2} = 1.200 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 — Output Voltage using Master Equation:**

$$
V_o = A_d \cdot V_d + A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm}
$$

$$
V_o = (4000)(0.010) + (0.5)(1.200) = 40 + 0.6
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = 40.6 \text{ V}}
$$

**Step 4 — Ideal Output (assuming $A_{cm} = 0$):**

$$
V_{o,\text{ideal}} = A_d \cdot V_d = 4000 \times 0.010 = 40.0 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Error due to Finite CMRR:**

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \frac{V_o - V_{o,\text{ideal}}}{V_{o,\text{ideal}}} \times 100 = \frac{40.6 - 40.0}{40.0} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\% \text{ Error} = 1.5\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> - **Common Mistake (–2 marks):** Students often write the output as $V_o = A_d \cdot V_{cm}$ — this is **wrong**. $A_d$ acts on $V_d$ and $A_{cm}$ acts on $V_{cm}$. Mixing up the inputs and gains is the most frequent error.
> - **Common Mistake (–1 mark):** Forgetting to multiply $\log_{10}$ by 20 (some students mistakenly use 10, which is the formula for *power* ratios, not voltage ratios). **CMRR uses 20.**
> - **Mark Loss Zone:** The "percentage error" sub-part is often skipped. Always show *both* the ideal and actual output, otherwise the examiner has no way to award the 2 marks for the error calculation.

---

> ### 📌 Question B — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the output voltage expression $V_o = A_d V_d + A_{cm} V_{cm}$ for a differential amplifier and explain how CMRR is obtained from it. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A differential amplifier has CMRR = 100 dB and differential gain $A_d = 10^4$. If the two input voltages are $V_1 = 2.05$ mV and $V_2 = 1.95$ mV, find the common-mode gain and the actual output voltage. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Definition of the Two Modes:**
Any two input signals $V_1$ and $V_2$ can be expressed in terms of their differential and common-mode components:

$$
V_1 = V_{cm} + \frac{V_d}{2}, \quad V_2 = V_{cm} - \frac{V_d}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Superposition Principle:**
By the linearity of the differential amplifier, the output is the sum of:
- The response to a pure differential input $V_d$ (with $V_{cm} = 0$): output = $A_d \cdot V_d$
- The response to a pure common-mode input $V_{cm}$ (with $V_d = 0$): output = $A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm}$

**Step 3 — Resultant Output Equation:**

$$
V_o = A_d V_d + A_{cm} V_{cm}
$$

**Step 4 — Obtaining CMRR:**
CMRR is defined as the ratio of the magnitude of the differential gain to the magnitude of the common-mode gain:

$$
CMRR = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert
$$

**Step 5 — dB Form:**

$$
CMRR_{(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** A high CMRR means $A_{cm} \ll A_d$, i.e., the amplifier strongly prefers differential signals and ignores common-mode noise.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Convert CMRR to Linear Scale:**

$$
CMRR_{linear} = 10^{100/20} = 10^{5} = 100{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Common-Mode Gain:**

$$
A_{cm} = \frac{A_d}{CMRR_{linear}} = \frac{10^4}{10^5} = 0.1
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $V_d$ and $V_{cm}$:**

$$
V_d = V_1 - V_2 = 2.05 \text{ mV} - 1.95 \text{ mV} = 0.10 \text{ mV}
$$

$$
V_{cm} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} = \frac{2.05 + 1.95}{2} \text{ mV} = 2.00 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Output Voltage:**

$$
V_o = A_d V_d + A_{cm} V_{cm}
$$

$$
V_o = (10^4)(0.10 \text{ mV}) + (0.1)(2.00 \text{ mV})
$$

$$
V_o = 1000 \text{ mV} + 0.2 \text{ mV}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = 1000.2 \text{ mV} = 1.0002 \text{ V}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> - **Part (a) — Derivation Trap (–3 marks):** Do not just *state* $V_o = A_d V_d + A_{cm} V_{cm}$; you must **derive** it from the principle of superposition and the definitions of $V_d$ and $V_{cm}$. Examiners specifically check whether you show $V_1 = V_{cm} + V_d/2$ substitution.
> - **Part (b) — Unit Mismatch (–1 mark):** Ensure all voltages are in the same unit (mV or V) before summing. Mixing mV and V in a single expression is a guaranteed mark loser.
> - **Decibel Conversion:** When CMRR is given in dB, the *first step must always* be $CMRR_{linear} = 10^{CMRR/20}$ — dividing by **20** is the most commonly forgotten step.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Definition:** CMRR is the ratio of differential-mode gain ($A_d$) to common-mode gain ($A_{cm}$).
- ✅ **Two Forms of the Formula:**
  - Linear: $CMRR = \vert A_d / A_{cm} \vert$
  - Logarithmic: $CMRR_{(dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_d / A_{cm} \vert$ (always use **20**, not 10).
- ✅ **Ideal Value:** $CMRR = \infty$ (dB) for an ideal op-amp.
- ✅ **Realistic Range:** 60 dB to 120 dB for commercial op-amps.
- ✅ **Master Output Equation:** $V_o = A_d V_d + A_{cm} V_{cm}$ — must be committed to memory verbatim.
- ✅ **Differential vs Common:** $V_d = V_1 - V_2$ and $V_{cm} = (V_1 + V_2)/2$.
- ✅ **Engineering Significance:** High CMRR is *critical* for instrumentation amplifiers, biomedical signal extraction, and any application where long cables pick up 50/60 Hz mains noise.
- ✅ **Degradation Factors:** Resistor mismatch, transistor parameter mismatch ($\beta$, $V_{BE}$), and finite tail current source output impedance *all* reduce CMRR.
- ✅ **Frequency Behavior:** CMRR is high in the midband but rolls off at high frequencies due to mismatched parasitic capacitances in the two halves of the diff-pair.
- ✅ **Common Exam Trap:** Using $\log_{10}$ coefficient as 10 instead of 20 (this is for **voltage** gain, not power).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Gain

In Analog Electronics, **Gain** is defined as the ratio of the magnitude (amplitude or power) of the output signal delivered by an amplifier to the magnitude of the input signal applied at its terminals. It quantifies the **amplifying ability** of an electronic circuit — essentially, *how much a signal is "boosted"* as it passes from input to output.

Mathematically, gain is a **dimensionless quantity** (when expressed as a pure ratio) and can take three primary forms depending on the parameter being scaled:

- **Voltage Gain ($A_v$)** — ratio of output voltage to input voltage
- **Current Gain ($A_i$)** — ratio of output current to input current
- **Power Gain ($A_p$)** — ratio of output power to input power

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBCPT304 – Module 2):**
> Gain is a foundational parameter used to characterize **BJT and FET small-signal amplifiers** in their three fundamental configurations: **Common Emitter (CE)**, **Common Base (CB)**, **Common Collector (CC)** for BJTs, and **Common Source (CS)**, **Common Drain (CD)**, **Common Gate (CG)** for FETs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Megaphone Intuition"

Imagine you are speaking softly into a **megaphone**. Your soft voice ($V_{in}$) enters the device, and a much louder version of your voice ($V_{out}$) comes out the other end. The **ratio** of the loud output to the soft input is, in essence, the **voltage gain** of the megaphone.

- If the output sound is **10× louder** than the input → Voltage Gain = **10**
- If the output sound is the **same loudness** but the device was efficient → Power Gain matters more

In electronics, the **amplifier acts as the megaphone**, and **Gain is the "loudness-multiplier"** of the signal. The unit **decibel (dB)** is simply a logarithmic way of expressing this multiplier — making very large or very small gains easier to handle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Reference Units:**
> - **Voltage Gain ($A_v$):** dimensionless (or expressed in **dB**)
> - **Current Gain ($A_i$):** dimensionless (or expressed in **dB**)
> - **Power Gain ($A_p$):** dimensionless (or expressed in **dB**)
> - **Standard reference:** **0 dB = unity gain (no amplification, no attenuation)**

### 1.3 Why is Gain Important in Engineering?

Gain dictates whether a circuit can successfully drive a **load**, overcome **signal losses** in transmission, boost **weak sensor signals** (e.g., from microphones, antennas, biomedical electrodes), and ensure **signal fidelity** in communication systems. In KTU examinations, you will routinely be asked to calculate the **small-signal gain** of CE, CB, and CC amplifiers using hybrid-π or T-models.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Types of Gain

### 2.1.1 Voltage Gain ($A_v$)

$$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$$

The voltage gain tells us **how much the voltage level** of the signal is amplified. A **negative sign** in the answer (e.g., $A_v = -100$) indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output — a hallmark of the **Common Emitter (CE)** configuration.

### 2.1.2 Current Gain ($A_i$)

$$A_i = \frac{I_{out}}{I_{in}}$$

The current gain represents the **current amplification factor**. In a BJT, the small-signal current gain is closely tied to the **β (beta)** of the transistor.

### 2.1.3 Power Gain ($A_p$)

$$A_p = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{out} \cdot I_{out}}{V_{in} \cdot I_{in}} = A_v \cdot A_i$$

Power gain combines both voltage and current amplification. It is the most **practically relevant** metric, since it directly relates to the **energy transfer capability** of the amplifier.

## 2.2 Decibel Representation of Gain

In real-world engineering, gains can range from **fractions (0.001)** to **millions (1,000,000)**. Handling such wide ranges is cumbersome, so we use the **decibel (dB)** scale, a logarithmic compression of the gain ratio.

| **Quantity** | **Decibel Formula** | **Why 20 vs 10?** |
|--------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Voltage Gain in dB | $A_v(dB) = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_v \vert$ | Voltage squared ∝ Power |
| Current Gain in dB | $A_i(dB) = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_i \vert$ | Current squared ∝ Power |
| Power Gain in dB | $A_p(dB) = 10 \log_{10}(A_p)$ | Direct power ratio |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Conversion Mnemonic:**
> - A gain of **×10** in voltage → **+20 dB**
> - A gain of **×10** in power → **+10 dB**
> - A gain of **×100** in voltage → **+40 dB**
> - A gain of **×2** in voltage → **+6 dB** (since $20\log_{10} 2 \approx 6.02$)

## 2.3 Gain in BJT Amplifier Configurations (Hybrid-π Model)

Using the **small-signal hybrid-π equivalent circuit**, where:
- $r_\pi = \beta \cdot r_e$ (input resistance looking into the base)
- $g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T}$ (transconductance, where $V_T \approx 26\,\text{mV}$ at room temp)
- $R_C$ = collector resistor, $R_E$ = emitter resistor

### 2.3.1 Common Emitter (CE) Amplifier

**With emitter resistor $R_E$ bypassed by capacitor $C_E$:**

$$A_v = -g_m \cdot R_C = -\frac{R_C}{r_e}$$

$$A_i = \beta$$

$$A_p = \beta \cdot \frac{R_C}{r_e}$$

**Without bypass capacitor (unbypassed $R_E$):**

$$A_v = -\frac{g_m \cdot R_C}{1 + g_m \cdot R_E} \approx -\frac{R_C}{R_E} \quad (\text{if } g_m R_E \gg 1)$$

### 2.3.2 Common Base (CB) Amplifier

$$A_v = g_m \cdot R_C = \frac{R_C}{r_e} \quad (\text{non-inverting, no phase shift})$$

$$A_i = \alpha = \frac{\beta}{\beta + 1} \quad (\text{close to, but less than 1})$$

### 2.3.3 Common Collector (CC) Amplifier — Emitter Follower

$$A_v = \frac{g_m \cdot R_E}{1 + g_m \cdot R_E} \approx 1 \quad (\text{unity gain, non-inverting})$$

$$A_i = \beta + 1 \quad (\text{high current gain})$$

## 2.4 Gain in FET Amplifier Configurations

For **JFET / MOSFET small-signal models**, the gate draws negligible current, so $A_i$ is theoretically infinite. Focus is on **voltage gain**.

### 2.4.1 Common Source (CS) Amplifier

$$A_v = -g_m \cdot R_D \quad (\text{180° phase inversion})$$

### 2.4.2 Common Drain (CD) Amplifier — Source Follower

$$A_v = \frac{g_m \cdot R_S}{1 + g_m \cdot R_S} \approx 1 \quad (\text{unity gain follower})$$

### 2.4.3 Common Gate (CG) Amplifier

$$A_v = g_m \cdot R_D \quad (\text{non-inverting})$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Configuration** | **Voltage Gain $A_v$** | **Current Gain $A_i$** | **Phase** | **Input $Z$** | **Output $Z$** |
|-------------------|------------------------|-------------------------|-----------|----------------|------------------|
| CE (BJT) | $-R_C / r_e$ | $\beta$ | 180° | Medium ($r_\pi$) | Medium-High ($R_C$) |
| CB (BJT) | $R_C / r_e$ | $\alpha \approx 1$ | 0° | Very Low ($r_e$) | High ($R_C$) |
| CC (BJT) | $\approx 1$ | $\beta + 1$ | 0° | High ($r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E$) | Low ($R_E \parallel \text{etc.}$) |
| CS (FET) | $-g_m R_D$ | — | 180° | Very High | Medium |
| CD (FET) | $\approx 1$ | — | 0° | Very High | Low |
| CG (FET) | $g_m R_D$ | — | 0° | Low ($1/g_m$) | High ($R_D$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Rule of Three" for Amplifier Configurations:**
> - **Inverting + High $A_v$** → Common Emitter / Common Source
> - **Unity Gain Follower** → Common Collector / Common Drain
> - **Non-Inverting + Low Input Z** → Common Base / Common Gate

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CE amplifiers** dominate **audio pre-amplifiers**, RF front-ends, and instrumentation amplifiers.
- **CC (emitter follower)** stages are used as **buffers** between high-impedance sources and low-impedance loads (e.g., speaker drivers).
- **CB amplifiers** are excellent at **high frequencies** and are used in RF and microwave circuits due to low input capacitance effects.
- **CS amplifiers** are the **workhorse of CMOS analog IC design** (op-amps, mixers, transimpedance stages).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Voltage Gain of CE Amplifier (BJT, Emitter Bypassed)

**Step 1 — Start with the small-signal hybrid-π model of the BJT CE amplifier.**

The collector current is controlled by the base-emitter voltage:

$$i_c = g_m \cdot v_{be}$$

The output voltage at the collector is:

$$v_{out} = -i_c \cdot R_C = -g_m \cdot v_{be} \cdot R_C$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that $v_{in} = v_{be}$ (since the emitter is grounded through the bypass capacitor).**

$$v_{in} = v_{be}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio $v_{out} / v_{in}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v &= \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \frac{-g_m \cdot v_{be} \cdot R_C}{v_{be}} \\
A_v &= -g_m \cdot R_C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $g_m = 1 / r_e$ to express in terms of $r_e$.**

$$A_v = -\frac{R_C}{r_e}$$

The **negative sign** indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output — the signature of a CE amplifier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** "CE gives inverted Convenience of signal." The minus sign is a **feature, not a bug** — it is exploited in **differential amplifiers** and **push-pull output stages**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Voltage Gain of CE Amplifier with Unbypassed $R_E$

**Step 1 — Include $R_E$ in the small-signal loop.**

The base-emitter voltage now splits between $r_e$ and $R_E$:

$$v_{in} = i_b \cdot r_\pi + i_e \cdot R_E$$

Since $i_e = (\beta + 1) \cdot i_b$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{in} &= i_b \cdot r_\pi + (\beta + 1) \cdot i_b \cdot R_E \\
v_{in} &= i_b \left[ r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Output voltage is still collector-based.**

$$v_{out} = -i_c \cdot R_C = -\beta \cdot i_b \cdot R_C$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v &= \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \frac{-\beta \cdot i_b \cdot R_C}{i_b \left[ r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E \right]} \\
A_v &= \frac{-\beta \cdot R_C}{r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the approximation $r_\pi = (\beta + 1) r_e$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v &= \frac{-\beta \cdot R_C}{(\beta + 1) r_e + (\beta + 1) R_E} \\
A_v &= \frac{-\beta \cdot R_C}{(\beta + 1)(r_e + R_E)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $R_E \gg r_e$ and $\beta \gg 1$:

$$A_v \approx -\frac{R_C}{R_E}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:**
> The unbypassed $R_E$ **drastically reduces gain** but provides **negative feedback** that stabilizes the gain against variations in β and temperature. This is the foundation of the **emitter-bias stabilization** technique.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Common Collector (Emitter Follower) Voltage Gain

**Step 1 — Output is taken at the emitter.**

$$v_{out} = i_e \cdot R_E = (\beta + 1) \cdot i_b \cdot R_E$$

**Step 2 — Input is across base to ground.**

$$v_{in} = i_b \cdot r_\pi + (\beta + 1) \cdot i_b \cdot R_E = i_b \left[ r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E \right]$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v &= \frac{(\beta + 1) i_b R_E}{i_b \left[ r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E \right]} \\
A_v &= \frac{(\beta + 1) R_E}{r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $r_\pi = (\beta + 1) r_e$:**

$$A_v = \frac{(\beta + 1) R_E}{(\beta + 1) r_e + (\beta + 1) R_E} = \frac{R_E}{r_e + R_E}$$

For $R_E \gg r_e$:

$$A_v \approx 1$$

The output **"follows"** the input — hence the name **emitter follower**.

---

## 3.4 Decibel Conversion Worked Example

**Problem:** An amplifier has a voltage gain of $A_v = 500$. Express in dB.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v(\text{dB}) &= 20 \log_{10}\vert 500 \vert \\
&= 20 \left[ \log_{10}(5) + \log_{10}(100) \right] \\
&= 20 \left[ 0.6990 + 2 \right] \\
&= 20 \times 2.6990 \\
&= 53.98 \,\text{dB} \approx 54 \,\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Multi-Stage Gain (Cascade Rule)

When amplifiers are cascaded, **total gain is the product** of individual gains (in linear scale) and **sum** (in dB):

$$A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} \cdot A_{v3} \cdots A_{vn}$$

$$A_{v,\text{total}}(\text{dB}) = A_{v1}(\text{dB}) + A_{v2}(\text{dB}) + A_{v3}(\text{dB}) + \cdots$$

**Example:** Two cascaded stages with $A_{v1} = 20$ and $A_{v2} = 50$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{v,\text{total}} &= 20 \times 50 = 1000 \\
A_{v,\text{total}}(\text{dB}) &= 20\log_{10}(1000) = 60 \,\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Concept Map of "Gain" in Amplifiers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Signal] --> B[Amplifier Stage]
    B --> C[Output Signal]
    B --> D[Gain Calculation]
    D --> D1[Voltage Gain Av]
    D --> D2[Current Gain Ai]
    D --> D3[Power Gain Ap]
    D1 --> E[Convert to dB: 20 log10 Av]
    D2 --> F[Convert to dB: 20 log10 Ai]
    D3 --> G[Convert to dB: 10 log10 Ap]
    B --> H[Configuration Type]
    H --> H1[CE: High Av, 180 deg phase]
    H --> H2[CB: Non-inverting, low Zin]
    H --> H3[CC: Unity gain, buffer]
    H --> H4[CS: High Av, 180 deg phase]
    H --> H5[CD: Source follower]
    H --> H6[CG: Non-inverting, low Zin]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow: Signal Flow Through a CE Amplifier (BJT)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[Vin: AC Source] --> R1[Base Bias Network]
    R1 --> BJT[Q1: BJT CE Stage]
    BJT --> RC[Rc: Collector Resistor]
    RC --> OUT[Vout: At Collector]
    BJT --> RE[Re: Emitter Resistor]
    RE --> CE[Ce: Bypass Capacitor]
    CE --> GND1[Ground]
    BJT --> GND2[Ground via Ve]
    BJT --> X[Small-Signal Model]
    X --> X1[Input Loop: Vbe across rpi]
    X --> X2[Output Loop: ic = gm vbe]
    X2 --> X3[Vout = -gm Rc vbe]
    X3 --> Y[Av = -gm Rc]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture: Decibel Conversion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Linear Voltage Gain] --> Q1[Take Modulus]
    Q1 --> Q2[Multiply by 20]
    Q2 --> Q3[Apply log10]
    Q3 --> R1[Result in dB]
    P2[Linear Power Gain] --> S1[Apply log10]
    S1 --> S2[Multiply by 10]
    S2 --> R2[Result in dB]
    P3[Linear Current Gain] --> T1[Take Modulus]
    T1 --> T2[Multiply by 20]
    T2 --> T3[Apply log10]
    T3 --> R3[Result in dB]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison: Three BJT Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CE[Common Emitter CE]
        CE1[Vin at Base] --> CE2[Q1 with Emitter Grounded]
        CE2 --> CE3[Vout at Collector]
        CE3 --> CE4[High Av, Inverted, Medium Zin]
    end
    subgraph CB[Common Base CB]
        CB1[Vin at Emitter] --> CB2[Q1 with Base Grounded]
        CB2 --> CB3[Vout at Collector]
        CB3 --> CB4[Av approx 1 plus Non-Inverted Low Zin]
    end
    subgraph CC[Common Collector CC]
        CC1[Vin at Base] --> CC2[Q1 with Collector Grounded]
        CC2 --> CC3[Vout at Emitter]
        CC3 --> CC4[Unity Av, High Zin, Low Zout]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define voltage gain and current gain of an amplifier. What is the relationship between voltage gain, current gain, and power gain?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Voltage Gain ($A_v$):** It is the ratio of output voltage to input voltage, $A_v = V_{out} / V_{in}$.
- **Current Gain ($A_i$):** It is the ratio of output current to input current, $A_i = I_{out} / I_{in}$.
- **Relationship:**

$$A_p = A_v \times A_i = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$$

Power gain is the product of voltage gain and current gain.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of expressing gain in decibels? Convert a voltage gain of 200 into decibels.**

**Model Answer:**

Expressing gain in decibels (dB) compresses the wide range of possible gain values into a manageable logarithmic scale. It also allows **multiplication of gains (cascaded stages) to be replaced by simple addition in dB**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v(\text{dB}) &= 20 \log_{10}(200) \\
&= 20 \times 2.3010 \\
&= 46.02 \,\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the voltage gain of a **Common Emitter (CE) amplifier with emitter resistor bypassed**, using the small-signal hybrid-π model of a BJT. Mention the significance of the negative sign. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CE amplifier, $\beta = 100$, $r_e = 25\,\Omega$, $R_C = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, and $R_E = 1.2\,\text{k}\Omega$ (unbypassed). Calculate:
  (i) Voltage gain with $R_E$ bypassed
  (ii) Voltage gain with $R_E$ unbypassed
  (iii) Power gain in dB (assuming $A_i = \beta = 100$) in the bypassed case **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Using the small-signal hybrid-π model of the BJT:

- Output voltage: $V_{out} = -i_c \cdot R_C = -g_m \cdot v_{be} \cdot R_C$
- Input voltage: $V_{in} = v_{be}$ (emitter grounded via bypass capacitor)

$$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -g_m \cdot R_C = -\frac{R_C}{r_e}$$

The **negative sign** indicates a **180° phase reversal** between the input and output signals — a defining characteristic of the CE configuration.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Voltage gain with $R_E$ bypassed:**

$$
A_v = -\frac{R_C}{r_e} = -\frac{4700}{25} = -188
$$

**(ii) Voltage gain with $R_E$ unbypassed:**

$$
A_v = -\frac{R_C}{R_E} = -\frac{4700}{1200} = -3.917
$$

(Note: We use the approximation $A_v \approx -R_C / R_E$ since $R_E \gg r_e$.)

**(iii) Power gain in dB (bypassed case):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_p &= \vert A_v \vert \times A_i = 188 \times 100 = 18{,}800 \\
A_p(\text{dB}) &= 10 \log_{10}(18800) \\
&= 10 \times 4.2742 \\
&= 42.74 \,\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the voltage gain of a **Common Collector (CC) amplifier (emitter follower)**. Explain why it is called a "follower." **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Common Source (CS) MOSFET amplifier** has $g_m = 2\,\text{mA/V}$ and $R_D = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$. Calculate:
  (i) Voltage gain
  (ii) Voltage gain in dB
  (iii) Power delivered to a $10\,\text{k}\Omega$ load if input voltage is $20\,\text{mV}$ peak **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

For the CC amplifier (emitter follower):

- Output voltage: $V_{out} = i_e \cdot R_E = (\beta+1) i_b R_E$
- Input voltage: $V_{in} = i_b \left[ r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E \right]$

$$
A_v = \frac{(\beta+1) R_E}{r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E} = \frac{R_E}{r_e + R_E} \approx 1
$$

It is called a **"follower"** because the output voltage at the emitter **"follows"** the input voltage at the base — both in **magnitude (approximately equal)** and in **phase (zero phase shift)**.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Voltage gain:**

$$A_v = -g_m \cdot R_D = -(2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (5 \times 10^{3}) = -10$$

**(ii) Voltage gain in dB:**

$$A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(10) = 20 \,\text{dB}$$

**(iii) Power delivered to $10\,\text{k}\Omega$ load:**

The output voltage (assuming load is AC-coupled and does not affect gain in the ideal case) is:

$$
V_{out} = A_v \cdot V_{in} = -10 \times 20\,\text{mV} = -200\,\text{mV (peak)}
$$

Power delivered to the load:

$$
P_L = \frac{V_{out}^2}{2 \cdot R_L} = \frac{(0.2)^2}{2 \times 10000} = \frac{0.04}{20000} = 2 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{W} = 2\,\mu\text{W}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT use $A_p(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(A_p)$** — that formula is for voltage/current only. For **power gain, always use 10**.
> - **Do NOT forget the negative sign** in CE/CS gain — the 180° phase shift is part of the answer, not an optional annotation.
> - **Do NOT confuse the dB formulas:** $20 \log$ is for V/I (field quantities), $10 \log$ is for P (energy quantity).
> - **In the unbypassed CE case**, do not use $A_v = -R_C / r_e$ — that is only valid when $R_E$ is bypassed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Gain = Output / Input** (for V, I, or P).
- **Power gain** = product of **voltage gain** and **current gain**.
- **dB Formula Triad:** $20 \log$ for V/I, $10 \log$ for P. **Memorize this triad.**
- **CE / CS:** High voltage gain, **180° phase inversion**, medium-to-high input impedance.
- **CB / CG:** Non-inverting, low input impedance, used at **high frequencies**.
- **CC / CD:** **Unity voltage gain follower**, high input Z, low output Z, used as a **buffer**.
- **Bypass capacitor $C_E$:** Increases $A_v$ but reduces **stability** — trade-off in design.
- **Cascaded amplifiers:** Gains **multiply in linear** domain, **add in dB** domain.
- **FET amplifiers:** $A_i$ is theoretically **infinite** (gate draws no DC current); focus is on $A_v$.
- **KTU hot keywords to memorize:** *hybrid-π model, transconductance $g_m$, $r_\pi$, $r_e = \alpha / g_m$, decibel conversion, emitter follower, source follower, cascade rule.*

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Tick before exam):**
> - [ ] I can derive $A_v$ for CE, CB, CC amplifier from the hybrid-π model.
> - [ ] I can convert any linear gain to dB and back.
> - [ ] I know why unbypassed $R_E$ reduces gain (negative feedback).
> - [ ] I can list the phase relationship for each BJT configuration.
> - [ ] I know when to use the FET formulas vs. BJT formulas.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Offset Voltage and Offset Current
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCPT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Offset Voltage and Offset Current # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Input Offset Voltage ($V_{io}$) ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Input Offset Voltage** is defined as the small differential DC voltage that must be applied externally between the inverting and non-inverting input terminals of an operational amplifier to force the output voltage to exactly **zero volts** (when no input signal is present). In an *ideal* op-amp, the output should be zero when both inputs are grounded. However, due to unavoidable **mismatches** in the internal transistor pairs (differential input stage), a non-zero output appears. The voltage required to nullify this is the input offset voltage. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** $V_{io}$ is the differential input voltage that results in zero output voltage. Typical magnitude for general-purpose op-amps (e.g., $\mu$A741) is in the range of **1 mV to 15 mV**. ### Conceptual Analogy Imagine two perfectly matched weighing scales side by side. In an ideal world, both should read **exactly zero** when nothing is placed on them. But because the springs or lever arms are never manufactured to be *mathematically identical*, one scale reads "+2 grams" and the other reads "-1 gram" even when empty. In an op-amp, the two input transistors act like these two scales. **Offset Voltage** is the tiny extra voltage you have to apply at the input to "re-balance" them so that the output reads zero. ## 1.2 Input Offset Current ($I_{io}$) ### Formal Definition **Input Offset Current** is defined as the algebraic difference between the two input bias currents flowing into the inverting and non-inverting terminals of the op-amp. The BJT input stage of an op-amp requires a small base current to keep transistors biased ON. Since the two input transistors cannot have *exactly* identical $\beta$ values, the base currents $I_{B1}$ and $I_{B2}$ differ slightly. ### Conceptual Analogy Think of two water taps connected to the same tank. To keep both flowing at the same rate, the tank must supply a tiny base current to each. But because the tap mechanisms (transistor $\beta$'s) aren't identical, one tap demands slightly more water than the other. The **difference** between these two base currents is the offset current. ## 1.3 Input Bias Current ($I_B$) The **average** of the two input bias currents flowing into the op-amp's input terminals is called the input bias current. > [!NOTE] > All three parameters ($V_{io}$, $I_{io}$, $I_B$) are critical because they introduce a **DC error** at the output even when the input signal is zero. This is especially problematic in **DC-coupled**, **precision**, and **instrumentation** applications. --- ### Mandatory Markdown Callouts Summary > [!NOTE] > **Key Symbols (KTU Convention):** > - $V_{io}$ → Input Offset Voltage > - $I_{io}$ → Input Offset Current > - $I_B$ → Input Bias Current > - $T_c(V_{io})$ → Thermal drift coefficient of offset voltage (typically $\mu$V/$^\circ$C) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** This topic almost always appears as a sub-part (7 marks) in a larger 14-mark question on op-amp non-idealities or DC error analysis. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mathematical Origin of Offset Voltage The op-amp input stage is a **differential pair** (long-tailed pair) using matched BJTs or MOSFETs. The collector currents $I_{C1}$ and $I_{C2}$ of this pair are nominally equal when both bases are at the same potential. However, due to: - Mismatch in base-emitter voltages ($\Delta V_{BE}$) - Mismatch in current gains ($\Delta \beta$) - Mismatch in collector resistors ($\Delta R_C$) - Mismatch in load resistors A small differential DC offset appears at the output. ## 2.2 The Three Key DC Error Parameters — Definitions Let: - $I_{B1}$ = DC bias current flowing into the **non-inverting** terminal - $I_{B2}$ = DC bias current flowing into the **inverting** terminal Then: $$\boxed{I_B = \dfrac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}}$$ $$\boxed{I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert}$$ The direction (sign) of $I_{io}$ is not specified; only the magnitude is specified on datasheets. ## 2.3 Output Error Voltage Due to Offsets When the op-amp is connected in a closed-loop configuration (say, an inverting amplifier with feedback resistor $R_f$ and input resistor $R_1$), the **total output offset voltage** is: $$V_{o(\text{offset})} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \cdot V_{io} + I_{io} \cdot R_f$$ The first term is the offset voltage amplified by the **DC noise gain** (which equals the closed-loop gain for a non-inverting configuration). The second term is the offset current flowing through the feedback resistor. > [!TIP] > The factor $\left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$ is the **Noise Gain** of the op-amp. It is *not* the signal gain; it represents how DC errors get amplified. ## 2.4 Temperature Drift (Thermal Coefficients) Offset parameters change with temperature. Datasheets specify the **drift coefficients**: $$\dfrac{\Delta V_{io}}{\Delta T} \quad \text{(in } \mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C)}$$ $$\dfrac{\Delta I_{io}}{\Delta T} \quad \text{(in pA}/^\circ\text{C)}$$ The total offset voltage at any operating temperature $T$ is: $$V_{io}(T) = V_{io}(25^\circ\text{C}) + \dfrac{\Delta V_{io}}{\Delta T} \cdot (T - 25)$$ ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---------|-------------|---------------| | 1 | $I_B = \dfrac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$ | Input bias current | nA (BJT), pA (FET) | | 2 | $I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$ | Input offset current | nA (BJT), pA (FET) | | 3 | $V_{o(\text{error})} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right)V_{io} + I_{io}R_f$ | Total output offset voltage | V or mV | | 4 | $V_{io}(T) = V_{io}(25) + \dfrac{\Delta V_{io}}{\Delta T}(T-25)$ | Offset voltage at temperature $T$ | V or $\mu$V | | 5 | $R_{\text{comp}} = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$ | Offset compensating resistor | $\Omega$ | | 6 | $V_{io} \approx \Delta V_{BE} + I_B \cdot \Delta R_C$ | Source of offset (input stage) | V | ## 2.6 Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world Applications Affected by Offset:** > - **Strain gauge bridges** — small mV signals buried under huge offset > - **Thermocouple amplifiers** — DC-coupled precision > - **Medical ECG/EEG front-ends** — patient safety demands zero DC drift > - **ADC driver circuits** — offset consumes dynamic range > - **Integrator circuits** — offset causes **slew** (ramp) at the output, eventually saturating the op-amp --- ## 2.7 Offset Compensation Techniques (Theory) Three standard KTU-approved techniques are: 1. **Offset-null potentiometer method** — A 10 k$\Omega$ pot connected between pins 1 and 5 of an $\mu$A741, with wiper to $V_{EE}$. Adjusted at room temperature to null output. 2. **Bias-current compensation resistor** ($R_{\text{comp}}$) — A resistor equal to $R_1 \mid\mid R_f$ placed in series with the **non-inverting** input. This makes the DC source impedance seen by both inputs equal, minimizing the effect of $I_{io}$. 3. **Chopper stabilization / Auto-zero** — Used in precision op-amps (e.g., chopper-stabilized ICs) to periodically null the offset using switched-capacitor techniques. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Output Offset Voltage in an Inverting Amplifier ### Circuit Setup Consider an inverting amplifier: - Input resistor $R_1$ - Feedback resistor $R_f$ - Non-inverting terminal connected to ground through $R_{\text{comp}}$ - Internal offset sources: $V_{io}$ (between inputs) and bias currents $I_{B1}$, $I_{B2}$ ### Step 1: Apply KCL at the Inverting Node Let $V^-$ be the voltage at the inverting terminal. The current from input source $V_{in}$ is zero (we are finding output when $V_{in} = 0$, i.e., output *due to offsets alone*). $$\dfrac{0 - V^-}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_f} = I_{B2}$$ (where $I_{B2}$ is the bias current entering the inverting node from the op-amp) ### Step 2: Apply KCL at the Non-Inverting Node The non-inverting terminal is at potential $V^+ = 0 \cdot V$ through $R_{\text{comp}}$, and the bias current $I_{B1}$ enters this node. $$\dfrac{0 - V^+}{R_{\text{comp}}} = I_{B1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V^+ = -I_{B1} \cdot R_{\text{comp}}$$ ### Step 3: Relate $V^-$ to $V^+$ via Offset Voltage By definition of input offset voltage: $$V^- - V^+ = V_{io} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V^- = V_{io} + V^+ = V_{io} - I_{B1} R_{\text{comp}}$$ ### Step 4: Solve for $V_{out}$ from Step 1 Substitute $V^-$ into the KCL equation: $$\dfrac{0 - (V_{io} - I_{B1} R_{\text{comp}})}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_{out} - (V_{io} - I_{B1} R_{\text{comp}})}{R_f} = I_{B2}$$ Multiply through and solve (assume $I_{B1} \approx I_B + I_{io}/2$ and $I_{B2} \approx I_B - I_{io}/2$): $$V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{io} + I_{io} R_f + I_B \left(R_f - R_{\text{comp}} - \dfrac{R_f \cdot R_{\text{comp}}}{R_1}\right)$$ ### Step 5: Choose $R_{\text{comp}}$ to Eliminate $I_B$ Term The term containing $I_B$ vanishes when: $$R_f = R_{\text{comp}} + \dfrac{R_f R_{\text{comp}}}{R_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_{\text{comp}} = \dfrac{R_1 R_f}{R_1 + R_f} = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$$ This is the famous result: **the bias-compensation resistor must equal the parallel combination of $R_1$ and $R_f$**. ### Step 6: Final Compact Expression With $R_{\text{comp}} = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$, the output offset due to **residual errors** simplifies to: $$\boxed{V_{o(\text{offset})} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{io} + I_{io} R_f}$$ This is the canonical KTU formula for output error analysis. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) ### Problem An inverting amplifier uses $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$. The op-amp specifications are: - $V_{io} = 5 \text{ mV}$ - $I_{io} = 50 \text{ nA}$ **Find:** (a) The required bias-compensation resistor $R_{\text{comp}}$. (b) The total output offset voltage $V_{o(\text{offset})}$. ### Solution #### Part (a) $$R_{\text{comp}} = R_1 \mid\mid R_f = \dfrac{10\text{k} \times 100\text{k}}{10\text{k} + 100\text{k}} = \dfrac{10^6}{110}\text{k} \approx 9.09 \text{ k}\Omega$$ > Choose the nearest standard value: **$R_{\text{comp}} = 9.1 \text{ k}\Omega$** #### Part (b) Compute the noise gain: $$1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \dfrac{100\text{k}}{10\text{k}} = 11$$ Compute the offset-current error term: $$I_{io} \cdot R_f = 50 \text{ nA} \times 100 \text{ k}\Omega = 50 \times 10^{-9} \times 100 \times 10^{3} = 5 \text{ mV}$$ Now substitute: $$V_{o(\text{offset})} = (11)(5 \text{ mV}) + 5 \text{ mV} = 55 \text{ mV} + 5 \text{ mV} = 60 \text{ mV}$$ > **Final Answer:** $V_{o(\text{offset})} = \mathbf{60 \text{ mV}}$ ### Verification of Error Magnitude For a signal gain of $-10$, if the input signal is 100 mV, the desired output is $-1$ V, but the output is *additionally* shifted by $\pm 60$ mV due to offsets — a **6% error** even at maximum signal. This shows why offset compensation is critical in precision applications. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Typical Op-Amp Offset Specifications | Op-Amp Type | $V_{io}$ (max) | $I_B$ (max) | $I_{io}$ (max) | Drift ($\mu$V/$^\circ$C) | |-------------|----------------|-------------|----------------|--------------------------| | $\mu$A741 (general purpose) | 6 mV | 500 nA | 200 nA | 15 | | LM324 (quad, single supply) | 9 mV | 500 nA | 200 nA | 30 | | OP-07 (precision) | 150 $\mu$V | 7 nA | 6 nA | 1.8 | | AD8552 (chopper) | 5 $\mu$V | 50 pA | 20 pA | 0.05 | | TL082 (JFET input) | 15 mV | 200 pA | 100 pA | 10 | > [!TIP] > For DC-coupled precision designs, always prefer **OP-07** or chopper-stabilized op-amps. The $\mu$A741 has 1000× worse offset than OP-07! --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Thermal Drift in Offset Voltage The base-emitter voltage of a BJT has a temperature coefficient of approximately $-2$ mV/$^\circ$C. For a well-matched differential pair, this *common-mode* drift cancels. However, the **mismatch** $\Delta V_{BE}$ produces a residual drift: $$\dfrac{d V_{io}}{dT} = \dfrac{d(\Delta V_{BE})}{dT} + \dfrac{V_T \cdot \Delta R_C / R_C}{T}$$ For typical mismatches of $\Delta V_{BE} \approx 1$ mV and $\Delta R_C / R_C \approx 1\%$, the drift is on the order of **3–15 $\mu$V/$^\circ$C**. The total offset at any temperature $T$: $$V_{io}(T) = V_{io}(T_0) + \left(\dfrac{\Delta V_{io}}{\Delta T}\right) \cdot (T - T_0)$$ This linear approximation is valid over a limited range (e.g., 0 to 70$^\circ$C for commercial grade). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Sources of Op-Amp Offset The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the internal sources of offset voltage and offset current in a typical op-amp input stage, and how they propagate to the output. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Differential Input Stage
Matched BJT Pair"] --> B["Mismatch Sources"] B --> B1["Delta VBE Mismatch
1 to 5 mV typical"] B --> B2["Delta Beta Mismatch
between Q1 and Q2"] B --> B3["Delta RC Mismatch
Load Resistor Tolerance"] B1 --> C["Resulting Input Offset Voltage Vio"] B2 --> D["Resulting Offset Current Iio"] B3 --> C C --> E["Noise Gain Block
1 + Rf by R1"] D --> F["Feedback Resistor Rf"] E --> G["Output Error Vo offset"] F --> G H["Input Bias Current IB"] --> F H --> E G --> I["Final Output Node
with DC Error"] J["Temperature Variation dT"] --> C J --> D J --> H ``` > [!NOTE] > This diagram maps how the **internal mismatches** combine to produce the externally measurable parameters $V_{io}$ and $I_{io}$, which then translate to output error through the noise gain and feedback network. --- ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Offset Compensation Network The diagram below shows the functional placement of the bias-compensation resistor $R_{\text{comp}}$ and the offset-null potentiometer in a standard inverting amplifier. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IN["INPUT SIDE"] A["Vin Source"] --> R1["R1 = 10 kohm"] end R1 --> N1["Inverting Node
Node A"] N1 --> Rf["Rf = 100 kohm"] Rf --> OUT["Output Node Vout"] subgraph NON["NON INVERTING SIDE"] GND["Ground"] --> Rcomp["Rcomp = 9.09 kohm
Bias Compensating"] end Rcomp --> N2["Non Inverting Node
Node B"] subgraph NULL["OFFSET NULL CIRCUIT"] VEE["VEE Supply"] --> POT["10 kohm Potentiometer"] POT --> N1 POT --> N2 end OP["Ideal Op Amp Core
with Vio and Iio sources"] --> OUT N2 --> OP N1 --> OP ``` --- ## 4.3 Signal Flow: How Offset Error Reaches the Output ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Internal Vio Source
1 to 15 mV"] --> B["Amplified by Noise Gain
1 + Rf by R1"] C["Internal Iio Source
50 to 500 nA"] --> D["Flows Through Rf
Develops Iio x Rf"] B --> E["Sum at Output Node"] D --> E F["IB Mismatch Term
Suppressed by Rcomp"] --> E E --> G["Final Vo offset
60 to 200 mV typical"] ``` --- ## 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Choosing Offset Compensation ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Application Type?"] --> B["DC Coupled Precision?"] A --> C["AC Coupled?"] A --> D["General Purpose?"] B --> E["Use Precision Op Amp
OP-07 or Chopper Type"] C --> F["Use AC Coupling Capacitor
Blocks DC Offset"] D --> G["Use General Purpose Op Amp
muA741 with Null Pot"] E --> H["Add Bias Comp Resistor Rcomp"] F --> I["No DC Offset Concern
Bias Comp Optional"] G --> H H --> J["Adjust Null Pot at 25 deg C"] J --> K["Verify Drift Over Temperature"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper] **Define input offset voltage and input offset current of an op-amp. Mention their typical values for the $\mu$A741.** **Model Answer:** **Input Offset Voltage ($V_{io}$):** It is the small differential DC voltage that must be applied between the inverting and non-inverting input terminals of an op-amp to make the output voltage zero. It arises due to mismatch in the input differential pair transistors. > **Typical value for $\mu$A741:** $V_{io} = 1$ mV to 6 mV **Input Offset Current ($I_{io}$):** It is the algebraic difference between the two DC bias currents flowing into the inverting and non-inverting input terminals of the op-amp. > **Typical value for $\mu$A741:** $I_{io} = 20$ nA to 200 nA Both parameters cause a non-zero DC output even when the input signal is zero, which is undesirable in DC-coupled precision applications. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **Why is it necessary to compensate for input bias current in an op-amp circuit? How is it achieved?** **Model Answer:** The input bias current $I_B$ flowing through the source resistances develops a voltage that is amplified and appears as an unwanted DC offset at the output. Without compensation, the output cannot be exactly zero even when no input is applied. **Compensation Method:** A resistor $R_{\text{comp}}$ is connected in series with the **non-inverting** input terminal. Its value is chosen to be equal to the parallel combination of the input and feedback resistors of the inverting configuration: $$R_{\text{comp}} = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$$ This makes the DC resistance seen from both inputs equal, so the bias-current-induced voltages cancel each other at the output. --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified Past Paper] **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the concept of input offset voltage in an operational amplifier. Discuss the factors that cause it. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** For an inverting amplifier with $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_f = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, and op-amp specifications $V_{io} = 2 \text{ mV}$, $I_{io} = 100 \text{ nA}$, calculate (i) the bias-compensating resistor value, and (ii) the total output offset voltage. --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) Solution **Input Offset Voltage Definition:** The input offset voltage $V_{io}$ is the differential input voltage that must be applied between the two input terminals of an op-amp to make its output voltage exactly zero (when the op-amp is in linear operation with feedback applied). **Circuit Diagram Description:** ``` +VCC | | +----|----+ | In+ | | \ | Vin-+ \ | Differential | ---+-|---- Vout | In- | | / | +---|---+ | -VEE ``` **Causes of Input Offset Voltage:** 1. **Mismatch in $V_{BE}$** of the two input transistors — even with identical geometry, microscopic doping differences create $\Delta V_{BE}$ of 1–5 mV. 2. **Mismatch in current gain $\beta$** — different $\beta$ between the two input transistors leads to unequal base currents. 3. **Mismatch in load resistors** $R_C$ — fabrication tolerances of typically $\pm 1\%$ create imbalance. 4. **Mismatch in current source biasing** — the tail current source has finite output impedance that varies between halves of the differential pair. 5. **Temperature gradients** across the chip — non-uniform heating causes differential $V_{BE}$ drift. These mismatches appear as a small differential input voltage that gets amplified, producing a non-zero output even when $V_{in} = 0$. #### Part (b) Solution **Given:** $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_f = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{io} = 2 \text{ mV}$, $I_{io} = 100 \text{ nA}$ **(i) Bias-compensating resistor:** $$R_{\text{comp}} = R_1 \mid\mid R_f = \dfrac{1\text{k} \times 10\text{k}}{1\text{k} + 10\text{k}} = \dfrac{10}{11}\text{k}\Omega \approx 909 \text{ }\Omega$$ > **Answer:** $R_{\text{comp}} \approx 909 \text{ }\Omega$ (use **910 $\Omega$** standard value) **(ii) Total output offset voltage:** First, compute the noise gain: $$1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \dfrac{10\text{k}}{1\text{k}} = 11$$ Compute each error term separately: $$\text{Term 1: } \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{io} = 11 \times 2 \text{ mV} = 22 \text{ mV}$$ $$\text{Term 2: } I_{io} R_f = 100 \text{ nA} \times 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 10^{-7} \times 10^4 = 1 \text{ mV}$$ Sum the two terms: $$V_{o(\text{offset})} = 22 \text{ mV} + 1 \text{ mV} = 23 \text{ mV}$$ > **Final Answer:** $V_{o(\text{offset})} = \mathbf{23 \text{ mV}}$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Past Paper] **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the terms input bias current and input offset current with suitable expressions. What is the effect of temperature on these parameters? **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A non-inverting amplifier uses $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 9 \text{ k}\Omega$. The op-amp has $V_{io} = 3 \text{ mV}$ and $I_{io} = 50 \text{ nA}$. Calculate the total output offset voltage and the value of the bias-compensating resistor. --- ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) Solution **Input Bias Current ($I_B$):** It is the average of the two DC currents flowing into the inverting ($I_{B2}$) and non-inverting ($I_{B1}$) input terminals of the op-amp: $$I_B = \dfrac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$$ **Input Offset Current ($I_{io}$):** It is the algebraic difference between the two input bias currents: $$I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$$ **Effect of Temperature:** Both $I_B$ and $I_{io}$ are highly temperature dependent: - $I_B$ approximately **doubles for every 10$^\circ$C rise** in temperature (for BJT-input op-amps). - $I_{io}$ also increases with temperature but at a slower rate since it represents a *mismatch*. - For FET-input op-amps (e.g., TL082), $I_B$ is much lower but doubles for every 10$^\circ$C too. Datasheets typically specify the drift coefficients $\dfrac{\Delta I_B}{\Delta T}$ and $\dfrac{\Delta I_{io}}{\Delta T}$ in nA/$^\circ$C or pA/$^\circ$C. #### Part (b) Solution **Given:** $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_f = 9 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{io} = 3 \text{ mV}$, $I_{io} = 50 \text{ nA}$ **Compute the noise gain** (same formula for non-inverting): $$A_{CL} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \dfrac{9\text{k}}{1\text{k}} = 10$$ **Compute the bias-compensating resistor:** $$R_{\text{comp}} = R_1 \mid\mid R_f = \dfrac{1\text{k} \times 9\text{k}}{1\text{k} + 9\text{k}} = 900 \text{ }\Omega$$ **Compute the output offset:** $$V_{o(\text{offset})} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{io} + I_{io} R_f$$ $$V_{o(\text{offset})} = 10 \times 3 \text{ mV} + 50 \text{ nA} \times 9 \text{ k}\Omega$$ $$V_{o(\text{offset})} = 30 \text{ mV} + 0.45 \text{ mV} = 30.45 \text{ mV}$$ > **Final Answer:** $V_{o(\text{offset})} = \mathbf{30.45 \text{ mV}}$, $R_{\text{comp}} = \mathbf{900 \text{ }\Omega}$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Wrong noise gain formula:** Many students use the *signal gain* $-R_f/R_1$ instead of the **noise gain** $1 + R_f/R_1$. This costs **3 marks** instantly. **Always remember: DC errors are amplified by noise gain, not signal gain.** > > 2. **Forgetting $R_{\text{comp}}$:** If a question says "with bias compensation," you **must** show $R_{\text{comp}} = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$. Omitting it loses 1–2 marks. > > 3. **Sign errors in offset current:** $I_{io}$ is always a **magnitude** (no sign). Use absolute value carefully in dB/log-scale problems. > > 4. **Unit conversion mistakes:** $I_{io}$ is often given in nA; $R_f$ in k$\Omega$. The product gives mV if you handle powers of 10 correctly. **1 nA × 1 k$\Omega$ = 1 $\mu$V**, not 1 mV. This error is rampant in KTU answer sheets. > > 5. **Forgetting the drift term:** If a question mentions temperature variation, you **must** include the thermal drift $\dfrac{\Delta V_{io}}{\Delta T} \cdot \Delta T$ term in your answer. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Input Offset Voltage $V_{io}$** = differential DC voltage needed to zero the output. - [x] **Input Bias Current $I_B$** = average of $I_{B1}$ and $I_{B2}$. - [x] **Input Offset Current $I_{io}$** = magnitude of difference of $I_{B1}$ and $I_{B2}$. - [x] **Source of offset:** mismatch in $V_{BE}$, $\beta$, $R_C$, and current source. - [x] **Output offset formula:** $V_{o(\text{offset})} = (1 + R_f/R_1) V_{io} + I_{io} R_f$ - [x] **Noise gain ≠ Signal gain** for DC error analysis — this is the #1 KTU trap. - [x] **Bias compensation resistor:** $R_{\text{comp}} = R_1 \mid\mid R_f$, always in the **non-inverting** input. - [x] **Null potentiometer** adjusts only $V_{io}$ (not $I_{io}$); it cannot compensate drift. - [x] **Temperature drift** is **not** corrected by room-temperature null adjustment. - [x] **$\mu$A741** has $V_{io} \sim 6$ mV; **OP-07** has $V_{io} \sim 150 \text{ }\mu$V — choose wisely. - [x] **BJT input** op-amps have higher $I_B$ but lower $V_{io}$ drift; **FET input** op-amps have lower $I_B$ but higher $V_{io}$ drift. - [x] For **AC-coupled** applications, you can ignore offsets by using a series coupling capacitor. - [x] The **drift coefficient** $\Delta V_{io}/\Delta T$ is in $\mu$V/$^\circ$C — convert to V/$^\circ$C before substituting. - [x] Always use **magnitude** in output offset calculations; the sign simply shifts the output up or down. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Mnemonic:** "**V**oltage **I**nput **O**ffset" = **VIO** → Differential Voltage Input that Offsets the balance. Remember: **V**-I-**O** = **V**oltage, **I**nput, **O**ffset.

topics.title: Differential and Common Mode Operation of Op Amps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT304 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Differential and Common Mode Operation of Op Amps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Differential Mode Operation** refers to the operation of an operational amplifier when **two distinct signals** are applied to its two input terminals — one at the non-inverting (+) terminal and the other at the inverting (−) terminal. The op-amp amplifies only the **difference** between these two signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Mode Operation** refers to the operation of an op-amp when **the same signal** is applied simultaneously to **both** the inverting and non-inverting input terminals. In an ideal op-amp, the output for such a signal should be **zero**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology — KTU Board Definition:**
> - **Differential Mode Signal ($V_d$):** $V_d = V_1 - V_2$ (where $V_1$ is the signal at the non-inverting input and $V_2$ is the signal at the inverting input)
> - **Common Mode Signal ($V_c$):** $V_c = \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ (the average of the two input signals)
> - **Differential Mode Gain ($A_d$):** Gain offered by the op-amp to the difference signal $V_d$
> - **Common Mode Gain ($A_c$):** Gain offered by the op-amp to the common mode signal $V_c$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Tug-of-War" Team:**
>
> Imagine two teams pulling on a single rope in opposite directions:
> - The **strength difference** between the two teams is like the **differential signal** ($V_d = V_1 - V_2$). The op-amp amplifies this difference.
> - If **both teams pull equally** in the same direction (e.g., both teams pulling forward with the same force), the rope doesn't move relative to a fixed center point. This is the **common mode signal** — both inputs shift together, and ideally the op-amp should ignore it.
> - However, due to mismatches inside the op-amp, the "rope" may still drift slightly. This drift is characterized by the **Common Mode Gain ($A_c$)**, and the ability to suppress it is measured by the **CMRR (Common Mode Rejection Ratio)**.

**Why does this matter in real engineering?**
When a sensor (like a thermocouple or strain gauge) is connected to an op-amp, it often picks up **noise** that is common to both signal lines (e.g., 50 Hz power-line hum, electromagnetic interference). A good op-amp must **amplify the real signal** (differential) while **rejecting the noise** (common mode). This is the essence of differential-mode operation.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **CMRR (Common Mode Rejection Ratio):** Typically expressed in **dB** (decibels). A high-quality op-amp (e.g., IC 741) has CMRR ≈ **90 dB**; precision op-amps (e.g., OP-07) achieve **120 dB or higher**.
> - **Differential Gain ($A_d$):** For an open-loop op-amp, $A_d \approx 2 \times 10^5$ (≈ 106 dB).
> - **Common Mode Gain ($A_c$):** Ideally **zero**; practically a very small value.
> - **CMRR Formula:** $CMRR = \dfrac{A_d}{A_c}$ (dimensionless ratio)
> - **CMRR in dB:** $CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$

---

## 1.4 Representation of Input Signals

Any two arbitrary input signals $V_1$ and $V_2$ can be decomposed into a **differential component** and a **common-mode component**:

$$
V_1 = V_c + \frac{V_d}{2}
$$

$$
V_2 = V_c - \frac{V_d}{2}
$$

where $V_d = V_1 - V_2$ and $V_c = \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$.

> [!NOTE]
> This decomposition is the **foundation** of all KTU numerical problems on this topic. Every question begins by asking you to find $V_d$ and $V_c$ from given $V_1$ and $V_2$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Decompose the Input Signals
Any two voltages $V_1$ and $V_2$ applied to the op-amp's input terminals are first decomposed into:
- A **common-mode component** $V_c$ (the average, affecting both inputs equally)
- A **differential-mode component** $\pm V_d/2$ (the half-difference, with opposite signs on the two inputs)

### Step 2: Apply the Superposition Principle
The op-amp responds to each component independently. The **total output voltage** is the sum of:
- The output due to the differential input (amplified by $A_d$)
- The output due to the common-mode input (amplified by $A_c$)

### Step 3: Why is Common-Mode Rejection Important?
In real-world applications (e.g., biomedical signal acquisition, industrial sensors), the signal of interest is small (millivolts) and is accompanied by large common-mode noise (volts). If the op-amp amplifies this noise along with the signal, the signal becomes unusable. Hence, a **high CMRR** is the most critical specification for instrumentation.

### Step 4: The Role of the Difference Amplifier
A **difference amplifier** (a 4-resistor op-amp circuit) is specifically designed to amplify only the differential signal and reject the common-mode signal — provided the resistors are **precision-matched**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Differential Mode Signal | $V_d = V_1 - V_2$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | Common Mode Signal | $V_c = \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ | Volts (V) |
| 3 | Reconstructed Inputs | $V_1 = V_c + \dfrac{V_d}{2}$ , $\quad V_2 = V_c - \dfrac{V_d}{2}$ | Volts (V) |
| 4 | Output Voltage (General) | $V_o = A_d \cdot V_d + A_c \cdot V_c$ | Volts (V) |
| 5 | Output Voltage (Dominated by $A_d$) | $V_o \approx A_d \cdot V_d \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{CMRR} \cdot \dfrac{V_c}{V_d}\right)$ | Volts (V) |
| 6 | Common Mode Rejection Ratio | $CMRR = \dfrac{A_d}{A_c}$ | Dimensionless (ratio) |
| 7 | CMRR in Decibels | $CMRR_{(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | dB |
| 8 | Difference Amplifier Output (Matched) | $V_o = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}(V_1 - V_2)$ when $\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_4}{R_3}$ | Volts (V) |
| 9 | Difference Amplifier Output (Unmatched) | $V_o = \left(\dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right)\left(\dfrac{R_1+R_2}{R_1}\right)V_1 - \left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)V_2$ | Volts (V) |
| 10 | Instrumentation Amplifier Gain | $A_v = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_g}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}$ | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Table Formatting:** All absolute-value bars have been written using the word "ratio" or omitted to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. For the magnitude of $V_d$, write $V_d$ directly.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> - **Biomedical Engineering:** ECG/EEG machines use instrumentation amplifiers to detect tiny bio-electric signals (mV range) in the presence of large 50 Hz mains interference. The high CMRR of the amplifier removes this noise.
> - **Industrial Process Control:** 4–20 mA current loops in industrial sensors use differential amplifiers to extract the signal while rejecting ground-loop noise.
> - **Audio Engineering:** Balanced audio lines (XLR cables) rely on differential amplifiers to cancel electromagnetic interference picked up along long cable runs.
> - **Automotive Sensors:** Wheel-speed sensors, airbag accelerometers — all use differential op-amp configurations for noise immunity in electrically noisy vehicle environments.

---

## 2.4 Mathematical Relationship — Output Voltage in Terms of CMRR

The output of any op-amp can be expressed in a single elegant equation:

$$
V_o = A_d \cdot V_d + A_c \cdot V_c
$$

Substituting $A_c = \dfrac{A_d}{CMRR}$:

$$
V_o = A_d \cdot V_d + \frac{A_d}{CMRR} \cdot V_c
$$

Factoring out $A_d \cdot V_d$:

$$
V_o = A_d \cdot V_d \left(1 + \frac{1}{CMRR} \cdot \frac{V_c}{V_d}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most frequently tested equation** in KTU examinations for this topic. It directly shows that for the output to be "clean," the term $\dfrac{V_c}{V_d \cdot CMRR}$ must be negligible — which is achieved by making CMRR very large.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Deriving the Difference Amplifier Output Equation (Matched Resistor Case)

### Starting Circuit Assumptions
- A standard **difference amplifier** has $R_1$ at the inverting input (with $R_2$ as feedback), and $R_3$ at the non-inverting input (with $R_4$ to ground).
- The non-inverting input voltage $V^+$ is found by voltage division of $V_1$ across $R_3$ and $R_4$.

### Step 1: Find the Voltage at the Non-Inverting Terminal ($V^+$)

Using the voltage divider rule on the $V_1$ branch:

$$
V^+ = V_1 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** Since no current flows into the ideal op-amp's non-inverting terminal, the current through $R_3$ equals the current through $R_4$. The voltage $V^+$ is therefore the fraction of $V_1$ dropped across $R_4$.

### Step 2: Find the Voltage at the Inverting Terminal ($V^-$)

Since the op-amp is in its linear region (due to negative feedback), we apply the **virtual short concept**:

$$
V^- = V^+ = V_1 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The virtual short property states that when negative feedback is present, the voltage difference between the two input terminals is zero.

### Step 3: Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the Inverting Node

The current flowing from $V_2$ through $R_1$ must equal the current flowing from $V^-$ through $R_2$ to the output (since no current enters the op-amp):

$$
\frac{V_2 - V^-}{R_1} = \frac{V^- - V_o}{R_2}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** This equation is the formal statement of KCL: the sum of currents into the inverting node equals zero.

### Step 4: Substitute $V^-$ from Step 2 into Step 3

$$
\frac{V_2 - V_1 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}}{R_1} = \frac{V_1 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} - V_o}{R_2}
$$

### Step 5: Cross-Multiply and Simplify

Multiply both sides by $R_1 R_2$:

$$
R_2 \left( V_2 - V_1 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right) = R_1 \left( V_1 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} - V_o \right)
$$

Expand both sides:

$$
R_2 V_2 - R_2 V_1 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} = R_1 V_1 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} - R_1 V_o
$$

### Step 6: Collect the $V_1$ Terms

Move all $V_1$ terms to one side:

$$
R_2 V_2 = R_1 V_o + V_1 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \left( R_1 + R_2 \right)
$$

### Step 7: Solve for $V_o$

$$
V_o = \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_2 - V_1 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** This is the **general (unmatched) difference amplifier output equation**. It applies regardless of resistor matching.

### Step 8: Apply the Matching Condition

For **common-mode rejection**, we require that the gain for $V_1$ and the gain for $V_2$ are equal in magnitude. This is the **matching condition**:

$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{R_4}{R_3} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1}
$$

Simplifying, this reduces to:

$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{R_4}{R_3}
$$

When this condition is satisfied, the output becomes:

$$
V_o = \frac{R_2}{R_1} (V_2 - V_1) = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} (V_1 - V_2)
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = \frac{R_2}{R_1} (V_1 - V_2)}
$$

**Textual Explanation (with sign convention):** Depending on the textbook, the sign convention may differ. The KTU 2024 syllabus defines the inverting input as $V_1$ and non-inverting as $V_2$ in some texts, or vice versa. **Always check the polarity given in the question diagram.** If $V_1$ is at the inverting input, the output is $-\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}(V_1 - V_2)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: CMRR in dB

### Given:
$CMRR = \dfrac{A_d}{A_c}$

### Step 1: Apply the Definition of Decibel
By definition, a power or voltage ratio in dB is:

$$
X_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{V_1}{V_2} \right) \quad \text{(for voltage ratios)}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the CMRR Ratio

$$
CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right)
$$

### Step 3: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** An op-amp has $A_d = 2 \times 10^5$ and $A_c = 0.5$. Find the CMRR in dB.

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$
CMRR = \frac{A_d}{A_c} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{0.5} = 4 \times 10^5
$$

$$
CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} (4 \times 10^5)
$$

$$
CMRR_{dB} = 20 \left[ \log_{10}(4) + \log_{10}(10^5) \right]
$$

$$
CMRR_{dB} = 20 \left[ 0.6021 + 5 \right] = 20 \times 5.6021
$$

$$
\boxed{CMRR_{dB} = 112.04 \text{ dB}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Effect of Mismatched Resistors on CMRR

### Starting Point:
For the **unmatched** difference amplifier:

$$
V_o = \left(\frac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right)\left(\frac{R_1+R_2}{R_1}\right)V_1 - \left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)V_2
$$

Let $A_1 = \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} \cdot \dfrac{R_1+R_2}{R_1}$ and $A_2 = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}$.

The output can be rewritten as:

$$
V_o = A_1 V_1 - A_2 V_2
$$

Substituting $V_1 = V_c + \dfrac{V_d}{2}$ and $V_2 = V_c - \dfrac{V_d}{2}$:

$$
V_o = A_1 \left(V_c + \frac{V_d}{2}\right) - A_2 \left(V_c - \frac{V_d}{2}\right)
$$

Expanding:

$$
V_o = (A_1 - A_2)V_c + \left(\frac{A_1 + A_2}{2}\right)V_d
$$

Comparing with $V_o = A_c V_c + A_d V_d$:

$$
A_d = \frac{A_1 + A_2}{2}, \qquad A_c = A_1 - A_2
$$

### Final CMRR Expression:

$$
CMRR = \frac{A_d}{A_c} = \frac{A_1 + A_2}{2(A_1 - A_2)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the master equation for KTU numericals on mismatched difference amplifiers.** When $A_1 = A_2$ (perfectly matched), $A_c = 0$ and CMRR → ∞ (ideal case). Any mismatch in resistor values causes finite $A_c$, reducing the CMRR.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Full KTU Pattern

**Problem:** For a difference amplifier, $R_1 = R_3 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = R_4 = 100\text{ k}\Omega$. If $V_1 = 5.05\text{ V}$ and $V_2 = 5.0\text{ V}$, find:
(i) Differential mode signal $V_d$
(ii) Common mode signal $V_c$
(iii) Output voltage $V_o$

### Solution:

**(i) Differential Mode Signal:**

$$
V_d = V_1 - V_2 = 5.05 - 5.0 = 0.05 \text{ V} = 50 \text{ mV}
$$

**(ii) Common Mode Signal:**

$$
V_c = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} = \frac{5.05 + 5.0}{2} = 5.025 \text{ V}
$$

**(iii) Output Voltage:**

Since the resistors are matched ($\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_4}{R_3} = 10$):

$$
V_o = \frac{R_2}{R_1}(V_1 - V_2) = 10 \times 0.05 = 0.5 \text{ V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Even though $V_c = 5.025$ V (much larger than $V_d = 0.05$ V), the **perfectly matched amplifier completely rejects** the common-mode signal. This is the power of differential operation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Common-Mode vs Differential-Mode Signal Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Voltage V1"] --> B["Signal Decomposition Block"]
    A2["Input Voltage V2"] --> B
    B --> C["Differential Mode Signal Vd = V1 minus V2"]
    B --> D["Common Mode Signal Vc = V1 plus V2 over 2"]
    C --> E["Amplified by Ad Differential Gain"]
    D --> F["Amplified by Ac Common Mode Gain"]
    E --> G["Summing Junction"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Output Voltage Vo"]
    H --> I["Vo = Ad Vd plus Ac Vc"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Any two input signals are first decomposed into their differential and common-mode parts. The op-amp amplifies each part by its respective gain, and the outputs are summed to give the final output. An ideal op-amp has $A_c = 0$, so the common-mode path is effectively "blocked."

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Difference Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input_Stage["Input Signal Stage"]
        V1N["V1 to Inverting Input"]
        V2P["V2 to Non-Inverting Input"]
    end
    subgraph Resistor_Network["Precision Resistor Bridge"]
        R1["R1 Series at Inverting"]
        R2["R2 Feedback Resistor"]
        R3["R3 Series at Non-Inverting"]
        R4["R4 Ground Reference at Non-Inverting"]
    end
    subgraph Opamp_Core["Operational Amplifier Core"]
        OP["Ideal Op-Amp"]
    end
    subgraph Output_Stage["Output Stage"]
        VOUT["Differential Output Vo"]
    end
    V1N --> R1
    R1 --> OP
    OP --> R2
    R2 --> VOUT
    V2P --> R3
    R3 --> OP
    R4 --> OP
```

> [!NOTE]
