```mermaid
    Hardware Assembly          :d2, after d1, 14d
    Software Development       :d3, after d1, 21d
    Integration and Testing    :d4, after d2, 14d

    section Phase 5 Closure
    Report Writing             :e1, after d4, 14d
    Final Jury Presentation    :milestone, 2024-11-25, 0d
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Phase 1 Jury Presentation milestone (Sep 5)** is the most critical KTU checkpoint. Missing this deadline almost guarantees a 0 in the first review component, cascading into a poor final ICE grade.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Question, ADE Module 4 PBL]

**Q: Define Project Based Learning (PBL) as per the KTU 2024 scheme. List any four key objectives of PBL for an engineering course like Analog and Digital Electronics.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Project Based Learning (PBL) is a learner-centered pedagogical methodology mandated by KTU 2024 NEP 2020 scheme wherein students, working in collaborative groups of 3–5, apply theoretical concepts of a specific course to identify, design, prototype, and present an engineering solution to a real-world problem, evaluated continuously by a jury.

**Four Key Objectives:**

1. **Strengthen fundamental understanding** by enforcing application of theoretical knowledge in a tangible engineering artifact.
2. **Boost technical skills** in modern EDA tools (e.g., Multisim, Proteus, KiCad) and embedded platforms (Arduino, STM32, FPGA).
3. **Widen the horizon of thinking** through exposure to literature surveys, problem decomposition, and iterative design.
4. **Develop professional competencies** — report writing, presentation skills, teamwork, and time management — aligned with industry R&D practices.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Question, ADE Module 4 PBL]

**Q: What is the significance of the "Block Diagram Presentation within the first month" as mandated by KTU for PBL activities? List the typical components of a block diagram for an ADE project.**

**Model Answer:**

**Significance:** The first-month block diagram presentation is a **Phase 1 critical checkpoint** that validates:
- The group's understanding of the **problem statement**.
- The **feasibility** of the proposed solution within semester constraints.
- The **syllabus alignment** with ADE course modules.
- Early identification of **risks** (component unavailability, infeasible architecture).

It prevents groups from investing time in an unworkable concept.

**Typical Components of an ADE Block Diagram:**

1. **Input block** — sensor, signal generator, or user interface (keypad/switch).
2. **Signal conditioning block** — amplifier, filter, rectifier.
3. **Mixed-signal block** — ADC or DAC (Module 4 focus).
4. **Processing block** — microcontroller, FPGA, or combinational/sequential logic ICs.
5. **Output block** — LCD/7-segment display, LEDs, motor, relay, buzzer.
6. **Power supply block** — 7805/7812 regulators with smoothing capacitors.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Writing generic, course-agnostic PBL theory without mapping to **specific ADE concepts** (op-amp, ADC, counters, 555 timer, etc.) results in **0–1 marks out of 7** in sub-parts.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Failing to justify platform choice (Arduino vs. FPGA vs. discrete ICs) is a **2-mark deduction** under CO5 (Modern Tool Usage).
> - **Pitfall 3:** Skipping the block diagram and jumping straight to "we built a circuit" — the jury interprets this as **lack of systematic design thinking**, losing 3–4 marks.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Citing non-IEEE references (random blogs, Wikipedia) is heavily penalized. Always use **IEEE numbered citation format**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model PBL Question]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **five phases of the KTU 2024 PBL lifecycle** for Analog and Digital Electronics. For each phase, identify the **deliverable artifact** and the **corresponding Course Outcome (CO)** that it satisfies.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Your group is assigned the project **"Design and Implementation of a Frequency Counter using TTL Logic ICs and a 7-Segment Display."** Develop a **detailed block diagram**, justify the **platform and component choice**, and outline the **testing strategy**.

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Phase | Activity | Deliverable Artifact | CO Mapped |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | Orientation & Group Formation | Group constitution form | CO1 (Foundation) |
| 2 | Problem Identification & Literature Survey | 5–10 page literature review | CO1, CO2 |
| 3 | Platform Selection & Block Diagram | Block diagram (presented to jury) | CO5 (Tool Usage) |
| 4 | Hardware & Software Implementation | Working prototype + code repository | CO3, CO4, CO5 |
| 5 | Report Writing & Final Evaluation | 6-chapter report + viva | CO6 (Societal Impact) |

The **mapping** demonstrates that PBL is not a stand-alone activity but a **CO-achievement accelerator**.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Block Diagram:**

```
Input Signal (Unknown Frequency)
        ↓
Signal Conditioning (Comparator LM311 → Square Wave)
        ↓
Gate Pulse Generator (555 Timer in Monostable Mode, 1-second gate)
        ↓
AND Gate (7408) — Combines Signal and Gate
        ↓
Counter (7490 × 3 cascaded for 3-digit display)
        ↓
Decoder/Driver (7447 × 3)
        ↓
7-Segment Display × 3 (0–999 Hz)
```

**Platform Justification:** Discrete TTL 74xx series is chosen because:
- Directly maps to **Module 3 (Combinational Logic)** and **Module 4 (Sequential Logic / Counters)** syllabus.
- No microcontroller dependency; pure hardware demonstration of ADE concepts.
- Low cost (₹300–₹500 total).
- **CO5 justification:** Manual design using datasheets is itself a "modern tool" exercise.

**Testing Strategy:**
- Inject known frequencies (1 Hz, 100 Hz, 1 kHz) from a function generator.
- Verify counter displays match (with ±1 count tolerance due to ±1 second gating).
- Test gate-pulse width accuracy using an oscilloscope.
- Edge test: 0 Hz (should display 000) and 999 Hz (boundary).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model PBL Question]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of **literature survey** in a PBL project. Explain the **IEEE citation format** with a suitable example, and justify why Wikipedia/blog sources are not acceptable.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the project **"Automatic Street Light Controller using LDR and 555 Timer,"** design the **circuit-level block diagram**, list the **major components with their specifications**, and explain the **working principle** in detail.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Role of Literature Survey:**
- Establishes **prior art** — what has already been done in the domain.
- Identifies **research gaps** that the student's project can fill.
- Provides **validated design references** — published circuits, simulation parameters.
- Builds **academic writing skills** required for IEEE journal/conference publication.
- Mandatory KTU deliverable in Phase 2.

**IEEE Citation Format Example (Book):**
> [1] A. S. Sedra and K. C. Smith, *Microelectronic Circuits*, 7th ed. New York, NY, USA: Oxford University Press, 2015.

**IEEE Citation Format Example (Journal Paper):**
> [2] R. Jacob Baker, "CMOS: Mixed-Signal Circuit Design," *IEEE Journal of Solid-State Circuits*, vol. 45, no. 3, pp. 112–125, Mar. 2010.

**Why Wikipedia/Blogs Are Not Acceptable:**
- Wikipedia content is **community-edited and non-peer-reviewed** — lacks academic rigor.
- Blogs are **opinion-based**, not validated by the scientific community.
- KTU mandates **peer-reviewed sources** (journals, conference proceedings, standard textbooks, manufacturer datasheets) to ensure technical accuracy.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Block Diagram:**

```
LDR + Voltage Divider (Light Sensing)
        ↓
Comparator (LM358 — Module 1 Op-Amp)
        ↓
Trigger Pulse to 555 Timer (Monostable)
        ↓
Relay Driver (BC547 Transistor Switch)
        ↓
Street Light Load (230V AC Lamp)
        ↓
Optional: Day/Night Override using 555 Astable for Dim Mode
```

**Major Components:**

| Component | Specification | Quantity |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| LDR | GL5528, 1 MΩ dark, 10–20 kΩ light | 1 |
| LM358 Op-Amp | Single supply, ±18V max | 1 |
| IC 555 Timer | Astable + Monostable modes | 1 |
| BC547 NPN Transistor | 100 mA collector current | 1 |
| 12V Relay | SPDT, 10A contact rating | 1 |
| 7805 Regulator | +5V regulated supply | 1 |

**Working Principle:**
- During **daylight**, LDR resistance is low → voltage at comparator non-inverting input is low → comparator output is low → 555 timer is not triggered → relay is OFF → street light is OFF.
- During **darkness**, LDR resistance increases → comparator output goes HIGH → 555 monostable triggers → generates a timed output pulse → transistor saturates → relay energizes → street light turns ON.
- The 555 timer can be configured with an RC network to provide **fade-in or timed-off** behavior, demonstrating the **timer as a system-building block** from Module 2 syllabus.

This project elegantly integrates **op-amp comparator (Module 1), 555 timer (Module 2), transistor switching, and relay interfacing**, making it a high-scoring PBL choice for ADE.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PBL Module 4 (ADE):**

- **PBL Definition (KTU 2024):** Collaborative, learner-centered, jury-evaluated application of theory to a real-world problem in groups of 3–5.
- **Five Phases:** Orientation → Literature Survey → Block Diagram → Implementation → Report & Viva.
- **Group Size:** 3–5 students. **Jury:** Minimum 2 faculty. **Reviews:** Minimum 3 progressive reviews.
- **Critical Timeline:** Block diagram presentation **within the first month** of class commencement.
- **ICE Marks:** Typically 20 marks mapped to **CO5 (Modern Tools)** and **CO6 (Societal Impact)**.
- **Deliverables:** Literature review (5–10 pages), Block diagram, Working prototype, 6-chapter report, Final viva.
- **Report Structure:** Introduction → Literature Review → System Design → Implementation → Testing & Results → Conclusion & Future Scope → References.
- **Citation Style:** Strictly **IEEE numbered format**. Wikipedia and blogs are **prohibited**.
- **Platform Choices (ADE-relevant):** Arduino/STM32, FPGA, discrete 74xx TTL/CMOS ICs, simulation tools (Multisim/Proteus/LTspice).
- **Sample ADE Projects:** Digital thermometer, traffic light controller, function generator, frequency counter, automatic street light, smart irrigation, 555-timer-based alarms, LED chaser, security alarm.
- **Feasibility Matrix:** Always use a weighted scoring matrix (syllabus alignment, technical feasibility, innovation, societal impact, component availability, budget) before finalizing a topic.
- **Block Diagram Must Include:** Input → Signal Conditioning → ADC/Mixed Signal → Processing → Output → Power Supply.
- **Common Pitfalls:** Off-syllabus projects, unjustified platform choice, missing block diagram, poor citations, non-working demo, plagiarism > 20%.
- **Engineering Significance:** PBL simulates the **industry R&D lifecycle** — problem statement → prior art → architecture → prototype → documentation — preparing students for engineering careers or higher studies.
- **Highest-ROI Activity:** PBL is a **20-mark ICE** component achievable through systematic effort; treat it as a **career-defining project**, not a burden.
